relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) study and Reliability Based Demand Curves (RBDCs) directly inform capacity market accreditation and resource-adequacy requirements that affect how much capacity hydropower and pumped storage can sell into MISO's Planning Resource Auction.
ai_summary: MISO presented PY 2026-2027 Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) curves and preliminary Reliability Based Demand Curves (RBDCs) showing systemwide summer LOLE risk as highest, but subregional variation (North/Central region peaks in summer; South region peaks in winter, a seasonal shift from prior year). MISO confirmed battery-storage modeling will use 'even loss methodology' and is implementing an enhanced wind-profile model for the PY 2027-2028 LOLE study (near-term), with long-term coordination toward consistent renewable profiles across all planning studies. The wind-profile refinement showed preliminary no material change to LOLE outcomes. These LOLE and RBDC outputs flow directly into MISO's capacity accreditation framework and Planning Resource Auction parameters; seasonal subregional shifts in reliability risk can reset the demand curves that cap how much capacity owners—including hydro and pumped storage—can bid and clear at given prices. Next LOLEWG meeting is June 23, 2026.

title: 20260617 AC Item 01b Agenda
relevance_reason: Board session on 'Maintaining Resource Adequacy in a Time of Transition' directly addresses MISO's capacity/RA mechanisms and strategic resource-adequacy constructs that affect hydropower accreditation, accreditation curves, and fleet retirement decisions in the MISO footprint.
ai_summary: MISO's Advisory Committee meeting on June 17, 2026 includes a full board session titled 'Maintaining Resource Adequacy in a Time of Transition,' with discussion between Board and AC members on resource adequacy in the context of ongoing market/grid evolution. The session touches MISO's capacity accreditation frameworks and RA methodology during a period of fleet transition (retirements, storage entry, renewables growth). No specific proposals, votes, or comment deadlines are announced in this agenda document; the session itself is the information node. Hydro owners in MISO should monitor outputs from this session for changes to ELCC methodology, seasonal accreditation, or capacity-auction parameters that would affect how conventional hydro and PSH count toward RA showings.

title: 20260617 AC Item 01d Legal Update
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Document previews near-term tariff filings on co-located resource interconnection limits, capacity accreditation deliverability (DLOL methodology for SAC calculation), large-load reliability requirements, and zone-based generation interconnection (ZGIA) — four filings that will directly affect how hydro (especially pumped storage) competes for capacity accreditation, how co-location affects revenue, and how new load interconnection rules alter dispatch and deliverability constraints.
ai_summary: MISO's Legal Update lists 13 tariff filings anticipated through late July 2026, including four with material relevance to hydro and storage operators: (1) delay of the Interconnection Service Limit Constraint for Co-Located Resources effective date (06/12/2026, docket ER26-1915-000); (2) tariff revisions to establish DLOL-based accreditation methodology for capacity resources beginning Planning Year 2028–2029, with new provisions for how deliverability is accounted for in SAC values for both traditional and co-located resources (06/19/2026); (3) Large Load reliability and informational requirements (06/30/2026); and (4) a non-queue Zone-based Generation Interconnection Approach (ZGIA) for generation co-located at the same substation as load (06/30/2026). The accreditation deliverability filing (item 3) is the most directly material to PSH and reservoir hydro, as it resets how MISO calculates seasonal accreditation value when transmission-constraint-based deliverability limits are incorporated into the accreditation formula — a shift that could reduce accreditation for resources in tight delivery zones. Co-location rules (items 1 and 4) affect PSH and small hydro co-located with load or industrial facilities. All filings are estimated dates unless tied to a FERC deadline; no comment deadlines are stated in the outlook.

title: 20260617 AC Item 05a Current Issue Discussion - Preventing Fraud and Manipulation Questions
relevance_reason: Demand-side resource (DSR) accreditation changes and fraud/manipulation in DSR registrations directly affect how MISO's capacity market and resource-adequacy constructs treat demand-side flexibility—a competing product category that hydro owners bid against for RA value and ancillary-service revenue, and fraud in DSR valuation distorts the pricing signals hydro faces.
ai_summary: MISO's Advisory Committee is discussing fraud and manipulation risks in demand-side resource (DSR) registrations and accreditation, prompted by FERC enforcement actions (Ketchup Caddy LLC, $25M penalty, December 2024; American Efficient LLC, $722M penalty, April 2026). The committee is exploring upcoming MISO accreditation changes and how fraud vulnerabilities in DSR programs intersect with the recent proliferation of large-load interconnection requests. For hydro owners, the relevance is indirect but material: if MISO tightens DSR accreditation rules or validation standards, the capacity value and ancillary-service revenue opportunities for demand-side flexibility may shrink, reducing competition for RA accreditation and improving pricing signals for dispatchable hydro. Conversely, if fraudulent DSRs have inflated RA accreditation or depressed prices, correcting those practices could revalue hydro's true contribution. No specific accreditation proposals or vote dates are stated in this discussion document.

title: 20260617 AC Item 06 MISO Strategy Update
relevance_reason: MISO's strategy update addresses load growth integration, resource adequacy mechanisms, operational risks tied to fleet evolution and large-load interconnection, and storage/hybrid resource participation — all of which touch hydro revenue (seasonal accreditation, RA pricing), strategic options (large-load co-location, transmission planning), and obligations (operational risk categories, interconnection queue management).
ai_summary: MISO reports record 14.7 GW nameplate capacity additions in 2026, with solar and storage dominating the resource mix, while facing accelerating large-load additions (24.9 GW proposed, 8.9 GW approved in Expedited Project Reviews) and evolving operational risks — particularly net-load variability, fewer synchronous resources, and load-pocket adequacy. The Reliability Imperative initiative includes market redesign targeting accreditation/peak-risk alignment, optimized storage participation, dynamic reserves, and congestion-pricing reform, alongside transmission evolution and large-load integration speed-to-power. For hydro owners, the strategic significance lies in (1) seasonal accreditation and capacity-market design — MISO notes summer/winter capacity accreditation at 9.4%–21.2% planning reserve margins, directly affecting what flexibility and seasonal-storage products hydro can sell into RA frameworks; (2) load-pocket adequacy analysis and transmission planning to support large loads, which opens opportunities for run-of-river and reservoir hydro to serve co-located or regionally constrained load; and (3) storage-participation optimization, which will likely reshape how pumped storage competes with batteries in accreditation and ramping products. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated; this is a strategic briefing to the Advisory Committee with implementation timelines for ERAS and market reforms to follow.

title: 20260617 AC Item 07a Session with the BOD Questions - Maintaining Resource Adequacy in a Time of Transition
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Resource adequacy accreditation methodology, auction design, and seasonal capacity markets directly affect how MISO values hydro and storage capacity, and MISO's announced changes to accreditation rules, dynamic loss-of-load methodology, and demand curves reshape the revenue recognition and adequacy contribution of all dispatchable and storage resources in the footprint.
ai_summary: MISO Advisory Committee and Board session reviewing resource adequacy maintenance strategy in light of accelerated load-growth projections since June 2023. MISO has implemented seasonal capacity auctions, reliability-based demand curves, resource accreditation changes, dynamic loss-of-load methodology, and expedited interconnection processes (ERAS). The session will assess whether these changes are sufficient and what additional measures are needed. Directly relevant because accreditation methodology, demand-curve design, and seasonal auction structure set the capacity value hydro and pumped storage receive in MISO's planning reserve auctions; the shift to seasonal accreditation and dynamic loss-of-load modeling will alter how hydropower's seasonal contribution is recognized, particularly in high-stress periods where hydro's flexibility has highest value.

title: 20260617 AC Item 08 Main Parent Entity Status Updates
relevance_reason: Multiple MISO subcommittees report on accreditation rules, resource adequacy, reserve deliverability, interconnection procedures for co-located load and generation, and transmission planning that affect hydro and pumped-storage participation, capacity value, and strategic deployment options.
ai_summary: MISO's Resource Adequacy Subcommittee is addressing two accreditation issues that affect all dispatchable and storage resources: (1) Solar accreditation hour-window adjustment for Planning Year 2027/2028, realigning risk hours with observed real-time performance as a glide path to Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) methodology in 2028/2029—a precedent for how MISO recalibrates accreditation as resource mix shifts; (2) Lead-time rules for offline resources, where MISO is tightening the 24-hour lead-time accreditation threshold because some resources have changed lead times approaching or during emergencies, potentially masking unavailability. MISO intends a FERC filing in Q3 or Q4 2026 on the lead-time issue. The Market Subcommittee is also reviewing reserve deliverability in day-ahead and real-time markets (MSC-2026-2), noting that changing resource mix has affected regulation performance and created clearance disparities when the reserve-delivery test (RDT) binds—feedback was due June 5. On the interconnection front, the Planning Advisory Committee is advancing a Zero-Injection Generation Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) proposal for co-located load and generation, with modifications to require substation-level connection and automatic disconnection of injections when load trips; FERC filing is planned before end of June. These mechanisms directly affect accreditation certainty for hydro and pumped-storage capacity showings, regulation/AS revenue exposure if reserve-delivery rules tighten, and strategic options for co-locating PSH or run-of-river hydro with large loads or behind-the-meter applications.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 05 Five Year Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's five-year plan addresses storage participation optimization, accreditation reform, ramping and flexibility products, demand response accreditation, large-load interconnection effects on grid stability, and resource adequacy—all direct levers on hydro and PSH revenue and operational strategy in MISO.
ai_summary: MISO's 2026–2029+ five-year strategic plan prioritizes reliability imperatives driven by solar growth, load growth (especially large commercial/industrial loads), and net-load-shape steepening. Key market-design initiatives affecting storage and dispatchable resources include: (1) storage optimization in markets and multi-interval storage optimization (2027–2029), improving accreditation alignment with peak-risk periods and refining accreditation to include external resources (2026–2029+), and dynamic reserves; (2) ramping-product pricing adjustments, transmission constraint demand curves, linear ramping for fast-ramp resources, and enhanced regional ramp deliverability to mitigate 4–6% ramp shortages during morning/evening peaks; (3) demand-response reforms (2026–2028) with accreditation and participation standardization; and (4) FERC Order 2222 foundational work for DER aggregation (2026–2029+). Operationally, MISO plans centralized control-room modernization (2026–2027) and Ambient Adjusted Ratings (AAR) implementation under FERC Order 881 (2027–2029+). Resource-adequacy ecosystem modernization includes LOLE modeling refinements, large-load adequacy impact assessments, and co-optimized seasonal capacity auctions. Pumped-storage and reservoir hydro owners—especially those holding RA positions or supplying flexibility into ramp markets—should monitor accreditation-reform specifics (Q2–Q3 2026) and storage-optimization market-design details (2027 design phase) for impacts on capacity value and dispatch incentives. Large-load interconnection studies (2026–2027) and ambient-adjusted-ratings deployment affect system stability margins and may require hydro dispatch changes or create scarcity-driven revenue opportunities.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 06 MISO Operations Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's operations report flags net-load ramp challenges from 20+ GW of solar growth, summer resource adequacy outcomes, and emergency procedure changes that affect hydro dispatch obligations and revenue opportunities from ramping/regulation services.
ai_summary: MISO's spring 2026 operations report shows mild conditions but identifies summer challenges: over 20 GW of solar creating net-load ramp risk, above-average temperature forecasts, and drought conditions in parts of the footprint. The region cleared sufficient capacity (142 GW procured against 132 GW reserve requirement) in the 2026/27 Planning Resource Auction, but real-time metrics show congestion forecasting gaps and RSG (uplift) volatility—in March, $1.7M RSG was paid across three trade dates with 66% concentrated in a single high-priced resource in the South. MISO rolled out revised emergency procedures (June 1, 2026) with simplified capacity-emergency steps and a new Transmission Warning alert to provide earlier non-emergency signaling before load shed becomes necessary. For hydro operators, the ramp-challenge callout signals growing dispatch value from flexible resources; the highlighted congestion forecasting misalignments and congestion-cost shortfalls (ECF ~$10.6M shortfall in March) suggest potential revenue volatility in real-time energy and congestion markets, particularly for resources in the South load pocket.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 07 IMM Quarterly Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO Independent Market Monitor quarterly report identifies energy storage resource (ESR/battery) operational flaws and price-formation issues affecting dispatch efficiency, and assesses capacity adequacy and resource mix shift toward renewables, all of which establish competitive context and market-design reference points for PSH and conventional hydro revenue and strategic positioning in MISO.
ai_summary: MISO's Independent Market Monitor reports Spring 2026 performance: real-time energy prices fell 7% despite higher load, but price volatility surged with 20 shortage intervals (vs. 11 last year), driven by evening net-load ramps averaging 15 GW as solar penetration increases and system experiences 187 negative-price intervals in daytime hours. The Monitor identifies a critical ESR state-of-charge (SOC) software flaw that removed 7% of ESR capability from dispatch when outside SOC limits, scheduling discharge during negative prices and charge during $200+/MWh scarcity — a July software fix is underway. Existing solar is over-accredited at 68% summer output (designated 2–5 pm hours) versus actual 4–7 pm peak-stress window, inflating solar's capacity value by ~2.6 GW and depressing summer PRA clearing prices by ~$240/MW-day; the Monitor recommends aligning accreditation to 4–7 pm hours for next year's auction. These findings directly affect hydro's relative market position: expanded solar and ESR penetration intensify evening ramp demand (where conventional hydro excels), yet dispatch rules and accreditation design are not yet optimized for the new resource mix. Pumped storage, if subject to similar SOC constraints, faces the same inefficient dispatch penalty; conventional hydro benefits from scarcity but faces sustained low daytime prices from solar excess.

meeting_title: System Planning Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 02 Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: The agenda covers resource-adequacy and generator-interconnection queue updates, plus a five-year system-planning outlook — all foundational to hydro's future revenue opportunities, interconnection timelines, and strategic positioning in MISO's resource mix.
ai_summary: MISO System Planning Committee meeting on June 16, 2026, will review the five-year system plan, transmission evolution, and emerging planning technologies, with a substantive focus on Resource Adequacy & Generator Interconnection Queue Update and the 2026 Work Plan. The queue and RA updates are critical for hydro and PSH owners assessing interconnection timelines, competitive entry risk, and RA accreditation treatment in MISO. The transmission-evolution item may signal changes in non-wires alternatives or storage-as-transmission opportunities relevant to PSH siting and valuation. No specific proposals or vote dates are stated in this agenda document; the substantive position will emerge only at the meeting.

meeting_title: System Planning Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 04 Five-Year Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's five-year plan directly addresses hydro and storage revenue, obligations, and strategic options through storage resource participation optimization, resource accreditation reforms, large-load interconnection enabling, dynamic reserves design, and capacity-adequacy modeling that will reshape how existing hydro and pumped-storage plants are dispatched and valued.
ai_summary: MISO's System Planning Committee approved a five-year strategic plan (2025–2029+) with major workstreams in market redefinition, transmission evolution, and operations modernization. In Market Redefinition, MISO will redesign storage participation models (including co-located storage optimization), reform resource accreditation to improve peak-risk-period alignment and reflect evolving resource risks, develop dynamic ramp and regulation reserves, and modernize resource adequacy modeling and loss-of-load expectation (LOLE) tooling to account for climate-change impacts and seasonal interdependencies. In Transmission Evolution, MISO will accelerate large-load interconnections (with "speed-to-partial-power" processes), implement FERC Order 2023 generation interconnection reforms, and conduct interregional transmission planning with PJM and SPP. These changes mean storage operators (especially pumped-storage owners) will face new accreditation rules that tie capacity credit to peak-risk periods rather than nameplate, new co-location optimization rules that can improve or constrain dispatch economics, and expanded opportunities to serve fast-growing data-center and large-load demand. Reservoir and run-of-river operators will be affected by changes to dynamic reserves procurement, ramp-product design, and seasonal resource adequacy modeling. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated in this overview document; the plan is framed as approved board guidance for 24–36 month execution cycles beginning 2025.

meeting_title: System Planning Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 05 Reliability Imperative_Transmission Evolution.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's transmission and load-growth planning directly affects hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and strategic positioning in evolving seams-governance arrangements (PJM-MISO TMEP, SPP-MISO CSP); long-range studies and interconnection queues determine which resources benefit from coordinated upgrades and transmission-as-option value.
ai_summary: MISO is executing a three-layered planning framework (local, regional, interregional) to accommodate 26.4 GW of large-load additions projected in MTEP26, with load-growth forecasts ranging 1.1–3.0% CAGR through 2046 (data-center CAGR at 103% through 2030). The South and Midwest Long Range Transmission Planning studies are launching this quarter using updated Futures scenarios to guide solutions for reliability and load-serving needs; outcomes will determine transmission and/or generation solutions in subsequent MTEP cycles. Parallel interregional coordination is underway with PJM (2026 Targeted Market Efficiency Project studying PJM-MISO congestion), SPP (2024–25 CSP yielded two portfolio options improving reliability and transfer capability; 2026–27 CSP announced), and broader alignment with FERC Order 1920. This acceleration of transmission and interregional planning directly raises the strategic value of existing hydro capacity and expands opportunities for hydro to serve as a flexibility and transfer solution, particularly for units near MISO seams or capable of serving co-located load; the 28 GW of joint capacity enabled by JTIQ transmission projects (19 GW approved in MISO-SPP) signals growing reliance on coordinated transmission to unlock interconnection participation.

meeting_title: System Planning Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 06 Current and Emerging System Planning Technology Overview.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue modernization directly affects hydro and storage project deliverability and timeline, a core strategic option and revenue-impact lever for NHA members seeking interconnection.
ai_summary: MISO is deploying SUGAR (Suite of Unified Grid Analyses with Renewables) to automate interconnection study workflows and compress Phase 1 processing from 93 business days (2022 cycle) to 47 days (2023 cycle) to 54 days (2025 cycle), with plans to extend automation to Phases 2 and 3. The system uses agentic AI to automate power flow analysis and network upgrade costing, achieving >99% accuracy match with legacy tools. Faster queue processing reduces time-to-decision for both generator and storage interconnection requests—a material advantage for PSH and conventional hydro seeking expedited network studies and cost determination. MISO's next stage expands automation to stability and short-circuit analysis (Phases 2–3), which historically consumed months; no comment deadline is stated, but this is a board-approved initiative moving into deployment (Q2 2026 status shown).

meeting_title: System Planning Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 07 Resource Adequacy and GI Queue Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue update and resource-adequacy outlook directly affect hydro and storage owners' interconnection timelines, accreditation pathways (particularly storage in ERAS cycles), and the region's capacity-margin trajectory—which shapes scarcity pricing and PSH dispatch value.
ai_summary: MISO reports record 2026 capacity additions (14.7 GW nameplate, 10.7 GW accredited) offsetting 2.6 GW of retirements, with 86 GW of approved projects still awaiting commercial operation and 43 GW experiencing delays. Storage projects represent 18–40% of active queue and ERAS applications across cycles 1–4 (as of 6/1/26), advancing through the Expedited Resource Addition Study process designed to accelerate generation additions amid load surge. However, 30 GW (218 projects) have exceeded the standard GIA grace period, with regulatory, contractor, and PPA issues cited as primary delay drivers; projects are securing federal waivers extending timelines 2–7 years beyond COD. For hydropower and PSH owners, the bottleneck between approved capacity and online generation prolongs scarcity conditions that support energy prices and ramping-value realization, while storage's prominent role in ERAS (12–17% of cycles studied) demonstrates MISO's reliance on storage flexibility—though the report does not quantify storage-specific accreditation treatment or ancillary-services pathways. Next steps include MISO exploring modifications to ERAS study cycles (expected Q2 2026) to maximize utilization and GIA negotiations expected over the next two years (920 projected total across Q2 2026–Q2 2027).

meeting_title: System Planning Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 08 2026 Work Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document outlines MISO's 2026 System Planning Committee agenda, which includes resource adequacy, generator interconnection queue updates, transmission expansion planning (MTEP), and reliability assessments — all of which affect hydro and PSH owners' capacity accreditation, interconnection timelines, and transmission-based strategic options in the MISO footprint.
ai_summary: MISO's System Planning Committee has scheduled four meetings in 2026 covering MTEP (Transmission Expansion Plan) review, generator interconnection queue updates, resource adequacy studies, and transmission evolution. Key agenda items include preliminary MTEP26 review (September 15), substantive comments review (November 12), and board recommendation (November 12). For hydro and PSH owners in MISO, these meetings will address transmission capacity available for resource delivery, interconnection wait times and queue status, and seasonal/long-term resource adequacy forecasting that determines the scarcity signals affecting energy and reserve pricing. The interconnection queue update appears as a standing item across all four meetings, suggesting ongoing queue congestion or significant new requests; the June 16 meeting will present a five-year system planning overview and reliability evolution framework that may signal new transmission constraints or dispatch requirements affecting dispatchable and storage resources.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is addressing co-located load and generation participation rules, interconnection reliability requirements for large loads, and firm service standards that directly affect how hydro and other dispatchable resources can compete for large-load contracts and serve behind-the-meter applications.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is advancing three initiatives: (1) a zero-injection GIA framework (PAC-2024-2B) governing market participation and registration of co-located load and generation, with tariff review scheduled for June 10, 2026; (2) interconnection reliability requirements (LLWG-2026-3) including ramp capability, ride-through, and stability standards for large loads, with transitional language, data/modeling, and ride-through requirements under review; and (3) a large load firm service step-up study (LLWG-2026-1) with study-process design details due June 10. These establish the rules under which conventional hydro and pumped-storage hydro can compete to serve large loads co-located at or behind load centers, and define the operational obligations (ramp, stability, ride-through) that such service requires. The firm-service framework may create new contracting or capacity-accreditation pathways for dispatchable resources, particularly in load-growth regions where large loads are interconnecting.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01c Action Items.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load interconnection and reserve-product enhancements directly affect how large hydro facilities and pumped-storage plants manage interconnection deliverability, participate in reserve markets, and compete with co-located loads for network access and capacity accreditation.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is tracking three active tariff initiatives: (1) ZGIA proposed draft tariff (PAC-2024-2B), (2) Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG-2026-3), and (3) Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG-2026-2), with feedback deadlines stated as May 29, May 26, and June 4 respectively. The interconnection-reliability and reserve-product initiatives directly affect how large existing hydro plants and pumped-storage facilities must operate alongside new large loads competing for interconnection service and regional reserve procurement. Hydro owners with generation in MISO should monitor whether these rules tighten synchronous-machine requirements, alter reserve accreditation for dispatchable units, or create preferential treatment for co-located load that would displace wholesale-market revenue from grid-serving generation.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260514.pdf
relevance_reason: LLWG discussions of zero-injection GIAs, co-located load-and-generation participation models, and interconnection reliability requirements directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources can register, dispatch, and participate in MISO markets — a strategic option for existing and new hydro capacity.
stakeholders: Eric Stephens (AMP (Association of Municipal Power Systems))
Werner Roth (TX PUC (Texas Public Utilities Commission))
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group approved a charter and reviewed draft tariff language for Zero-Injection Generator Interconnection Agreements (ZGIAs), which govern how co-located generation and load behind a single point of withdrawal participate in the MISO market. The proposal addresses resource adequacy, market participation, and reliable operations for large-load campuses with on-site or nearby generation (including hydro). MISO will post final tariff language prior to filing at end of June 2026, with business practice manual language expected Q3/Q4. The LLWG also discussed Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG-2026-3). Hydro owners operating or developing capacity co-located with large loads should track this mechanism: ZGIAs and campus-level participation models create revenue pathways for hydro serving behind-the-meter load, but tariff design choices on scheduling, resource-adequacy accreditation, and storage state-of-charge treatment will determine whether existing flexibility value transfers to the co-located arrangement.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and co-located load/generation rules directly affect hydro owners' ability to serve loads behind the meter and their market participation pathways, especially for pumped storage and run-of-river resources seeking direct offtake arrangements.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is developing policies on large load interconnection, firm service options, reserve products, and operational requirements (forecasting, network modeling, telemetry/PMU standards). A June 2026 agenda includes discussion of "Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation / Consideration of Zero-Injection GIA" (PAC-2024-4), which directly governs how hydro owners (especially those with on-site load or storage) register and participate in MISO markets. The group is also addressing "Large Load Firm Service Step Up" (LLWG-2026-1) and "Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements" (LLWG-2026-2), with draft tariff redlines and final recommendations expected by August–November 2026. These initiatives reshape the interconnection, market registration, and reserve-procurement framework for resources co-located with large loads—a key strategic pathway for PSH and run-of-river facilities seeking firm offtakes or behind-the-meter service.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 05 Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) proposal directly affects how co-located generation and load participate in capacity adequacy, resource registration, market offerings, and interconnection — touching revenue accreditation, market participation obligations, and strategic participation pathways for owners with on-site generation or hybrid assets.
ai_summary: MISO proposes a Zero-Injection GIA framework to formalize market participation and interconnection for co-located generation serving on-site load at the same substation and voltage level. The proposal addresses interconnection planning (using familiar surplus/replacement/ERAS processes), resource adequacy accreditation (ZGIA resources offset only their associated local load's capacity obligation, accredited under current MISO rulesets and eligible for zonal resource credits limited by Schedule 53 Direct Loss of Load accreditation or local load obligation), market registration (independent metering and telemetry required; market resources must maintain offer limits ≤ local load demand; MISO will monitor injections and consider charges for overage), and transmission planning (ZGIA included as Non-Transmission Alternative in MTEP). Draft tariff language has been posted; formal feedback is requested from the PAC (pending) and LLWG (June 10, 2026), with a FERC filing planned for late June 2026. Most exposed are owners of on-site generation (including small hydro or pumped storage sited at industrial or utility facilities) seeking to participate as co-located resources or hybrid assets; the framework clarifies accreditation caps and injection limits that will govern revenue from capacity and energy markets.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 06 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements Presentation (LLWG-2026-3).pdf
relevance_reason: Large load interconnection requirements directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage operators' strategic options for co-located load development, their transmission deliverability, and may impose new operational modeling/telemetry/ramp obligations on facilities with computational loads or adjacent generation.
ai_summary: MISO is finalizing tariff language for Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements, targeting a June 30, 2026 filing. The framework distinguishes between Computational Loads (digitally driven, requiring advanced modeling, PMU, ramp, and ride-through requirements) and Non-Computational Loads (baseline data and telemetry only), with applicability triggered at 50 MW or expansions of 25+ MW. For hydro owners, this matters because (1) co-located data centers, cryptocurrency mining, and HPC workloads with which hydro may be sited or co-operate are now subject to detailed telemetry, PMU, dynamic modeling, and forecasting requirements that will flow upstream to the interconnecting hydro facility; (2) large-load definition and transition provisions now allow flexibility for in-flight projects, creating windows for strategic positioning; and (3) the distinction between computational and non-computational behavior may allow run-of-river or variable hydro paired with load to avoid some advanced-modeling burdens if properly characterized. Stakeholder feedback significantly shaped the revised language, particularly on applicability date, transition timelines, and load-type distinctions.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 06 MISO Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements_Technical Compendium Draft.pdf
relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection reliability requirements establish grid-performance and operational-readiness obligations that will affect hydropower's ability to maintain system stability during rapid load-consumption changes and disturbances, and may constrain hydro's operational flexibility or impose new coordination/telemetry burdens; also signals that MISO's rapid load growth (data centers, AI computing) creates scarcity conditions that raise the market value of existing hydro's dispatchability and ramping capability.
ai_summary: MISO published a technical compendium establishing reliability requirements for large transmission-connected loads (data centers, AI computing, crypto mining) that are growing rapidly across its footprint. The framework mandates telemetry and PMU requirements, data/modeling disclosures, short-term load forecasting, ramp-rate limits, voltage and frequency ride-through standards, and transient-stability and oscillation assessments for new large loads. This affects hydro owners in two ways: (1) large-load behavior—rapid changes in power consumption and sudden disconnections during disturbances—will drive more volatile and unpredictable demand patterns that hydropower must balance in real time, potentially increasing the operational value of hydro's ramping and regulation services; and (2) ride-through and stability rules imposed on loads may shift grid-stability burdens away from demand-side and back onto traditional generation (including hydro), creating new obligations for synchronous units. The document does not indicate a comment deadline or vote schedule; it appears to be a draft technical standard (marked as compendium draft) with stakeholder-engagement appendix noted but not shown in excerpt.

title: 20260610 LLWG Item 07 Firm Service Step Up Study Process.pdf
relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and interim service step-up procedures materially affect hydro's strategic options for co-located load participation, transmission deliverability, and resource-adequacy obligations, and establish precedent for how RTOs structure partial-service pathways that could extend to storage and other dispatchable resources.
ai_summary: MISO is formalizing a Firm Service Step Up process to allow large load customers to begin service on partial load blocks before full transmission upgrades are complete, using only interim available capacity. The process requires each partial load addition to pass NERC TPL-001 reliability studies (90-day cycle, quarterly submissions, first-ready-first-served prioritization) and mandates that the load-serving entity procure generation capacity in MISO's Planning Resource Auction to cover the interim load. Approved partial loads become effective at the start of the planning year and persist until the full load interconnection is operational; service is revoked if a load block fails to energize within 90 days of its approved date. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, this is strategically relevant: (1) co-located hydro can now supply large-load customers incrementally rather than waiting for full transmission readiness, shortening payback and improving investment returns; (2) the requirement that interim loads participate in the PRA means the resource-adequacy framework now extends to partial-service loads, establishing that generation must be procured at each step, a model that could define how storage and other flexible resources are accredited in segmented operations; (3) hydro deliverability and transmission-constraint management now explicitly account for multiple overlapping interim-service blocks, which affects zonal scarcity pricing and ramping-reserve demand. MISO requests stakeholder feedback on the prioritization methodology, submission granularity, study frequency, and generation-availability accounting by Monday, June 24, 2026, with tariff language to follow in August.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 01b Agenda
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Storage charging treatment in generator interconnection agreements (GIA) directly affects pumped-storage hydro revenue and operational flexibility, and MTEP reliability planning with storage sensitivities shapes the transmission and adequacy environment where PSH competes.
ai_summary: MISO Planning Subcommittee will address two storage-relevant items: (1) removal of storage charging limitations in generator interconnection agreements (PAC 2024-3, item 5, presenter Grant Larson at 9:55 ET), which expands the operational envelope for PSH charging; and (2) MTEP reliability sensitivities for NERC TPL update (item 6, presenter Joe Dunn at 10:15 ET) incorporating storage in transmission adequacy modeling. The first item signals MISO's shift toward lifting GIA restrictions that have historically constrained PSH charging-mode operations and revenue capture. The second embeds storage (including PSH) into the transmission planning baseline, potentially improving the visibility of storage's contribution to reliability and the case for new transmission or storage-backed reliability solutions. Both are scheduled for discussion at the June 10, 2026 meeting; no explicit vote or comment deadline is stated in this agenda.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 01c Minutes 20260429
relevance_reason: MISO's proposed changes to storage charging study requirements in interconnection and reliability planning (BPM-015 updates and DPP process modifications) directly affect how pumped-storage and energy-storage projects are modeled, studied, and cost-allocated, touching both interconnection obligations and revenue exposure through queue treatment.
ai_summary: MISO proposed updates to BPM-015 (Business Practice Manual) to remove storage charging limitations in generator interconnection agreements (PAC-2024-3), replacing the Shoulder off-peak charging case with a Light Load charging case and limiting charging study phases to II and III of the Definitive Planning Process. Stakeholders raised concerns about how these changes interact with existing queue positions, cost exposure, and study treatment across all generators including storage. The changes do not affect DPP cycles already underway and are expected to begin with DPP-26 if approved by year-end; feedback is due May 20, 2026, and MISO will introduce language to the Planning Advisory Committee in Q3. Pumped-storage operators should scrutinize how Light Load charging assumptions affect their accreditation and cost allocation during interconnection studies, particularly for projects already queued or in mid-study phases.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 01d Management Plan
relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION PLANNING: The PSC oversees MISO's MTEP reliability planning, transfer-capability analysis, and multi-year transmission projects that directly affect deliverability and revenue for hydro generators; storage-charging transmission service requirements (PAC-2023-2) and removal of storage-charging limitations in grid interconnection agreements (PAC-2024-3) materially alter the operational and economic constraints on pumped-storage hydro.
ai_summary: MISO's Planning Subcommittee manages the Multi-Year Transmission Evaluation Process (MTEP) and reliability assessments across 2026–2027, with recurring quarterly reviews of approved transmission projects, voltage stability, and transfer-capability analyses. Two active initiatives directly affect storage and hydro: PAC-2023-2 (Transmission Service Requirements for Charging Electric Storage Resources from the Grid) and PAC-2024-3 (Removing Storage Charging Limitations in GIAs) are under discussion at the June 10, 2026 meeting and scheduled for final review through Q3 2026. PAC-2024-3 removes contractual caps on charging volume in generator interconnection agreements, potentially unlocking incremental pumped-storage dispatch and charging flexibility; PAC-2023-2 addresses how the grid must serve charging load, affecting cost allocation and dispatch freedom for PSH. The PSC's four quarterly cycles through 2027 include dispatch-model assumption reviews and sensitivity studies that will incorporate updated wind/solar forecasts and storage assumptions, shaping the transmission baseline against which hydro's operational value and export capability are evaluated.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 02 Liaison Report
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Storage charging treatment in interconnection studies and transmission planning directly affects PSH (and battery) participation economics; removal of charging limitations in GIAs and model changes to Phase 3 injection studies signal MISO's evolving approach to how stored-energy resources are studied and integrated into grid planning, with downstream impact on PSH project viability and cost allocation.
ai_summary: MISO has decided to remove storage charging limitations in Phase 3 of its Distributed Generation Proposal (DPP), shifting the modeling approach from SH to LL methodology to include battery charging in interconnection studies; however, MISO will not remove charging constraints in Phase 1 of DPP. This decision elevates to the Planning Advisory Committee (PAC). The change directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and battery resources model their charging footprint in interconnection impact assessments, potentially lowering their study costs and revealing more favorable network impacts when charging is explicitly accounted for. Additionally, MISO posted feedback responses confirming its Annual Review of Dispatch Wind/Solar Assumptions and Transmission Cost Estimation Guide for MTEP26, which will inform transmission expansion studies and affect whether existing hydropower resources receive congestion-relief value or face new constraint headroom requirements. Pumped-storage projects seeking interconnection approval and existing PSH facilities relying on congestion revenue should monitor the PAC review timeline and MTEP27/DPP2027 modeling implementation.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 03a Modeling Update - Planning Process
relevance_reason: MISO's multi-year planning model series (MISO26, 2026 ERAG MMWG) underpins resource-adequacy studies, transmission expansion, and seasonal energy-adequacy analyses that directly affect hydro revenue projections, ELCC accreditation, and deliverability planning.
ai_summary: MISO Planning Modeling team has completed the 2025 ERAG Multiregional Modeling Working Group (MMWG) series and is advancing the 2026 and MISO26 series, which serve as the backbone for NERC transmission planning (TPL) and MISO's Planning Resource Auction (resource-adequacy) studies. The MISO26 power-flow models are on track for June 2026 submission to MMWG, with final Pass 4 models released April 1, 2026; dynamics models use PSSE v35.6.4 and include summer-peak, light-load, and shoulder-load cases across 2026, 2030, and 2035 horizons, with wind and solar capacity-credit penetrations specified for each case. These model series are the inputs to seasonal energy-adequacy studies and transmission needs assessment that determine whether existing hydro plants' RA contributions (ELCC values) and transmission-delivered energy values remain stable or erode under future generation mixes; changes to model structure or resource assumptions in future years (e.g., solar saturation levels, minimum-load scenarios) cascade into accreditation and planning-reserve-margin calculations.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 05 Removing Storage Limitations in GIAs (PAC 2024 3)
relevance_reason: MISO is modifying generation interconnection study procedures for storage charging analysis, which directly affects how pumped-storage hydro interconnection impacts and cost allocations are evaluated in the DPP process.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to replace the 'Summer Shoulder' charging scenario with a 'Light Load' charging scenario in generation interconnection (DPP) studies, effective DPP 2026 (pending approval by December 2026). The Light Load model represents early-morning hours at 45–65% loading, continuing to evaluate both storage charging and injection of queued generation across all three DPP phases. This change affects how network upgrades triggered by storage charging are identified and cost-allocated; PSH developers will now see upgrades tied to morning charging rather than shoulder-period charging, potentially altering which interconnection service categories bear upgrade costs. The mechanism matters because interconnection study procedures determine whether PSH charging access is operationally feasible and cost-justified; shifting the charging scenario from shoulder to light load could reduce or defer upgrade requirements, lowering interconnection costs for PSH projects. Most exposed are pumped-storage developers in the queue; MISO requests feedback by July 15, 2026 (PAC meeting) on the proposed BPM-015 language, with legal review and posting of updated BPMs targeted for Q4 2026.

title: 20260610 PSC Item 06 MTEP Reliability Sensitivities of NERC TPL
relevance_reason: MTEP scenario changes affecting transmission planning reliability assessment directly impact the operational and dispatch conditions under which hydro resources are modeled for interconnection, deliverability, and resource-adequacy accreditation purposes.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to replace the 2-year Light Load, 0% wind planning sensitivity with a 2-year Winter Peak, low wind case (0% solar, wind TBD but below 67%) in its MTEP reliability sensitivities to better reflect winter operating conditions and stress the transmission system under NERC TPL compliance. The change affects how MISO models generation dispatch and transmission adequacy in planning studies used for both MTEP and generator interconnection (DPP), ERAS, and surplus-service reviews. Stakeholder feedback is due June 26, 2026, before BPM-020 and the Planning Modeling Manual are updated for MTEP27. Hydro owners should consider whether the winter-peak dispatch assumptions (low solar, lower wind) accurately represent the seasonal value and dispatch patterns of their resources in MISO's planning studies, particularly regarding accreditation in seasonal resource-adequacy analyses and interconnection deliverability assessments.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO interconnection queue reform (Attachment GGG and Generator Interconnection Portal updates) directly affects hydro entry and uprate pathways, and the DPP study schedule determines when generation projects—including hydro—advance to deliverability assessment.
ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group will review feedback on Attachment GGG tariff improvements (PAC-2024-6) and introduce additional tariff redlines on June 9, 2026, and will receive updates on the Definitive Planning Phase (DPP) study schedule and a Generator Interconnection Portal product update. Attachment GGG modifications and the DPP study cadence directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage projects navigate the queue, secure deliverability, and compete with other resources for interconnection positions. The agenda does not specify which segments are most exposed or include comment deadlines, but the tariff redlines phase indicates active rule-setting; hydro owners should monitor the next IPWG meeting (July 21, 2026) for substantive tariff proposals and any formal comment windows.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01b Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: FERC Order 2023 compliance and interconnection queue management directly affect hydro and PSH owners' ability to build or uprate plants and the timing/cost of grid interconnection, a core strategic and operational lever.
ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group (IPWG) is tracking multiple Order 2023 implementation workstreams: BPM-015 (project-ownership-transfer rules), interconnection queue-cap mechanics (PAC-2023-1), Attachment GGG improvements affecting study timelines and cost allocation, and electromagnetic-transient (EMT) system-strength screening for inverter-based resources. The group is also implementing SUGAR (presumably Standardized Unified Generator Aggregation Review) to standardize application-model data and reviewing the MISO/SPP Joint Operating Agreement. Order 2023 penalty rules are scheduled for October 2026 discussion. For hydro and PSH owners in MISO, these queue and study-process reforms directly shape interconnection lead times, uprate feasibility, and the cost of surplus interconnection service — and the June 9, July 21, October 13, and December 8, 2026 meetings are the immediate decision gates. Small and run-of-river hydro seeking DER-aggregation pathways (Order 2222) should monitor queue-standardization and portal-product updates.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01c Action Items.pdf
relevance_reason: Interconnection Process Working Group action items track interconnection queue reform, IBR conformance requirements, and deliverability standards that directly affect hydro plant interconnection, upgrade pathways, and operational compliance obligations.
ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group closed three action items as of June 9, 2026: feedback on Attachment GGG interconnection improvements (PAC-2024-6), follow-up on Inverter Based Resource (IBR) performance and modeling conformance with OEM compliance by June 1, 2026 (status update issued May 11), and administrative updates to the SUGAR implementation slides. The IBR conformance deadline is most material: it sets modeling and performance standards that may establish precedent for or constrain how energy-storage and hybrid hydro resources qualify in MISO's interconnection process. The Attachment GGG improvements (status closed by May 12) likely address queue reform, surplus interconnection, or interconnection service rules that directly affect upgrade economics and delivery timelines for conventional hydro uprates and new PSH interconnections. Hydro owners should monitor MISO filings for final Attachment GGG language and IBR conformance criteria to assess impact on their own interconnection applications and existing plant compliance obligations.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01c Minutes 20260428.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue reforms (ERAS, Surplus Interconnection, Generator Replacement) and IBR modeling/system-strength screening directly affect hydro and PSH developers' ability to interconnect, study timelines, and grid-interaction requirements for new and uprated plants.
ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group meeting on April 28, 2026, covered three interconnection-related initiatives affecting all generators: (1) Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS) program has 53 of 68 allowed projects active, with Cycles 1–2 complete and Cycle 3 ongoing; (2) Surplus Interconnection and Generator Replacement rules received FERC approval April 14, 2026, with new $150,000 study-deposit amounts effective immediately, and BPM-015 redlines pending finalization; (3) IBR Performance, Modeling, and Conformance (PAC-2024-2) is developing system-strength screening for electromagnetic-transient (EMT) studies to target weak-grid interconnection cases as IBR penetration grows. For hydro and PSH, the Surplus Interconnection approval and revised deposit structure directly lowers barriers to uprates and replacement generation at existing hydroelectric sites; ERAS eligibility and study-cycle progression affect delivery timelines for new run-of-river and reservoir projects. IBR modeling requirements (June 1, 2026, deadline) impose conformance obligations on synchronous machines co-located or competing for queue position, though hydro's synchronous generator status may exempt traditional plants from inverter-specific rules. Feedback on Attachment GGG MHVDC improvements was due May 12, 2026; stakeholders should track the June 2026 BPM posting and Q3–Q4 FERC filings.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 02 Liaison Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue reform, DPP cycle scheduling, and Attachment GGG improvements directly affect interconnection timelines and cost-recovery pathways for all generators including hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking network access.
ai_summary: MISO reports progress on Attachment GGG improvements (PAC-2024-6) addressing cost-recovery clarifications for Necessary vs. Network upgrades, with stakeholder feedback incorporated and PAC review pending. The DPP 2026 cycle has an application deadline of September 10, 2026, with a $7,600 application fee (recalculated triennially for inflation); study-cycle kick-off is December 9, 2026. MISO's restructured Resource Planning Team consolidates interconnection project management, DPP studies, ERAS studies, and tariff administration, with new roles in surplus interconnection and replacement-generation queuing. Hydro and PSH owners in MISO should flag the September 10 DPP deadline and monitor Attachment GGG clarifications on how Network-upgrade costs are recovered when their projects trigger system improvements.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 03b DPP Study Schedule.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Distributed Project Portfolio (DPP) queue-study schedule directly affects interconnection timelines and deliverability for hydro and storage projects seeking queue advancement; delays in stability analysis and TO mitigations cascade across study cycles, impacting when generators can execute GIAs and begin commercial operation.
ai_summary: MISO's Distributed Project Portfolio Study Schedule (version 5/1/2026) shows systematic delays across multiple study cycles through 2027, driven primarily by stability-model audits, transmission-operator (TO) mitigation delays from Ameren, PPI, and NIPSCO, and cascading effects from the 2022 Phase 2 cycle. DPP cycles are progressing through DPP 2 and DPP 3 phases with GIA execution dates extending into 2027; for example, DPP-2022 Central (121 projects, 23,908 MW) is delayed 3–7 calendar days monthly due to stability analysis and mitigation delays. The schedule notes that MISO's Reduced GIP Timeline (effective March 15, 2022) applies to all active cycles, and final reports post 15 business days before completion dates. For hydro and storage interconnection customers, these delays directly extend the time to GIA execution and commercial operation; projects queued in 2021–2025 cycles should monitor their specific region (Central, East ATC/ITC, South, West) and track whether stability or TO-mitigation bottlenecks are the binding constraint on their advancement.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 04 Att GGG (PAC-2024-6)_redlines.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Merchant HVDC Transmission Connection Procedures (MHCP) govern interconnection of external HVDC lines to the transmission system, establishing study, cost-allocation, and injection-rights processes that directly affect deliverability and transmission-access economics for hydro resources seeking to interconnect or export across MISO's footprint.
ai_summary: MISO has posted redlined revisions to Attachment GGG (Merchant HVDC Transmission Connection Procedures) for its June 9, 2026 IPWG meeting. The document defines the terms, scope, and procedural framework for entities seeking to interconnect merchant HVDC transmission lines to MISO's transmission system, including definitions of Connection Facilities, Network Upgrades, Injection Rights, and cost-allocation mechanisms (Common Use Upgrades, Multi-Party Facilities Construction Agreements). While the excerpt provided contains only definitions and scope language (effective July 19, 2018), redlines indicate updates are under review. Hydro owners and independent power producers using HVDC interconnection for long-distance export of generation—particularly from low-hydropower-density regions into MISO or across MISO seams—are directly affected by the study timelines, upgrade cost-sharing rules, and injection-rights certification procedures these procedures establish.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 04 Attachment GGG Improvements (PAC-2024-6).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's clarifications to the MHVDC interconnection procedure (Attachment GGG) affect how non-MISO generation, including hydropower and pumped-storage resources, can interconnect and inject power into MISO — touching deliverability, interconnection pathways, and the cost-recovery treatment of network upgrades that directly impact project economics.
ai_summary: MISO proposes final tariff and business-practice-manual changes to Attachment GGG (MHVDC Interconnection Procedure) to clarify the Module B transmission-service injection pathway as an alternative to Attachment X external Network Resource Interconnection Service, distinguish between Necessary Upgrades and Network Upgrades cost recovery, and update the Multi-Party Facility Construction Agreement. Stakeholders in the April 28 IPWG meeting expressed general support but raised concerns about the scope of Necessary Upgrades in connection studies and cost-recovery pathways for Module B versus Attachment X. MISO plans to file the first tariff change to FERC within 30 days (introducing Limited Operations provisions and clarifying injection/withdrawal-rights scope) and a second filing in Q3–Q4 2026 (updating application forms and clarifying Module B as injection-only, not restricted to External NRIS). Most affected are outside generators—including remote hydropower and pumped-storage facilities seeking to export into MISO—for whom these pathway and cost-allocation rules determine interconnection feasibility and return on investment.

title: 20260609 IPWG Item 05 GI Portal Product Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue portal automation directly affects how hydropower and other generators apply for interconnection service and queue position, touching both the interconnection-queue obligation and the strategic timing/cost of interconnection entry.
ai_summary: MISO launched the Generation Interconnection Application Automation (GIAA) portal in November 2025 and is executing incremental releases (April, May 2026, with July/September/November 2026 planned) to improve application processing automation, data migration from the legacy Grid Unity system, and user experience. The portal now supports Surplus and Replacement applications, Inverter-Based Resource (IBR) uploads, and expanded transformer and Point of Interconnection voltage-level fields. Known issues include file-size limitations, lack of project-list sorting/filtering, and unclear user-type account options—problems that can slow application review or create friction for interconnection customers (generators including hydro and pumped-storage developers). A dedicated product team was established March 1, 2026, with ongoing focus on bug resolution and data cleanup from the legacy system. Generators relying on timely queue processing should monitor the portal's usability and watch for the next release cycle (tentatively July 18, 2026) to assess whether validation and automated-review improvements reduce application review delays.

title: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Agenda
relevance_reason: MISO-PJM interregional transmission planning and FERC Order 1920 implementation directly affect hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and market-seam governance—all strategic options for hydro owners operating across or exporting from the MISO-PJM footprint.
ai_summary: The MISO-PJM IPSAC agenda covers three topics: (1) a Targeted Market Efficiency Project Study update, (2) MISO-PJM FERC Order 1920 compliance progress, and (3) interregional transmission planning status. FERC Order 1920 requires improved interregional coordination and transmission planning between RTOs; implementation here affects how hydro resources in or serving the MISO-PJM seam can compete for capacity, how interregional energy flows are priced and scheduled, and whether transmission constraints limit hydro export capability. The June 8 meeting includes verbal reports on these items with no public comment period advertised in this agenda, but the transmission-planning update and Order 1920 progress are material to hydro owners evaluating long-term positioning in the MISO-PJM region and to any with portfolio exposure across the seam.

title: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 01 TMEP Study Update
relevance_reason: TMEP (Transmission and Market Enhancement Projects) study identifies and evaluates interregional transmission solutions between MISO and PJM, directly affecting the deliverability and revenue opportunity for hydro and storage resources at the seam.
ai_summary: MISO and PJM are conducting a coordinated Transmission and Market Enhancement Projects (TMEP) study to address ~$681 million in persistent congestion on Market-to-Market flowgates between the two RTOs (2024–2025 data). The study, initiated following a March 13, 2026 Issues Review IPSAC decision, will evaluate 23 flowgate candidates and assess whether non-transmission solutions (operator adjustments, market redesigns, or asset-owner flexibility) can relieve binding constraints more cost-effectively than capital projects. Study steps run June–November 2026, with outcomes informing which interregional bottlenecks impede hydro export capability and which may be solvable via operational or market changes rather than new wires. For hydro owners with resources near the MISO-PJM seam, the study outcomes determine whether transmission constraints limiting their revenue will be addressed via infrastructure investment, demand-response/flexibility products, or remain as persistent opportunity costs. The next IPSAC meeting is scheduled for August 17, 2026.

title: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 02 FERC Order 1920 Interregional Compliance _UPDATED
relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 interregional long-term transmission planning compliance will reshape how MISO and PJM coordinate on transmission expansion, directly affecting interregional deliverability of hydro and pumped-storage resources and their strategic transmission-planning participation opportunities.
ai_summary: MISO and PJM are implementing FERC Order 1920/1920-A through two-phase compliance: Phase 1 (regional planning, MISO June 12, 2026; PJM Dec. 12, 2025) and Phase 2 (interregional coordination, due Dec. 12, 2026). The RTOs assert their existing long-term regional planning processes (MISO LRTP, PJM Long-Term Planning) and interregional procedures are generally compliant; key reforms include explicit long-term transmission-needs posting, joint evaluation of interregional facilities, and allowance for any entity to propose interregional projects within regional planning processes. Stakeholder review (redlines July–August 2026, regional committee engagement through October–November 2026) precedes the December 2026 FERC filing. For hydro owners, interregional facility eligibility and transparent cost-benefit evaluation of cross-regional transmission solutions directly affect how PSH and reservoir hydro assets can be sited and valued for export; the expanded entity-proposal rights create a formal pathway for hydro interests to nominate interregional transmission solutions to meet long-term needs, with December 12, 2026 as the RTO filing deadline and stakeholder feedback windows in July–October 2026.

title: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 03 Interregional Transmission Planning Status Update
relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning between MISO and PJM directly affects hydro and PSH deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and strategic options for resources sited in or serving both RTOs, particularly regarding long-range adequacy, benefit-metric development for transmission that may indirectly influence scarcity pricing and capacity accreditation frameworks.
ai_summary: MISO and PJM are jointly developing an expanded interregional transmission planning framework to overcome structural limitations in their Joint Operating Agreement's ability to identify and evaluate interregional solutions, with emphasis on long-range scenario-driven benefit identification under FERC Order 1920. They are stress-testing three benefit metrics (reliability, production-cost, and extreme-weather benefits) through an Interregional Transmission Coordinated Study (ITCS), with a compliance filing expected in Q4 2026 and ongoing coordination of planning assumptions and horizons across MTEP/RTEP cycles. This effort is material to hydro and PSH owners in the MISO-PJM seam because transmission expansion projects evaluated under the new framework could alter interregional transfer limits, change the economic value of storage flexibility across the seam, and shape how long-duration or seasonal hydro resource adequacy is credited in joint reliability studies — owners should monitor the Q4 2026 filing and the benefit-metric definitions MISO and PJM adopt, as they may reframe how interregional hydro exports and PSH cyclic operation are valued in future planning.

title: 20260604 OMS-MISO Survey Results Workshop Presentation.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's 2026 OMS-MISO Survey establishes seasonal resource-accreditation (SAC) forecasts and transitions to Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) methodology for capacity accreditation starting PY 2028/29, directly affecting how hydropower and pumped storage are credited toward resource adequacy and capacity revenue.
ai_summary: MISO's 2026 OMS-MISO Survey projects 15.0 GW/yr of summer capacity additions and 12.4 GW/yr of winter additions over the next five years, with 99.5% participation from resource owners. The region is implementing a transition to Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) accreditation methodology targeted for PY 2028/29, which will value resources based on their contribution during highest-risk hours rather than peak load hours — a shift designed to better capture storage and flexible resources' reliability value when weather and load patterns decouple traditional peaks from risk periods. This methodology change affects how pumped-storage hydro and hydropower are credited in MISO's capacity market, potentially altering their accreditation values and RA-revenue implications; seasonal accreditation also shows winter becoming the critical season as the resource mix evolves, raising strategic value of winter-capable dispatchable resources like hydro.

title: 20260603 SSPM Item 01c Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MTEP26 reliability planning and generation interconnection study directly determine which projects receive transmission investment and interconnection priority—shaping the strategic deliverability and resource-adequacy outlook for hydro and storage assets in MISO's South region.
ai_summary: MISO's South Subregional Planning Meeting on June 3, 2026 will present the MTEP26 (Midterm Expansion Plan) reliability planning scope, schedule, and proposed projects for the region. Zackary Bearden (MISO), Amanda Schiro (MISO), Nestor Martinez, Bradley Burns, Andrew Tiedt, and Jonah Norton will lead discussions on reliability-driven transmission projects and generation interconnection study plans. The agenda does not specify decisions or comment deadlines, but MTEP26 project proposals presented here will establish transmission-investment priorities and interconnection-queue resolution for the South region—directly affecting the future deliverability and resource-adequacy value of existing and prospective hydro and storage capacity in MISO. The next planning meeting is scheduled for September 4, 2026.

title: 20260603 SSPM Item 02 Review of Generation Interconnection Study Plans.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's generator interconnection queue status and network upgrade costs directly affect hydro and storage developers' interconnection timelines, deliverability assessments, and cost allocation — core strategic planning inputs for hydro plant uprates, new PSH development, and queue position decisions.
ai_summary: MISO's South Subregional Planning Meeting reviews the current status of the Definitive Planning Phase (DPP) generator interconnection queue, with DPP-2021-South now finalized showing $1.14 billion in network upgrade costs (thermal, stability, short-circuit, deliverability/NRIS, and direct-assigned TO upgrades) to accommodate predominantly solar facilities. DPP-2022, DPP-2023, and DPP-2025 Phase-2 studies are in progress with cost estimates pending. For hydro and PSH developers, the scale of shared network-upgrade obligations, the timeline for completion of DPP cost estimates, and the composition of the queue (solar-dominant, implying fast-ramping variable generation) influence both transmission-cost risk allocation and the locational/operational advantages of flexible resources like pumped storage. The latest DPP schedule is available on MISO's website; Jesse Duhn (jduhn@misoenergy.org) is the South DPP Project Manager.

title: 20260603 SSPM Item 03 MISO26 Series Model Development Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's annual power-flow and dynamic model development directly supports transmission planning, resource-adequacy studies (PRA, LOLE), and system-support-resource (SSR) studies that determine which generation (including hydro) must operate for reliability and which can be retired; model assumptions on wind/solar dispatch and capacity credits also establish the baseline against which hydro's scarcity value and RA accreditation are measured.
ai_summary: MISO has completed the MISO26 series power-flow models and is building dynamic models (pre-final review due early June 2026, final release mid-June). These models include base and sensitivity cases across 2028, 2031, and 2036 horizons with specific wind and solar capacity-credit assumptions and load profiles (summer peak, light load, winter peak, shoulder). The models feed MTEP transmission-expansion studies, Generation Interconnection queue assessments, System Support Resource studies (which determine forced-operation obligations on existing generation for reliability), and Planning Resource Auction adequacy calculations. Hydro owners' ability to negotiate must-offer exemptions, avoid reliability-driven RMR or mothball processes, and establish RA capacity credits depends on these baseline assumptions; changes in model inputs (load forecasts, wind/solar penetration, transmission paths) shift scarcity signals and the effective capacity value of existing hydro. The document also flags that ERAG MMWG 2026 power-flow model development begins June 2026, affecting Eastern Interconnection–wide resource-adequacy and interregional transfer studies in which hydro participates.

title: 20260603 SSPM Item 04a MTEP26 Reliability Planning Scope Overview.pdf
relevance_reason: MTEP26 transmission planning scope directly affects hydro deliverability, interconnection queue management, and interregional transfer capability — all critical to hydro revenue and dispatch optionality in MISO and neighboring regions.
ai_summary: MISO's MTEP26 (2026 Transmission Expansion Plan) targets 574 new projects, with 98 proposed in the South region, driven predominantly by load growth and large data-center load additions; the plan includes 4,000+ MW of new spot load in the South and sharp growth in generator interconnection projects, particularly in Arkansas. The reliability assessment scope is consistent with NERC TPL standards and covers steady-state, transient stability, voltage, transfer capability, and nuclear plant assessments across 2, 5, and 10-year horizons. For hydro owners, massive load growth and queue expansion directly increase scarcity and system stress, raising energy-price and ancillary-service value, but also compress interconnection timelines and deliverability thresholds for new or uprated hydro capacity; the South region's concentration of load-growth projects and generator interconnection requests elevates competition for transmission headroom that existing reservoir hydro may need to serve or export energy. Key deadlines: August 19, 2026 (data-entry cutoff for SPM3 presentation), September 28–October 12 (stakeholder feedback window), December 3 (full Board approval).

title: 20260603 WSPM Item 01c Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MTEP26 reliability planning and generation interconnection studies directly shape transmission deliverability, resource adequacy constructs, and long-term strategic capacity decisions affecting hydro owner revenue and strategic options.
ai_summary: MISO's West Subregional Planning Meeting on June 3, 2026, will review generation interconnection study plans, MTEP26 model development, and reliability planning scope and recommended projects for the 2026 transmission expansion plan. The meeting agenda includes status on MTEP26 reliability studies (scope, schedule, projects) and recommended reliability projects, with open stakeholder input. This is a planning gate where interconnection queue processing, transmission bottleneck identification, and resource-adequacy-driven transmission build-outs are presented — developments that determine where new hydro or pumped-storage capacity can be sited, what transmission constraints affect revenue-producing flows, and whether existing hydro is accredited in regional adequacy studies. No specific projects, deadlines, or positions are stated in this agenda document.

title: 20260603 WSPM Item 02 Review of Generation Interconnection Study Plans.pdf

DPP-2022-West Phase-1 alone carries $11.9 billion in estimated costs (excluding TOIF), while multiple phases remain in study with costs TBD. Hydro and storage projects in MISO's queue face potential cost-sharing liability and delivery timelines shaped by these study outcomes; developers—particularly those seeking surplus interconnection or uprates at existing facilities—depend on these schedules to plan commercial operation dates and capital deployment. Contact points for queue and study questions are Kurt Guthmueller (West/ATC DPP Project Planner) and Kyle Trotter (DPP Manager).
