title: 20260602 CSPM Item 03 MISO26 Series Model Development Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's MISO26 series model development—including powerflow, dynamics, and resource adequacy modeling—establishes the baseline simulation inputs for Generation Interconnection studies, SSR (System Support Resource) studies, LOLE (Loss of Load Expectation) studies, and PRA (Planning Resource Auction) studies that directly determine hydro plant accreditation, transmission deliverability, and seasonal capacity value.
ai_summary: MISO reports completion of MISO26 powerflow models and ongoing development of MISO26 dynamic models (prefinal review in early June, final release mid-June) and initiation of ERAG MMWG 2026 powerflow modeling in June 2026. These model series are foundational inputs to Generation Interconnection queue studies, SSR retirement assessments, Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) studies that set Planning Reserve Margin requirements, and the Planning Resource Auction (PRA) that determines resource adequacy obligations and seasonal accreditation. The models include wind and solar capacity-credit assumptions (18.2% wind, 50% solar in base cases) that drive the effective capacity value assigned to all dispatchable resources including hydro in capacity and adequacy determinations. Hydro owners relying on RA showings, interconnection queue progress, or SSR/retirement studies should monitor data requests: MISO issued dynamics data requests on March 28 with April 25 deadline (now passed); powerflow trial 1 information is due to Powertech in late June and MISO will email member requests for required updates.

title: 20260602 CSPM Item 04a MTEP26 Reliability Planning Scope Overview.pdf
relevance_reason: MTEP26 reliability planning scope addresses transmission expansion driven by load growth and generator interconnection, which directly affects hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and strategic options for hydro owners participating in MISO's 15-state footprint.
ai_summary: MISO's MTEP26 transmission planning cycle, entering subregional planning meetings in June 2026, targets 574 new Appendix A projects (190 in the Central region) with $4 billion in Expedited Project Review (EPR) investment as of May 2026. The planning process uses NERC reliability standards (TPL-001-5, FAC-013/014, PRC-023) across six analytical dimensions: steady-state, transient, voltage stability, transfer capability, nuclear plant, and deliverability assessment for 2/5/10-year and 20+ year horizons. For hydro owners, the scope is material: load-growth-driven transmission projects directly alter export pathways and scarcity signals that determine when hydro units clear energy and ancillary-services markets; interconnection of new generation (especially in Central region with a sharp uptick in Generator Interconnection requests) shifts locational marginal prices and ramping needs; and large-load additions (data centers, electrification) raise baseline energy scarcity and extend the value horizon for flexible dispatchable resources. The MTEP Portal is the single source of project data; stakeholder feedback windows close September 15 and October 27, with final Board approval by December 3, 2026.

title: 20260602 ESPM Item 03 MISO26 Series Model Development Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's model-series development directly supports transmission planning, resource adequacy, and generation-interconnection studies that affect hydro deliverability, seasonal adequacy assessments, and competitive positioning in MISO.
ai_summary: MISO reports completion of MISO26 powerflow models and progress on dynamics models (pre-final review in early June, final release mid-June), which feed MTEP, PRA (Planning Resource Auction / resource adequacy), generation-interconnection queue studies, and Eastern Interconnection reliability assessments (ERAG MMWG). These models incorporate wind and solar capacity credits (18.2% wind, 50% solar in base cases) and sensitivity scenarios across 2028–2036 with varying load and renewable-dispatch assumptions. The model series is foundational to MISO's seasonal adequacy studies and transmission-deliverability analysis—hydro owners rely on these inputs to understand seasonal scarcity conditions, interregional transfer constraints, and system needs driving future ancillary-service and energy prices. Member feedback on pre-final dynamics models is due mid-June; stakeholders should monitor for model assumptions (e.g., renewable dispatch profiles, hydro availability representation) that may misstate seasonal energy adequacy or undervalue dispatchable resources.

title: 20260602 ESPM Minutes 20260210.pdf
relevance_reason: MTEP26 transmission planning process identifies large load growth and generator interconnection as drivers of $8.8B in proposed projects; load growth and interconnection queue developments directly affect hydro asset deliverability and scarcity value.
ai_summary: MISO held its East Subregional Planning Meeting (SPM1) on February 10, 2026, to preview MTEP26 transmission planning scope. MTEP26 encompasses $8.8B in proposed projects across local, regional, and interregional needs, with the East Region alone proposing 102 projects totaling $1.123B—dominated by age/condition work ($426M, 70%) but with sharp growth in generator interconnection projects. Notably, Expedited Project Review (EPR) requests are driven primarily by load growth spanning the entire MISO footprint. The active generator interconnection queue consists of 754 projects (East Region ITC: 131 projects with 27.38k MW). Large load additions are listed as a core reliability study item alongside interregional planning with PJM and SPP. Next steps: MISO will perform independent reliability assessments of proposed projects and alternatives; stakeholders and TOs should continue using the MTEP Portal and provide input on proposed projects. SPM2 is scheduled for June 2, 2026. For hydro owners, the surge in load-growth-driven transmission expansion and interconnection activity signals increasing scarcity value and potential transmission constraints on hydro export capability; the portfolio of projects affecting Michigan (a key hydro-adjacent region) warrants tracking through completion.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: NYISO is proposing changes to ICAP Manual rules governing seasonal capacity accreditation (UDR/EDR elections) and must-offer requirements for dispatchable resources, which directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage facilities are accredited and obligated to participate in the capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee will vote on proposed revisions to the ICAP Manual and attachments governing Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements, specifically seasonal UDR (Unforced Demand Response) and EDR (Emergency Demand Response) elections and must-offer requirements. The presentation and vote are scheduled for June 18, 2026, 10:05–10:35 am. The mechanic affects hydro and PSH accreditation methodology: changes to seasonal capacity elections and must-offer obligations determine whether and how run-of-river, reservoir, and pumped-storage facilities count toward winter RA showings and what dispatch obligations attach to that capacity. Owners of dispatchable hydro and PSH should monitor this vote, as the revised rules will reset their seasonal capacity election rights and operational requirements.

title: 2 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements – Seasonal UDR/EDR Elections and Must Offer Requirement: Proposed ICAP Manual and Attachment Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO establishes seasonal must-offer requirements and penalties for holders of External Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest of State Deliverability Rights (EDRs), which directly affects how external capacity—including hydropower—can be exported from and must be offered to meet NYCA obligations, and creates an enforcement mechanism (penalty methodology in new Attachment Q) that changes an owner's compliance exposure and dispatch obligations.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing seasonal elections for External UDRs and EDRs starting May 1, 2027 (2027–2028 Capability Year), with elections due August 1, 2026. Holders of External UDRs or EDRs must submit distinct seasonal elections (one for Summer, one for Winter) to return capacity to the NYCA, or face a must-offer requirement: capacity not returned must be offered in each ICAP Spot Market Auction or certified to meet LSE minimum UCAP requirements, and failure triggers a penalty calculated using Market Clearing Price and a new Attachment Q methodology converting ICAP to UCAP. This reshapes the options available to external capacity owners (including hydro exporters to NYCA) by forcing them to commit seasonally to either export or domestic service, with financial penalties for non-compliance; pumped-storage and reservoir hydro operators holding external rights into New York will need to evaluate seasonal dispatch strategies against the penalty risk. The proposal was filed with FERC in Docket No. ER26-1431 with requested effective date June 28, 2026; ICAP Manual revisions become effective one business day after the 10-day appeal period if approved (by July 7, 2026 absent appeal or FERC rejection).

title: 2a Draft ICAP Manual
relevance_reason: NYISO's ICAP Manual governs capacity accreditation, duration-adjustment factors, and reporting requirements that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are valued and compensated in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's draft Installed Capacity (ICAP) Manual 17.0, effective April 2, 2026, specifies minimum capacity requirements, unforced-capacity calculations, and resource-specific test conditions and reporting obligations for all capacity suppliers. The manual explicitly addresses energy-limited resources (including hydro), co-located storage resources (directly relevant to PSH), duration-adjustment factors, and DER aggregations. Sections 4.1.1 and 4.1.4 detail Energy Duration Limitations and Duration Adjustment Factors for installed-capacity suppliers and co-located storage; Section 4.2 governs Demonstrated Maximum Net Capability (DMNC) testing that determines hydro's accredited capacity; and Section 4.4 establishes monthly operating-data reporting requirements (including intermittent power resources and energy-limited resources). The document is in draft for discussion at the Business Issues Committee (BIC) meeting on June 18, 2026, following circulation at the June 9, 2026 ICAPWG meeting (yellow-highlighted changes noted). Hydro and PSH owners need to review the updated duration-adjustment methodology, DMNC test procedures, and state-of-charge rules for co-located storage to confirm accreditation treatment and compliance deadlines.

title: 2b Motion
relevance_reason: NYISO proposes seasonal capacity accreditation and must-offer requirement changes affecting hydro and PSH revenue recognition and operational obligations in the Installed Capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved revisions to the Installed Capacity Manual establishing seasonal Unforced Demand Response (UDR) and Energy Demand Response (EDR) elections and must-offer requirements tied to Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements (FERC Docket ER26-1431). The proposal changes how hydro and storage assets are accredited for capacity in seasonal winter conditions and imposes new must-offer obligations during those periods. The revisions become effective only upon FERC acceptance; rejection triggers automatic rescission. Reservoir hydropower and pumped-storage operators face potential changes to their capacity revenue recognition and dispatch obligations during the winter season, with implementation contingent on FERC's decision in ER26-1431.

title: 00 Agenda
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 and Storm Watch Redispatch revisions touch interconnection/deliverability rules and real-time dispatch protocols that affect hydro asset dispatch obligations and transmission-dependent revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee agenda for June 11, 2026 includes two items with hydro relevance: (1) Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 (action item, presenter Prasad Shinde, 10:35–10:50), which may affect interconnection capacity and delivery rights for new or existing hydro resources; (2) Storm Watch Redispatch manual revisions (action item, presenter Afreen Vahora, 10:50–11:05), which modifies real-time dispatch instructions during grid stress and could change hydro's dispatch flexibility and revenue opportunity during high-value periods. Both items are scheduled for action/vote at this meeting. Additionally, the Summer 2026 Operating Study update (Raj Dontireddy) provides seasonal adequacy context relevant to hydro's peaking and flexibility value.

title: 03 Operations Report - May 2026
relevance_reason: Monthly operational metrics tracking NYISO generation mix, hydro/pumped-storage capacity and availability, intermittent resource forecasting, curtailment, and transmission constraints that directly affect hydro dispatch and revenue opportunities.
ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 operations report shows hydro (including pumped-storage hydro) at 6,324 MW nameplate capacity, comprising 12% of NYCA generation resources. The report tracks month-to-month generation mix, day-ahead market capacity unavailable in real-time (broken by fuel type including hydro), wind and solar curtailment by transmission constraint, net load ramps, and reserve activations. Two new transmission projects (Smart Path Connect and additional 345 kV facilities) came online in May, and the Champlain Hudson Power Express merchant transmission facility entered the real-time energy market on 5/13, potentially improving deliverability for hydro exports. Hydro owners use this baseline data to assess system balance, transmission constraints limiting their dispatch, and competitive positioning versus wind/solar capacity (11,547 MW intermittent nameplate vs. 6,324 MW hydro). The report shows May 2026 wind and FTM solar curtailment at 2.7% system-wide, with specific transmission constraints listed, affecting revenue for hydro resources competing in the same nodal zones.

title: 05a Summer 2026 Operations Study - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Changes in transmission infrastructure (CHPE HVDC addition, Smart Path Connect upgrades, interface limit redistribution) and generation retirements affect hydro dispatch patterns, interregional transfer capability, and revenue opportunity from exports and locational scarcity pricing—all of which touch hydro's strategic deliverability and the value of flexible ramping capacity.
ai_summary: NYISO's Summer 2026 operations study reports 1,225 MW of generation retirements (including large thermal units like Danskammer 532 MW and Narrows 352 MW) offset by 225 MW of solar and storage additions. Major transmission projects—Champlain Hudson Power Express HVDC, Smart Path Connect 345 kV stations, and Hertel–Astoria additions—are now modeled in service. These changes redistribute flows and alter interface thermal limits: Total East interface limit decreased 125 MW, UPNY-ConEd increased 250 MW, UPNY-SENY increased 225 MW, and Moses South increased 250 MW. Sprain Brook Dunwoodie South interface limit was raised to 4,225 MW and Total East to 4,050 MW. The study's status is a June 2026 presentation update to the Operating Committee; no forward deadlines are stated. For hydro owners, the net effect is mixed: retirement of thermal generation reduces dispatchable competition and may elevate real-time energy prices and ramping value, while the HVDC and transmission upgrades improve northern import capability and may compress NY price separations, affecting both in-state dispatchable hydro revenue and Northwest-to-NY export economics. Pumped-storage operators should monitor how the reduced Moses South thermal limits and increased UPNY interfaces change the voltage-support and frequency-response service opportunities in those zones.

title: 05c Summer 2026 Operating Study Appendices Draft - Redline
relevance_reason: Summer operating study details interregional transmission flows, HVdc converter schedules, and phase-angle regulator dispatch that directly affect hydropower export deliverability and revenue from cross-border power transfers — particularly from TransÉnergie (Quebec hydro) into NYISO and ISONE.
ai_summary: NYISO's Summer 2026 Operating Study appendices specify base-case assumptions for generation dispatch, load, and interregional transfers. TransÉnergie is modeled exporting 2,362 MW to NYISO, 1,500 MW to ISO-NE via Sandy Pond HVdc, and 1,250 MW to Ontario (IESO) — all major hydro corridors. HVdc converter schedules list in-service status for Champlain Hudson Power Express (1,250 MW), Sandy Pond (1,500 MW), and four other TransÉnergie HVdc links (total ~3,300 MW). Phase-angle regulator dispatch on key NY interfaces (Ramapo, Uniondale, and cross-Sound) is specified to manage congestion. This study informs summer transfer limits and is foundational for RA adequacy and reserve requirement calculations; export-dependent hydro owners (Northwest suppliers to WECC/California, Quebec hydro exporters) should verify their assumed dispatch levels match actual operating conditions and test sensitivity to transmission constraints.

title: 06a Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 - Presentation
relevance_reason: NYISO Expedited Deliverability Study determines which generation and storage projects can deliver power without system upgrades — directly affecting interconnection timelines, costs, and deliverability certainty for new hydro, PSH, or co-located resources seeking NYISO queue status.
ai_summary: NYISO's Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 analyzes three projects (Curtis/Palmer natural gas/hydro hybrid, Soltage battery energy storage, RP New York BESS) for deliverability at requested CRIS (capacity resource interconnection service) levels without triggering system-wide upgrades. All three projects passed their respective interface and byway tests and are deemed fully deliverable at requested capacity: Curtis/Palmer at 58.8 MW summer, Soltage at 20 MW, and RP New York BESS at 5.1 MW. The study is on track for Operating Committee approval with a 5-business-day decision window for each interconnection customer to accept or reject its deliverable MW; non-acceptance triggers re-study of remaining projects. For any hydro or hybrid generator seeking interconnection in NYISO, this procedural pathway offers faster deliverability determination than full Cluster Study if transmission constraints are minimal — but only for projects already through Class Year or Cluster study phases for ERIS (energy resource interconnection service).

title: 06b Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 - Report
relevance_reason: NYISO interconnection deliverability study determines whether a hydropower project (Curtis/Palmer Hydro) can inject its requested capacity without network upgrades — directly affecting hydro interconnection timelines, costs, and strategic feasibility of delivery into high-value zones.
ai_summary: NYISO's Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 concludes that the Curtis/Palmer Hydro Project in the Rest-of-State Capacity Region is fully deliverable at its requested CRIS level without requiring system upgrades. The study evaluated three projects (Curtis/Palmer Hydro, Soltage battery, RP New York BESS) using 2029 summer peak conditions, transfer-limit analysis across capacity regions, and contingency screening under NPCC/NYSRC criteria. For hydro interconnection customers, a 'fully deliverable' determination removes the risk of being forced to undersize their injection or fund costly network upgrades to achieve their requested MW — streamlining path to commercial operation. The analysis covers methodology, base-case modeling, and transfer-capability assessments but contains no stated comment deadlines or next procedural steps in this excerpt.

title: 07a Storm Watch Redispatch - Presentation
relevance_reason: Tariff and settlement methodology changes to Storm Watch Redispatch (Transmission Security Analysis) cost allocation affect how real-time dispatch costs are computed and allocated to load zones, which can influence uplift/make-whole charges borne by dispatchable resources including hydro.
ai_summary: NYISO is clarifying its Services Tariff (MST 4.1.8) and revising the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual to document a long-term improvement to Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost calculation. The enhancement will use Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass (instead of initial Day-Ahead schedules) and real-time schedules to determine flows across TSA constraints, while keeping the real-time shadow-price methodology and Zone J customer allocation unchanged. This refinement stems from review of July 2025 TSA settlements that exceeded $53 million and aims to more accurately reflect the actual redispatch obligation. NYISO is seeking votes on the proposed tariff revisions at the June 2026 Membership Committee meeting.

title: 07b Storm Watch Redispatch - T&D Manual - Redline
relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch procedures affect real-time dispatch operations, transmission constraints, and emergency redispatch authority — core operational obligations and dispatch mechanisms that touch hydro's real-time pricing exposure and must-offer/dispatch-instruction compliance.
ai_summary: NYISO's Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) redlines the Storm Watch Redispatch procedure, a real-time operational protocol for managing transmission constraints and emergency conditions. The document is in DRAFT status, accepted by the BIC on 04/15/2026 and Operating Committee on 04/16/2026, with an effective date of 05/01/2026. Storm Watch Redispatch is a corrective control strategy that allows NYISO to issue real-time dispatch instructions to manage transmission violations and security constraints — a tool that can override normal market clearing and compel hydro resources to adjust output. For hydro operators, the relevance lies in the operational obligation to accept Storm Watch redispatch directives, which may suppress market-cleared prices during constraint periods or require uneconomic output changes. Reservoir and run-of-river hydro are equally exposed to these dispatch obligations; the mechanism affects real-time revenue predictability and compliance burden.

title: Final Motions
relevance_reason: Motion #2 approves the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02, which directly addresses interconnection and deliverability—a core strategic option for hydro owners evaluating uprates, new resources, and transmission-constrained resource placement.
stakeholders: Greg Yozzo (Central Hudson Gas & Electric Corp.)
Jason Ragona (Consolidated Edison Co. of NY, Inc.)
Scott Knewasser (New York State Electric & Gas (NYSEG) / Rochester Gas and Electric Corp.)
ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee approved three routine motions on June 11, 2026: prior-meeting minutes, the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 Report, and revisions to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual for Storm Watch Redispatch. The deliverability study approval is substantive for hydro owners: deliverability studies determine whether generation—including hydro uprates and new hydro interconnection—can physically export power and receive full capacity accreditation, and expedited studies typically flag resource-constraint relief needed for queue-clearing. The Storm Watch manual revisions affect real-time dispatch and redispatch authority during extreme weather, a standing operational obligation for synchronous hydro units. No comment deadlines or further votes are stated in this motion document.

title: Proposed Motions
relevance_reason: Motion #2 approves an Expedited Deliverability Study that affects interconnection and transmission deliverability for new and existing resources, and Motion #3 modifies dispatch operations manual rules that govern how generation responds to grid conditions — both touch hydro's operational obligations and strategic positioning for interconnection and uprates.
ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee approves the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 Report and revisions to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual related to 'Storm Watch Redispatch.' The deliverability study directly affects which new and existing generation (including hydro uprates and storage projects) can physically deliver power; study approval clears the path for resource interconnection decisions. The Storm Watch redispatch manual revisions change how NYISO instructs synchronous generation to operate during weather events — a compliance obligation for conventional hydro units. No comment deadline or vote date beyond the June 11, 2026 approval is stated in this motion summary.

title: 2 Draft April 15, 2026 Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO capacity accreditation and winter ICAP demand-curve methodology directly affects pumped-storage and conventional hydro revenue through seasonal accreditation factors (CAFs) and locational capacity-market clearing prices; the CHPE UDRs decision also touches interregional hydro deliverability and market-seam governance.
ai_summary: NYISO's BIC approved tariff revisions to align winter ICAP demand curves for 2026–2027 to account for potential capacity supply via CHPE (Champlain Hudson Power Express) UDRs from HydroQuébec into New York City, contingent on CHPE commencing summer participation. The proposal resets winter-peak and shoulder ICAP demand curves and their UCAP translations using capacity-accreditation factors (CAFs) that assume CHPE participation ('CHPE-In' parameters). Stakeholders raised concerns that actual winter capacity supply via CHPE could diverge from the 0-MW assumption for winter peaks, potentially inverting NYC winter-to-shoulder price signals and reducing accreditation revenue for existing capacity suppliers. The NYISO rejected a lower-curve stakeholder alternative and declined to impose a prohibition on CHPE winter sales, asserting the proposal maintains revenue sufficiency for the reference technology. The proposal is pending FERC approval under ER26-1431 and will become effective on FERC's permit date; it resets baseline assumptions for the broader Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements design scheduled for 2027–2028. Most exposed are existing hydro and PSH owners in NYC and rest-of-state capacity zones whose seasonal RA valuations and clearing-price signals are recalibrated.

title: 2 Draft May 13, 2026 Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: capacity market accreditation and participation rules (CHPE UDRs, capacity parameter changes), automatic ICAP surplus-offer rules affecting capacity availability and revenue, and RA/reliability planning updates all touch hydro's capacity revenue streams and strategic options in NYISO; also load-forecasting and DER participation developments relevant to hydro's competitive position.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved an Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers proposal (Motion #1, May 13, 2026) requiring automatic offering of surplus capacity at $0.00/kW-month with clearing price payment, with May 2027 implementation. Separately, CHPE transmission line commenced real-time energy-market operation May 13, 2026 and capacity-market participation July 2026 delivery month, triggering changes in NYISO capacity-market parameters that will affect existing resources' capacity availability ratings starting July 2026. The committee also reviewed ongoing Electric System Planning and Installed Capacity Working Group activities addressing reliability enhancements (FERC Order 1920 compliance), 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook updates, DER unavailability methodologies, and flexible large-load wholesale participation. For hydro owners, the surplus-offer rule eliminates the option to withhold resale capacity (historically 2.1 MW unoffered Jan 2026), forcing systematic market clearing at spotauction prices; the CHPE parameter shift reallocates capacity value across the system, requiring owners with reduced ICAP ratings to secure makeup capacity or release excess through July 2026 auctions.

title: 3 Market Operations Report
relevance_reason: NYISO Market Operations Report contains energy price formation data, uplift/make-whole mechanisms, ancillary services pricing (regulation, reserves, voltage support, black start), and cost-allocation developments directly affecting hydro revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Operations Report (June 2026) shows energy and ancillary services cost composition for NYISO customers through May 2026, revealing material shifts in price formation and uplift structures. LBMP (locational energy price) has risen sharply to $43.63/MWh in May 2026 YTD average vs. $36.99/MWh in May 2025; year-to-date NYISO average cost/MWh stands at $102.99 (May 2026) versus $80.72 (May 2025). Uplift (make-whole payments) shows month-to-month volatility, with statewide uplift share swinging between positive $1.68/MWh (January 2026) and negative values in subsequent months. Voltage support and black start services remain stable at approximately $0.14–$0.38/MWh monthly, while reserve and regulation costs track energy scarcity. The report's detailed monthly breakdown by LBMP, NTAC (transmission), reserves, regulation, uplift components, and ancillary services provides visibility into how NYISO's price-formation mechanisms and market-mitigation rules are translating to customer costs — critical for hydro operators managing opportunity costs and dispatch decisions.

title: 4a MST 26.4 redline
relevance_reason: This tariff redline modifies NYISO's credit and collateral requirements for market participants, including specific provisions affecting energy and ancillary services bids, import/export transactions, and capacity market participation — revenue streams and operational obligations that directly shape hydro and PSH participation economics.
ai_summary: NYISO has redlined tariff section 26.4 governing Operating and Bidding Requirements — the credit collateral framework that participants must post to trade energy, ancillary services, and participate in capacity and TCC auctions. The revision clarifies calculation formulas for the Energy and Ancillary Services Component (with separate factors for prepaid vs. non-prepaid customers), refines Import and Export Credit Requirements using 98th and 97th percentile price differentials respectively over rolling one- and five-year lookback windows, and specifies how historical price data is weighted and applied when insufficient data exists at a location. For hydro and PSH operators, this affects the upfront credit burden to bid flexible energy and ramping products, particularly for facilities engaged in import/export arbitrage or frequent real-time adjustment — the collateral requirement formulas directly reduce available capital to deploy in energy or AS markets. Most exposed are PSH and run-of-river operators using interregional transactions to optimize revenue, and any facility with volatile net-settlement exposure. No explicit deadline or next-step action is stated in this redline excerpt.

title: 5 Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) Costs: Tariff & Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost settlement and uplift allocation directly affects NYISO energy market price formation and cost recovery mechanisms that apply to all dispatchable resources including hydropower.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes tariff and manual revisions to clarify the calculation of Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) costs—uplift charges triggered by emergency transmission constraints during severe weather. The Long-Term Improvement will enhance Day-Ahead and real-time flow calculations by using Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass and real-time schedules to determine flows across TSA constraints, while keeping the real-time Shadow Price and allocation to Zone J customers unchanged. NYISO will seek votes on manual revisions at the June Oversight Committee and tariff revisions at the June Management Committee. This affects how NYISO recovers emergency redispatch costs from the market and thus the magnitude of uplift/make-whole payments that hydro and other dispatchable resources encounter during scarcity events—clarifying the calculation methodology may reduce settlement volatility following extreme events like the July 2025 storms that triggered $53 million in initial costs.

title: 5a Transmission & Dispatch Manual redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual redline update governs NYISO real-time dispatch, commitment, monitoring, and transmission operations — the core operational procedures that determine when and how hydro resources are called upon, constrained, and compensated for reliability services and energy delivery.
ai_summary: NYISO's redlined Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) updates operational procedures for real-time commitment, dispatch monitoring, transmission operations, and voltage/reactive power control. The manual covers reserve calculations, regulation requirements, corrective control strategies, and phase-angle regulator operations — all of which affect when hydro must respond, how its flexibility is valued in real-time dispatch, and revenue exposure to constraint relief and emergency operations. The document is in draft for Business Issues Committee discussion (BIC acceptance dated 04/15/2026, Operations Committee 04/16/2026) and effective 05/01/2026. Most directly material are sections on real-time commitment procedures (6.1), reserve and regulation requirements (3.1.4), and voltage/reactive support via STATCOM and SVC guidance (4.2.3) — all operational levers that determine whether hydro is selected for ancillary services or energy dispatch in competitive real-time markets.

title: 5b Motion
relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch Costs tariff revisions may alter dispatch procedures, cost allocation, and operational obligations for dispatchable resources including hydropower during weather emergencies — a category of dispatch instruction and settlement changes that affect revenue and compliance.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee recommends approval of tariff and manual changes for 'Storm Watch Redispatch Costs,' covering both Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff revisions and updates to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual. The motion advances the proposal to the Management Committee for further approval. The document does not specify the substance of the redispatch-cost changes, their impact on dispatch procedures or settlement, or affected resource classes; the presentation titled 'Storm Watch Redispatch Costs: Tariff and Manual Revisions' (referenced but not included here) would contain the operational and financial mechanism details necessary to assess whether hydro operators face new cost-allocation rules, dispatch constraints, or state-of-charge/operational mandates during storm conditions.

title: 6b Accounting & Billing Manual redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Settlement and billing manual redlines affect how hydro operators' energy and ancillary services revenues are calculated, metered, invoiced, and disputed—foundational operational and financial obligations.
stakeholders: NYISO Customer Settlements (New York Independent System Operator)
Randy A. Bowers (New York Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: NYISO has issued a draft redline (version 7.0, effective 04/30/2026) of its Accounting and Billing Manual for Business Issues Committee approval (scheduled 06/10/2026). The manual governs settlement processing, invoicing schedules, meter data management, challenge and dispute procedures, and supplier settlements for energy (DAM and real-time), ancillary services, and demand response. Sections 4 and 5 detail energy and ancillary-services settlement mechanics for generators (including hydro and energy storage resources), encompassing Bid Production Cost Guarantees, balancing-market uplift, and voltage-support and regulation payments—the revenue streams on which hydro operators depend. Hydro operators and pumped-storage owners must track redline changes to settlement rules, metering timelines (including meter-data revision sunset provisions and resettlement procedures in section 1.4–1.5), and dispute deadlines, as errors in these procedures directly affect cash settlement.

title: 7 Proposed Changes to Attachment N of the ICAP Manual
relevance_reason: NYISO proposes seasonal translation factors for run-of-river hydro and intermittent resources affecting UCAP accreditation and capacity-requirement calculations in IRM/LCR studies, directly impacting hydro's RA value and seasonal revenue recognition.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes revisions to Attachment N of the ICAP Manual to establish separate summer and winter translation factors for Limited Control Run-of-River Hydro Resources and Intermittent Power Resources. Currently, translation factors (which convert Installed Capacity to Unforced Capacity for RA calculations) are computed using June–August production data; the proposal adds winter factors using December–February data, weighted by hourly LOLE factors from the most recent LCR study base case. The revision aligns with NYISO's Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC on 2/18/2026, effective 6/28/2026, targeted for 2027–2028 Capability Year implementation) and responds to observed winter LOLE in the 2026–2027 IRM study. The document also proposes a formula clarification to better document the current hourly LOLE-weighted calculation method. Run-of-river and seasonal-variable hydro owners will see their RA accreditation shift between seasons—winter production now counts toward winter capacity needs separately—which may raise or lower total RA credits depending on the seasonal production profile relative to winter LOLE weighting.

title: 7a ICAP Manual Appendix Attachment N redline
relevance_reason: ATTACHMENT N establishes the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro in NYISO's capacity market, directly affecting hydro's installed-capacity accreditation and thus revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO's ICAP Manual Attachment N (redlined draft, dated June 2026) governs how intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources translate their nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (UCAP) for capacity-market accreditation. This translation-factor methodology is the core mechanism by which variable hydro (which cannot be reliably dispatched at full output across all conditions) receives a derated ICAP credit versus synchronous resources, directly reducing the capacity revenue such hydro can claim in NYISO's auctions. The attachment specifies the procedure by which hydro owners request and substantiate the calculation of these factors, affecting which hydro plants qualify as energy-limited resources (Attachment M) and how their seasonal or weather-dependent output profile is translated into a single UCAP value for RA showings. No comment deadline or next step is stated in the excerpt provided.

title: 7b ICAP Manual Appendix Attachment N incremental redline
relevance_reason: ICAP Manual Attachment N describes the procedure for calculating unforced-capacity translation factors for run-of-river hydro and intermittent resources—directly affecting how NYISO accredits run-of-river hydro in the capacity market and thus hydro's RA participation and revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO is revising ICAP Manual Attachment N, which governs the calculation of translation factors (the fraction of installed capacity that counts as unforced capacity) for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources in NYISO's capacity market. This redline was circulated for the June 2, 2026 ICAP WG meeting and is flagged for discussion at the June 10, 2026 Business Issues Committee. The translation-factor methodology determines what fraction of a run-of-river plant's nameplate capacity NYISO credits toward a seller's capacity obligation—a critical parameter for run-of-river owners' RA revenue and portfolio planning. The document is in draft-for-discussion status; specific redline content is not fully visible in the excerpt, but the procedural revision is scheduled for committee action.

title: 7c ICAP Manual Appendix Attachment N clean
relevance_reason: Attachment N specifies the NYISO procedure for calculating translation factors for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources, directly affecting how these units' unforced capacity is determined and accredited in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: This is NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual Appendix, issued June 2026, covering capacity-market procedures and definitions. Attachment N (referenced in the table of contents but not fully excerpted here) addresses the translation-factor calculation procedure for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro—the mechanism by which NYISO converts such resources' nameplate or maximum output into their contribution to installed capacity (unforced capacity, or UCAP). This translation-factor methodology directly determines capacity-accreditation and revenue for variable-output hydro; changes to the procedure affect both how a plant's UCAP obligation is calculated and its market revenue under NYISO's mandatory participation rules. The document also covers external capacity import rights (including Hydro-Quebec deliveries via Champlain Hudson Power Express and Cedars interface) and DMNC testing protocols for hydro generation. No immediate action deadline is stated in the excerpt, but this is the operative manual governing NYISO's treatment of hydro resources in capacity markets.

title: 8 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Presentation
relevance_reason: 20-year NYISO transmission and resource outlook identifies firm dispatchable capacity requirements, transmission constraints affecting generator deliverability, and resource adequacy challenges in a high-decarbonization future — all determinants of hydro's system value, dispatch patterns, and investment conditions.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook projects electricity demand outpacing resource additions, with firm dispatchable capacity remaining essential across all decarbonization scenarios to provide energy, operating reserves, ramping, regulation, and voltage support during periods of low renewable output. The study identifies Central East and Northern New York transmission interfaces as bottlenecks constraining renewable deliverability and system efficiency; targeted dynamic voltage support upgrades are recommended. Coordinating the timing of resource retirements and new additions is flagged as critical to avoid tightening system conditions. The findings inform NYISO's biennial Comprehensive System Planning Process and identify scenarios for Economic Transmission Project Evaluation and Requested Economic Planning Studies. Board approval is scheduled for July 2026; the full report and 12 technical appendices (including capacity expansion and production cost simulations, renewable generation pocket analysis, and sensitivities) are available in the data catalog.

title: 8a 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Report
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly affects hydropower revenue through capacity-adequacy accreditation, generation-pocket curtailment risk, transmission congestion signals that alter locational value, and strategic location decisions for new capacity — all core inputs to hydro asset valuation and dispatch strategy in the Northeast.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook examines how New York's grid will evolve under multiple policy and demand scenarios through 2044, focusing on generation adequacy, resource-mix composition, and transmission constraints. The analysis finds that renewable generation concentration in upstate pockets creates curtailment risk (especially by 2035–2044) and drives bulk-transmission congestion through the Central East interface, with fossil-generation retirements and large-load growth (data centers, etc.) tightening system conditions and raising downstate capacity payments. For hydropower owners, this outlook establishes the transmission-planning baseline affecting deliverability and locational scarcity value; for pumped-storage operators, it identifies whether seasonal adequacy stress and interregional export opportunities (low-cost clean-energy export potential noted in 2035 scenarios) will raise PSH cycling revenue and RA accreditation premiums. The document is a draft for NYISO Business Issues Committee discussion (June 2026); no formal comment deadline is stated, but future comprehensive reliability plans and transmission procurements will flow from these scenarios.

title: 8a 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Report - Revised
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly shapes hydro's strategic positioning in New York, including load-growth impacts on scarcity and revenue, interregional hydro-Québec interchange, transmission constraints affecting hydro deliverability, and seasonal resource-adequacy outlooks that inform whether existing hydro retains or loses value.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook is a biennial scenario-based planning study examining how demand, resource mix, and transmission evolve under different policy futures. The Outlook models two demand scenarios (95x and 100x) and varying policy stringencies, projecting load growth driven by large loads (data centers and economic development), fossil retirements, and clean-energy targets; it identifies curtailment risk in renewable-generation pockets, bulk transmission congestion (especially on Central East corridor limiting upstate generation export), and strategic value of interregional flows and energy interchange with Hydro-Québec. For hydro asset owners, this study anchors NYISO's capital planning and can influence resource-adequacy constructs, transmission investment priorities, and the marginal value of flexible/dispatchable generation as load-growth tightens supply conditions. The Outlook was presented to the Business Issues Committee on June 10, 2026 (draft status); formal adoption and next-step timeline are not stated in this excerpt.

title: 8b 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Appendices
relevance_reason: System Resource Outlook is a long-range adequacy and planning study that directly informs transmission planning, resource-mix assumptions, and seasonal energy sufficiency — all material to hydro's strategic options, deliverability, and revenue exposure in NYISO.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System Resource Outlook draft appendices support the 10-year reliability and resource-adequacy baseline. This outlook is foundational to transmission planning (affecting hydro export/import paths and congestion signals), seasonal energy-adequacy forecasting, and capacity accreditation methodologies — including how NYISO models and credits hydro, including pumped storage, under varying water/climate scenarios. The appendices likely contain resource mix, renewable buildout assumptions, and transmission-expansion scenarios that shape the scarcity and flexibility value of existing hydro plants. Status and comment windows are not extractable from this excerpt.

title: 8c Motion
relevance_reason: The 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook is a long-term resource-adequacy and transmission study directly affecting hydro fleet planning, seasonal energy adequacy, and strategic generation-mix decisions across NYISO's footprint.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee recommends Board approval of the draft 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook, a 20-year resource-adequacy and transmission study. This outlook informs seasonal energy-adequacy assessments, transmission expansion priorities, and resource-mix planning that shape scarcity conditions, transmission deliverability constraints, and the revenue environment for existing and new hydro and pumped-storage capacity in NYISO. The motion advances to the Management Committee and Board for final approval; the document itself carries no explicit stakeholder comment deadline, but the prior ESPWG and TPAS meetings cited indicate the outlook has been through iterative stakeholder review.

title: 8d Draft Appendix B 2025-2044 Outlook Production Cost Model Assumptions Matrix - Redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's production-cost modeling assumptions directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources are valued in long-term resource and reliability planning; the document specifies how PSH dispatch, conventional hydro modeling, and battery-storage precedent shape the inputs to capacity-expansion models that drive NHA members' strategic options.
ai_summary: NYISO released a draft Production Cost Model Assumptions Matrix for its 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook, establishing modeling parameters for long-term reliability and resource-adequacy studies. The matrix specifies that conventional hydro (excluding Niagara) is modeled as hourly modifiers based on 5-year historic data with 2018 weather shapes; Niagara is modeled as a pondage unit with monthly energy targets; and pumped-storage hydro is modeled with endogenous optimization of pumping and dispatching. Battery storage similarly undergoes endogenous optimization by the production-cost model. These assumptions feed into capacity-expansion models that determine new-generation requirements and economic retirement risk by scenario (Base, Contract, and Policy cases with lockdown dates ranging 9/1/2025–10/31/2025). The document is a draft for NYISO Business Issues Committee discussion on June 10, 2026, and represents a critical methodological snapshot for how NYISO values existing and prospective PSH and conventional hydro in RA showings and long-run planning.

title: 8e Draft Appendix G 2025-2044 Outlook Production Cost Model Results - redline
relevance_reason: Production cost modeling of NYCA generation fleet, LBMPs, and energy storage utilization through 2044 directly informs hydro asset owners' long-term revenue outlook, reserve-margin tightness, and the competitive positioning of energy storage relative to conventional hydropower in the region.
ai_summary: NYISO's draft Appendix G to the 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook presents production cost model results across four scenarios (Base Case, Contract Case, and two Policy Cases) for study years 2030, 2035, 2040, and 2044. The modeling shows load growth concentrated in downstate zones (NYC and Long Island), with LBMPs rising across the study period and becoming sharply elevated in later years (2040–2044) under the Contract Case due to assumed fossil-generator retirements outpacing new renewable additions; Policy Cases with higher capacity additions substantially mitigate LBMP growth and reduce net imports. Energy storage utilization analysis reveals that battery capacity additions exhibit high zone-level correlation with utilization but experience diminishing marginal utilization per unit as total storage capacity rises, suggesting a saturation point in battery efficacy. This outlook is a reference document for NHA members modeling demand-side scarcity, transmission congestion effects on zonal pricing, and the substitution dynamics between storage and dispatchable hydro in a low-carbon NYCA.

title: 8f Draft Appendix I 2025-2044 Outlook Operational Analysis - redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year operational outlook characterizes hydropower's flexibility role and ramp-following capability relative to fossil generation and renewables, directly informing revenue opportunities for hydro's dispatchable and ramping services as the grid evolves under high-renewable scenarios.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 operational analysis (part of its System & Resource Outlook) models the generation fleet's hourly behavior under Base and Contract Cases, analyzing how different resource types respond to load and net-load (load minus intermittent renewables) changes across daily, hourly, and seasonal timescales. Hydro is characterized as serving both a baseline and dispatchable role with 'limited capability to respond to changes in load/net load' compared to fossil generation, which dominates ramp-following. The analysis shows hydro–load correlation is the second-strongest among generation types (after fossil), and hydro ramps track load ramps more reliably than weather-dependent renewables, positioning hydro as a secondary flexibility resource. This operational characterization — which NYISO will use to assess future resource needs and market design — affects how NYISO values hydro's ramp and ramping-product contributions and shapes transmission and flexibility adequacy planning that influences hydro owners' ability to monetize flexible output. Most exposed are hydro owners with ramp-capable resources; the analysis provides baseline data NYISO may use to set demand curves or capacity factors for flexibility products in forthcoming market-design reviews.

title: 8g Draft Appendix J 2025 - 2044 Outlook Renewable Generation Pockets - redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's renewable pocket analysis identifies transmission constraints affecting hydro deliverability in northern and western New York; hydro resources compete with wind and solar for shared transmission paths, directly impacting energy-delivery value and strategic interconnection/transmission planning.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook Appendix J analyzes renewable generation pockets and finds that hydro resources in Pockets W and X (western and northern New York) face rising curtailment and congestion in high-penetration renewable scenarios. Pocket X1 (St. Lawrence hydro corridor) exhibits the second-highest congestion among all pockets; hydro curtails before wind and solar because hydro is ineligible for Tier 1 RECs and therefore carries no negative-bid cost in the production-cost model, putting it at a competitive disadvantage when transmission is constrained. The analysis is draft (dated June 10, 2026 BIC) and used to inform longer-range transmission-upgrade planning; approved Phase 1 and Phase 2 transmission projects in Northern New York (Smart Path Connect) are expected to alleviate some congestion, but Policy Case scenarios with 13–35 GW of additional land-based renewable capacity by 2035 will intensify resource-concentration pressures on hydro energy deliverability.

title: 9 Order No. 1920 Long-Term Regional Transmission Planning
relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 transmission planning and cost allocation framework directly affects hydro deliverability, interconnection, and strategic transmission expansion that determines whether existing and new hydro resources can reach markets; long-term scenarios and resource-mix assumptions will drive transmission investment decisions that enable or constrain hydro export capability.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing FERC Order 1920 (and subsequent clarifications 1920-A and 1920-B) through a redesigned Long-Term Transmission Planning Process that expands the 20-year planning horizon, incorporates New York Department of Public Service scenario input, and establishes an ex ante cost-allocation methodology for selected transmission projects. The process creates a new Catalog of Transmission Expansion Opportunities, solicits private-sector project proposals competing against NYISO baseline solutions using seven FERC-mandated benefit metrics (avoided reliability facilities, LOLE reduction, production cost savings, reduced losses, outage-congestion mitigation, extreme-weather resilience, and capacity benefits), and gates selection through NYPSC declaration of Long-Term Transmission Needs before cost recovery is allocated. The framework directly affects where transmission investment flows: hydro owners in New York face a new, longer-term planning horizon and competing non-wires alternatives that may or may not prioritize inter-area hydro exports versus local reliability; the cost-allocation methodology (details not yet finalized) determines whether transmission enabling hydro delivery is socialized regionally or assigned to specific beneficiaries. The compliance mechanics are active — NYISO Board approval of the planning structure is imminent, and the first Outlook cycle and PSC filings will begin shortly thereafter.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: NYISO's seasonal capacity accreditation (Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements) and must-offer rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources are credited toward RA obligations and when they can be dispatched.
ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Working Group will discuss Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements (seasonal UDR/EDR accreditation), must-offer requirement changes, and ICAP Manual revisions on June 9, 2026. Seasonal accreditation mechanisms determine how much RA value hydro and PSH claim in winter versus summer, a critical lever for use-limited resources whose output is constrained by water availability. The meeting also covers must-offer obligations that force hydro units into certain market states. Potomac Economics will present highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report, which typically includes performance assessments of capacity accreditation models. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the agenda.

title: 2 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements – Seasonal UDR/EDR Elections and Must Offer Requirement: ICAP Manual and Attachment Revisions (Incremental Changes)
relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market design adds seasonal must-offer requirements and UDR/EDR elections that directly affect external resource commitments and penalties for underutilized capacity rights — a novel compliance obligation touching revenue and strategic flexibility for hydro and storage resources holding deliverability rights.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes seasonal (Summer/Winter) elections for Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest-of-State Deliverability Rights (EDRs) starting with the 2027-2028 Capability Year (effective May 1, 2027), filed with FERC February 18, 2026 in Docket ER26-1431 with requested effective date June 28, 2026. Holders must submit separate seasonal return-to-NYCA elections by August 1 prior to each capability year; capacity associated with external UDRs/EDRs not returned must be offered in ICAP spot auctions or certified for LSE compliance, with sanctions for failure — including a revised must-offer penalty calculated monthly. This reframes deliverability-rights holders' compliance posture: seasonal elections create a new obligation to commit or forfeit, converting a flexible holding into a binary choice with penalties. Pumped storage and large reservoir hydro holding external rights face the most direct impact; small hydro with external positions must track August elections and ensure compliance or incur monthly penalties.

title: 2a ICAP Manual Incremental Revisions (with highlighting)
relevance_reason: NYISO ICAP manual revisions directly govern unforced capacity accreditation, duration adjustment factors for energy-limited resources (hydro's core issue), co-located storage rules, and operating data/certification requirements — all determinants of how much capacity revenue hydro and PSH can claim.
ai_summary: NYISO has revised its Installed Capacity Manual (version 17.0, effective 04/02/2026, Committee Acceptance 03/18/2026 BIC), with incremental changes highlighted as of the 06/09/2026 ICAPWG meeting. The manual governs minimum unforced capacity requirements for LSEs, accreditation calculations for ICAP suppliers, duration adjustment factors for energy-limited resources, and co-located storage participation rules. Key sections address DMNC (Demonstrated Minimum Net Capacity) testing procedures, operating data reporting requirements (including for municipally owned generation and intermittent/energy-limited resources), and provisions for co-located storage resources — all mechanisms that directly affect how much capacity hydro and pumped-storage facilities can supply and at what accreditation level. The revisions appear to be in draft/discussion phase as of the June 2026 working group meeting; the document provides the current manual text with yellow highlighting indicating changes from the prior 05/27/2026 version.

title: 2b ICAP Manual Incremental Revisions (single redline)
relevance_reason: NYISO is revising its Installed Capacity Manual to update accreditation rules for co-located storage resources, energy-duration limitations, and DER aggregation — all direct mechanisms for how pumped storage and hybrid hydro resources are valued and accredited in the capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual (Manual 04) is being revised with incremental changes effective April 2, 2026, after BIC acceptance on March 18, 2026. Key sections touch energy-duration limitations and duration-adjustment factors for ICAP suppliers (4.1.1), dual-participation rules (4.1.2), DER and aggregation provisions (4.1.3), and dedicated treatment for co-located storage resources (4.1.4). For pumped storage and hybrid hydro owners, the document signals that NYISO is codifying distinct accreditation pathways for storage paired with generation: co-located resources face specific DMNC/DMGC testing requirements (4.2), operating-data reporting obligations (4.4.10), bidding procedures (4.8.7), and capacity-supply calculations that differ from standalone generators. Storage owners should review sections 4.1.1 (duration factors that constrain RA value) and 4.1.4 (state-of-charge and co-location rules) for direct impact on accreditation and revenue; the manual's treatment of energy-limited resources and intermittent resources also establishes the framework by which run-of-river hydro is accredited.

title: 2c ICAP Manual Appendix Att. Q Incremental Edits (single redline)
relevance_reason: ICAP Manual updates governing NYISO capacity accreditation, external import rights, and deliverability for hydro and storage resources affect revenue recognition and participation mechanics for NHA members.
ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products has issued incremental edits to ICAP Manual Appendix Q, updating maximum allowances for external installed capacity and deliverability rights for 2025–2027. The document revises ICAP allocation limits for neighboring control areas (PJM, ISO-NE, Ontario, Quebec), details Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) for major transmission interfaces including 1,250 MW Champlain Hudson Power Express (Hydro-Quebec to NYC Zone J) and 660 MW Neptune Cable (PJM to Long Island), and specifies External-to-ROS Deliverability Rights (EDRs). Hydro and storage resources in NYCA seeking external-area capacity credits or import rights must navigate these transmission-constrained allocations; the inclusion of intermittent-resource and run-of-river translation factors (Attachment N) and energy-limited resource procedures (Attachment M) indicates the manual also governs accreditation mechanics for variable hydro. This is a draft for discussion; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

title: 2c ICAP Manual Appendix Att. Q Incremental Edits (with highlighting)
relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market rules for external imports and deliverability affect hydro's ability to export power (especially Quebec HQ and Northeast hydro) into New York and its accreditation revenue under ICAP; the manual specifies UDRs and EDRs that govern reservoir hydro export paths and seasonal capacity allocations.
stakeholders: NYISO Capacity Market Products (New York Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products issues an updated ICAP Manual Appendix Q reflecting 2025–2026 and 2026–2027 maximum external installed-capacity allocations by control area and interface, including grandfathered and conditional rights. Key routes for hydro exports: Quebec via Chateauguay (1,110 MW of External CRIS Rights, with seasonal variation November–April); Hydro-Quebec Cedars Interface (80 MW EDR to Rest of State Zone D); Champlain Hudson Power Express from HQ (1,250 MW UDR to Zone J); Cross Sound Cable from New England (330 MW UDR to Zone K). The document is in draft form for discussion by the Installed Capacity Working Group (meeting 2026-06-09) with highlighted revisions from the 2026-05-27 version. Changes affect which hydro suppliers can qualify for capacity revenue and at what seasonal limits into high-value zones (NYC Zone J, Long Island Zone K); owners exporting Quebec or run-of-river Northeast hydro must track these allocations and any ICAP auction implications.

title: 3 Highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report for the NYISO Markets
relevance_reason: NYISO Market Monitoring Unit analysis of energy price formation, ancillary-services co-optimization, shortage pricing (ORDC), reserve-requirement allocation, and demand-response settlement rules that directly affect hydro revenue through energy and reserve pricing, uplift mechanisms, and locational scarcity signals.
stakeholders: Pallas LeeVanSchaick (Potomac Economics (NYISO Market Monitoring Unit))
ai_summary: Potomac Economics (NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit) presents highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report addressing energy and ancillary-services market performance. Key findings: energy prices rose 66–79% in 2025 due to higher gas prices, peak loads, reduced OOM commitment, increased exports to Quebec, and reduced hydro availability; the MMU identifies $221M in misallocated costs annually, including $40M from statewide allocation of local reserve charges and $38M from largest contingencies not paying their impact on reserve requirements; the MMU recommends urgent reforms to shortage pricing (Rec #2017-2: raising NYCA 30-min ORDC to $2000/MWh up to 150% of largest contingency) to prevent economically unjustified exports to ISO-NE and PJM, and to dynamically adjust reserve requirements and model locational constraints in day-ahead commitment. Hydro operators benefit from higher scarcity pricing and better locational reserve signals, but face exposure to large downward corrections if demand-response baseline issues and cryptocurrency-miner over-consumption (paying full LMP despite uneconomic curtailment) are fixed; the report documents $34M annual uplift from paying demand response as if it generates electricity, which competes with traditional ramping and flexibility revenue.

title: 2 2027 Proposed Market Projects
relevance_reason: NYISO is prioritizing 59 market projects for 2027, including multiple energy market, capacity market, and ancillary-services designs directly affecting hydro revenue (pricing, reserves, demand-curve design, accreditation) and a new mandatory project on large-load interconnection; stakeholders including hydro operators will score projects by July 15, 2026.
ai_summary: NYISO has identified 59 proposed Market projects for 2027 prioritization (5 mandatory, 2 continuing, 41 prioritize, 11 future), spanning energy pricing, capacity accreditation, reserves, and resource design. Stakeholder survey opens June 30 with a July 15 deadline; each registered stakeholder organization votes 100 points across projects. A new sixth mandatory project will be added after FERC's expected June 2026 ruling on large-load interconnection. Key hydro-relevant projects include ancillary-services reforms (dynamic reserves, operating-reserve recovery, shortage pricing), capacity accreditation improvements, demand-curve reset methodology, and modeling of ramping and fast-start resources. The stakeholder survey results and project constraints will be reviewed July 29; final project list and 2027 budget recommendation proceed through August–November for Board approval in December 2026.

title: 2 2027 Proposed Market Projects - Revised
relevance_reason: NYISO is proposing 59 market projects spanning energy pricing, capacity accreditation, demand response, reserves design, and large-load interconnection — several directly affecting hydro revenue streams (dynamic reserves cost recovery, ICAP accreditation modeling, locational pricing of capacity, balancing intermittency/duration needs) and one major obligation trigger (mandatory project for FERC large-load interconnection rule expected June 2026).
ai_summary: NYISO is soliciting stakeholder priorities on 59 proposed 2027 market projects, with a survey deadline of July 15, 2026, and Board vote on budget recommendation scheduled for November 16, 2026. The list includes projects directly material to hydropower: 'Dynamic Reserves – Review Operating Reserve Supplier Cost Recovery' (energy market, touching reserve product revenue), 'Improve Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling' (capacity market, affecting hydro's ICAP qualification), 'Enhancing Locational Pricing of Capacity' (locational signals for hydro dispatch value), and 'Balancing Intermittency Phase 2: Evaluating Needs for Duration in Reserves (ENDURE)' (duration/flexibility product design, central to hydro's operational edge). NYISO has also flagged a forthcoming mandatory project responding to FERC's Large Load Interconnection rulemaking (order expected end of June 2026), which will reshape hydro's strategic options for co-located load serving and baseline accreditation. Hydro owners should prioritize survey response by July 15, 2026, and track the mandatory project scope once FERC rules, as large-load interconnection rules will affect hydro's participation pathways and capacity value in the coming year.

title: 2027 NYISO Project Prioritization Survey
relevance_reason: NYISO is soliciting prioritization of six 2027 projects including one directly affecting hydro/PSH transmission capability (5-minute interchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec) and others touching DER aggregation, demand response baselines, and capacity market rules that create revenue and compliance precedent for storage and flexible resources.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2027 project-prioritization survey solicits stakeholder input on six initiatives, due July 15, 2026. Project 1 (5-Minute Transaction Scheduling) expands a 2020 study to develop market rules and mechanisms for scheduling interchange with Hydro-Québec at five-minute frequency, aligning with internal real-time dispatch and improving price convergence and ramping flexibility — directly increasing hydro's strategic options in cross-border trade and within-hour balancing. Project 3 (Aggregation System Integration) streamlines DER and aggregation enrollment processes via API integration, supporting Order 2222 participation pathways for small hydro. Project 4 (Demand Response Baseline Alignment) investigates whether demand-response baselines can be net suppliers of ICAP capacity, following MMU recommendations — the rules examined also govern how DSRs and storage interact with capacity markets. Project 5 (Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers) proposes automatic resale of unforced-capacity surplus to reduce market-clearing prices. These collectively reshape how hydro and storage interact with capacity, flexibility, and cross-border revenue mechanisms; survey responses and comments will be made public and posted with Budget & Priorities Working Group materials.

title: 2a 2027 Market Project Descriptions
relevance_reason: NYISO's 2027 market project portfolio includes multiple projects directly affecting hydro revenue streams (ancillary services pricing, dynamic reserves, energy pricing, capacity accreditation), strategic flexibility products, storage-as-transmission, and interregional exchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec.
ai_summary: NYISO has identified 57 candidate market projects for 2027, organized as Prioritize (38), Mandatory (4), Continuing (2), and Future (13) classifications. Key projects affecting hydro and storage include: 5-Minute Transaction Scheduling with HQ (expanding real-time interchange frequency to improve price convergence and flexibility); Balancing Intermittency Phase 2 (evaluating duration in reserves, directly relevant to PSH scheduling); Dynamic Reserves Phase 1 (reviewing cost recovery for operating-reserve suppliers); Storage-as-Transmission (broadening PSH use cases); Capacity Zone Redesign and Enhancing Locational Pricing of Capacity (affecting hydro siting value); Seasonal ICAP Market Improvements and ICAP Demand Curve Reset (capacity accreditation); FERC Order 2222 Compliance (DER/hybrid participation); Voltage Support Service for Inverter-Based Resources (expanding ancillary-service procurement); and Energy Market Mitigation screening enhancements. The document reflects stakeholder requests from JERA Americas, National Grid, and NYS UIU. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated in this metadata excerpt; this is a project-scoping document for internal budget prioritization.

title: 3 2027 Proposed Enterprise Projects
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Projects 4 (Activate VSA Application), 14 (Demand Forecasting Machine Learning), 40 (REFIRE), and 48 (Uncertainty Response Tool) touch hydro-specific market operations (variable-resource forecasting, advanced dispatch tools, energy-market modeling) that affect real-time co-optimization, flexibility-product procurement, and settlement accuracy — core revenue and compliance domains for hydro operators in NYISO.
ai_summary: NYISO's Budget & Priorities Working Group is evaluating 49 proposed Enterprise projects for 2027, including four that directly affect hydropower market participation and dispatch: (1) Activate VSA Application (Energy Markets, deployment phase); (2) Demand Forecasting Machine Learning (Operations & Reliability, study complete); (3) Renewable Energy Forecast Integration and Reporting Enhancements / REFIRE (Operations & Reliability, software design phase); and (4) Uncertainty Response Tool / URT (Operations & Reliability, $0.45M, software design phase). These projects improve forecasting accuracy for variable renewable and hydro resources, real-time dispatch modeling, and uncertainty quantification — directly affecting how hydro's flexibility and ramping capability are valued in energy and ramping-product markets, and how settlement of forecast-error charges is computed. The committee will conduct cost revisions and NYISO scoring on June 24, 2026, and stakeholder presentations and advocacy are scheduled through August; final project prioritization and NYISO budget recommendation are due to the Markets Committee on October 5, 2026, with Board approval on November 16, 2026. Hydro operators should monitor how these tools are designed to avoid systematic undervaluation of hydro flexibility and overcharging of hydro operators for forecast error.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market and manual updates touching accreditation, intermittency treatment, and modeling rules that directly affect hydro and storage revenue and dispatch obligations.
ai_summary: NYISO Installed Capacity Working Group meeting (June 2, 2026) will address four rule changes: DER market-participation enhancements, ICAP Manual Attachment N updates, revised treatment of intermittency in capacity accreditation, and improved duct-firing modeling for fossil units. The intermittency revisions are most material — they modify how NYISO accredits variable and run-of-river hydro in the capacity market, and how storage is modeled in the reserve-margin calculation. Manual updates will be presented by Sushant Varghese and Vijay Kaki; Potomac Economics will discuss 2025 State of the Market findings, which often include capacity-accreditation trends and hydro-performance analysis. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda.

title: 2 DER Market Enhancements
relevance_reason: DER aggregation participation rules directly affect small hydropower owners' ability to participate in NYISO markets under Order 2222 frameworks; the DADSR concept and proposed telemetry/accreditation changes alter the strategic options and compliance burden for hydro resources enrolled in DER aggregations.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes a Day-Ahead Demand-Side Resource (DADSR) variant within its existing DER & Aggregation participation model, designed to accommodate DSR with longer start-up times and limited real-time flexibility. DADSR would bid day-ahead, lock to a fixed real-time schedule, and be ineligible for operating reserves or regulation service; it would qualify for ICAP participation with 2–24-hour energy duration limitations and potentially reduced telemetry requirements (6-second with 30-second scan rates for sub-1 MW facilities versus current standards for 1+ MW). Small hydropower and run-of-river resources enrolled in DER aggregations face a strategic choice: participation under current DER rules (with full telemetry/ancillary-service obligations) or under DADSR (simpler telemetry, day-ahead certainty, but loss of regulation/reserve revenue and real-time flexibility upside). NYISO is also evaluating MNBT and ECBL enhancements and the 10 kW threshold; stakeholder discussion continues at the July ICAPWG/MIWG meeting.

title: 3 ICAP Manual Attachment N Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO is revising Attachment N methodology for calculating translation factors that convert nameplate capacity to unforced capacity (UCAP) for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources, directly affecting hydro accreditation in resource adequacy and capacity-requirement studies.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes revisions to ICAP Manual Attachment N to implement seasonal (summer and winter) translation factors for intermittent resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro, replacing the current summer-only methodology. The change responds to observed winter Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) beginning in the 2026–2027 IRM study and supports the Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC 2/18/2026, target implementation 5/1/2027 for the 2027–2028 Capability Year). The proposal also corrects the availability-factor formula to apply proper weighted averaging, aligning calculation with current implementation in IRM, LCR, and deliverability studies. For run-of-river hydro owners, this means winter availability will now drive a separate winter UCAP translation factor based on December–February production patterns weighted by winter LOLE distribution, potentially improving winter accreditation if winter production is high relative to winter scarcity hours, or reducing it if winter is a dry season; the revision will be presented to the Business Issue Committee on 6/10/2026.

title: 3a ICAP Appendix N redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Attachment N directly governs how NYISO calculates capacity accreditation for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources—a core revenue determinant—and the redline signals revision to those translation-factor procedures.
ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products is revising Appendix N of the Installed Capacity Manual (draft for discussion, issued June 2, 2026). Attachment N contains the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro, which convert nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (the quantity offered into NYISO's ICAP auction). Changes to these translation factors directly alter the megawatt-accreditation—and thus auction revenue—that run-of-river and variable hydro operators can claim.
