The document is in draft-for-discussion phase with the Installed Capacity Working Group; the redline indicates active revision, though no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

title: 3b ICAP Appendix N clean
relevance_reason: Attachment N establishes the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources in NYISO's capacity market accreditation, directly affecting how hydro capacity is credited and valued in NYISO's ICAP auctions.
ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual Attachment N specifies the methodology for calculating translation factors that convert nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (UCAP) for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro. This accreditation procedure determines how much capacity credit variable hydro can claim in NYISO's ICAP auctions and capacity showings. The document also references Attachment M (Energy Limited Resource and Energy Duration Limitation procedures) and Attachment D (Dependable Maximum Net Capability test procedures including a hydro-generation form), indicating NYISO uses performance-data-driven methods to credential hydro's contribution to resource adequacy. For run-of-river and variable-output hydro operators in NYISO, the translation-factor calculation directly sets the capacity value realized in auctions and affects their RA contribution and revenue.

title: 4 Balancing Intermittency Manual Updates
relevance_reason: NYISO's Uncertainty Reserve Requirement (URR) design directly affects how intermittent resources and ramping/flexibility are procured and priced in both day-ahead and real-time markets, creating new reserve products that hydro will be called upon to supply and creating new demand curves that establish payment terms for flexibility hydro traditionally provides.
ai_summary: NYISO is finalizing manual revisions to implement its Uncertainty Reserve Requirement (URR), a new 24-hour reserve product designed to hedge forecast uncertainty for net load, land-based wind, offshore wind, and front-of-meter solar. The design was accepted by FERC in July 2025 (ER25-1998) and is scheduled for deployment in August 2026. The updated manuals clarify URR calculation methodology (incorporated in new Attachment D to the Ancillary Services Manual), specify that URR can be met using 30-Minute Reserve in the day-ahead market, introduce locational demand curves with a new Long Island requirement row (270 MW and 540 MW separately), and refine operating-capacity accounting for seasonal differences. Stakeholder votes are scheduled for June 10–11, 2026 (BIC and OC); comments can be submitted at the June 4 SOAS meeting. For hydro owners, URR creates both a revenue opportunity (new procurement of a flexibility product hydro naturally supplies) and a compliance obligation (must-offer rules in the 30-minute reserve product that backs URR); pumped-storage facilities are particularly positioned to capture value from locational reserve requirements, especially on Long Island where the demand curve now explicitly separates 270 MW and 540 MW tiers.

title: 4a T&D Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual revision affects hydro compliance obligations: reserve calculation, regulation requirements, outage scheduling, dispatch procedures, and real-time commitment processes that directly govern how hydropower units are monitored, operated, and settled in the market.
ai_summary: NYISO is issuing a revised Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) effective May 1, 2026, following committee acceptance on April 15–16, 2026. The manual covers operational monitoring (real-time data, security assessment, reserve and regulation requirements), transmission operations (operating limits, voltage control, phase angle regulators), real-time commitment and mitigation processes, and scheduling procedures. Hydro operators must comply with the manual's reserve calculation methodology, regulation-requirement definition, outage scheduling processes, and real-time dispatch instruction protocols—changes to any of these affect when hydro units are called on, how their output is metered and settled, and the operational flexibility they must maintain. The document's status as draft-for-discussion suggests revisions are still in circulation; the June 2, 2026 Installed Capacity Working Group meeting will likely discuss final language.

title: 4b Day Ahead Scheduling Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: Day-ahead scheduling manual revisions affect hydro's energy commitment rules, storage constraint evaluation processes, and ancillary service demand curve participation—core elements of revenue and operational flexibility in the day-ahead market.
ai_summary: NYISO has issued revisions to its Day-Ahead Scheduling Manual (Manual 11, effective 10/29/2024, accepted by BIC 08/14/2024 and OC 08/15/2024). The manual governs SCUC initialization, unit commitment of dispatchable resources including hydropower, energy storage resource constraint evaluation, ancillary service demand curves, and bilateral transaction evaluations that directly shape day-ahead market dispatch and pricing. For hydro owners, key mechanics documented include scheduling of 'must run' resources, multiple response rates for resources, energy storage limits constraints, and limiting participation constraints—all of which define the operational envelope within which hydro's dispatch and AS offerings are optimized. Hydro and PSH operators rely on these rules to understand how their bids enter unit commitment and how their flexibility is valued in the scheduling process.

title: 4c Ancillary Services Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO's Ancillary Services Manual revisions directly govern how hydropower and pumped storage are compensated and must perform for regulation, frequency response, voltage support, and operating reserves — the core revenue streams and operational obligations for dispatchable hydro and PSH.
stakeholders: NYISO Operations Engineering (New York Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: NYISO issued a draft Ancillary Services Manual (v10.0, effective May 1, 2026) with revisions to regulation and frequency response service, voltage support, operating reserves, and energy imbalance rules. The manual specifies qualification criteria, performance-tracking methods, settlement mechanics, and testing procedures for generators, synchronous condensers, HVDC with voltage-source converter (VSC) technology, and energy storage resources providing these services. Hydro and PSH operators must comply with reactive capability testing protocols (section 3.6), regulation service prequalification and performance audits (section 4.11), and operating-reserve supplier eligibility and day-ahead/real-time bidding rules (sections 6.2–6.4). This is a working draft for discussion at the Installed Capacity Working Group (committee acceptance 04/15/2026, OC acceptance 04/16/2026); the effective date is May 1, 2026, making this a compliance-critical update for all NYISO hydro and storage participants.

title: 6 2025 State of the Market Report
relevance_reason: NYISO's 2025 State of the Market Report addresses capacity-market design, seasonal accreditation, resource-adequacy signals, and locational pricing — all mechanisms that directly shape revenue for hydro and pumped-storage resources in NYISO and influence investment and retention decisions.
stakeholders: Joe Coscia (NYISO Market Monitoring Unit / Potomac Economics)
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit (Potomac Economics) identifies structural gaps in the capacity market that undervalue reliability contributions and distort investment incentives. Key findings: (1) demand curves based on 2-hour battery cost understate Net CONE, risking premature retirements even as load grows; (2) seasonal incentives are misaligned—firm fuel/UDR elections locked 16 months in advance don't respond to winter market prices, and elected capacity for 2026/27 (15.2 GW) exceeds planning assumptions (12.3 GW), creating cost-allocation risk; (3) transmission security is undercompensated—downstate LCRs increasingly bind at floor, overpaying bottled generation (SCRs, large units) and underpaying non-firm gas units while creating $74M annual inefficiency post-CHPE. Recommendations filed or pending include raising the demand-curve floor (Rec. 2025-1a), moving seasonal-election deadlines closer to winter (Rec. 2022-2), introducing locational CAFs (Rec. 2025-2), and expanding capacity zones. These touch pumped-storage accreditation (2-hour battery CONE undermines longer-duration PSH value), seasonal energy adequacy (firm fuel elections timing), and locational value recognition (hydro deliverability and transmission-security contribution). No formal comment deadline is stated in this highlights presentation, but the full 2025 SOM Report (posted May 19, 2026) and tariff filing ER26-1431 are the operative documents.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: ICAP (capacity market) surplus-offer rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources bid and earn capacity revenue; this agenda item flags an active NYISO discussion and vote on capacity-market mechanics.
ai_summary: NYISO's Management Committee will vote on changes to automatic ICAP surplus-offer rules on May 27, 2026. The mechanism affects how capacity is offered into NYISO's capacity market when resources have surplus capacity beyond their committed obligations — a setting that shapes net-revenue outcomes for hydro and PSH that participate in ICAP. The specific proposal details are not disclosed in the agenda, but this is a live vote item; no comment deadline is stated in the document.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: The document addresses ICAP (Installed Capacity) market rules and winter reliability enhancements that directly affect hydropower's capacity accreditation and resource adequacy participation in NYISO.
ai_summary: This agenda covers ICAP Manual revisions related to winter reliability capacity enhancements, which are directly relevant to how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are accredited for capacity value and contribute to resource adequacy in NYISO. Changes to ICAP rules materially affect hydro participation and compensation.

title: 2a Operations Report
relevance_reason: Hydro asset owners use monthly operational metrics to understand system conditions affecting their revenue (scarcity pricing opportunity during stress events, curtailment risk, interchange patterns) and obligations (reserve activations, load-following demand). This April 2026 report includes hydro outage data, net-load-ramp trends affecting flexibility value, wind/solar forecast performance and curtailment (competitive pressure on hydro's flexibility premium), and transmission changes (CHPE MTF, Smart Path Connect) affecting hydro deliverability and inter-regional pricing.
ai_summary: NYISO's April 2026 operations report tracks system reliability and market outcomes. Hydro-relevant findings: (1) Monthly generation mix shows 6,324 MW hydro nameplate (including pumped storage); (2) Hydro and other resources showed measurable day-ahead capacity unavailability in real time (charted but values not extracted due to daily volatility); (3) Net load ramps driven by wind and solar variability remained significant (15–35 MW hourly average), sustaining demand for flexible ramping; (4) Wind and FTM solar curtailment totaled 1.5% system-wide in April (wind 8,453 MWh, FTM solar 2,486 MWh), indicating transmission constraints that compress energy value for all resources in congested zones; (5) Champlain Hudson Power Express (CHPE MTF) merchant transmission completed integration testing in late April and began commissioning, with Smart Path Connect incrementally entering service throughout the month—these transmission additions will improve hydro export paths from Quebec and reduce NY congestion costs. No member action required; document is reference material for understanding April revenue drivers and seasonal resource-adequacy conditions.

title: 3 Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO is revising the ICAP market's surplus-offer rules to automatically resell capacity that load-serving entities and ICAP suppliers procure but do not need, at zero-dollar bids. For hydropower and storage owners selling capacity into NYISO's ICAP market—whether as primary capacity suppliers or via bilateral trades—this rule removes their option to withhold surplus capacity and directly affects the monetization path and price realization for unsold UCAP.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes a new tariff section (5.14.4) that automatically submits zero-dollar offers for any Unforced Capacity (UCAP) surplus held by load-serving entities, ICAP suppliers, and ICAP marketers after the monthly certification deadline, effective May 1, 2027 (Board approval and FERC filing required). Currently, surplus UCAP often goes unoffered, potentially raising market-clearing prices; the automatic zero-dollar offer guarantees resale at the auction clearing price. Hydropower and pumped-storage owners—particularly those acquiring capacity via bilateral trades or selling excess UCAP—will face mandatory resale of any certified surplus, eliminating the ability to withhold capacity or negotiate off-market prices for unexpectedly available MW; however, capacity associated with a physical transmission interface (PTID) and 'unoffered MW' remain subject to physical-withholding rules and are excluded from automatic resale.

title: 3a 2026 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements: ICAP Manual Revisions - Presentation
relevance_reason: This document addresses ICAP capacity market rules, seasonal capacity requirements, and deliverability rights (UDRs/EDRs) that directly affect how hydropower and energy storage resources participate in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO is proposing seasonal ICAP minimum requirements, seasonal elections for Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest of State Deliverability Rights (EDRs), and must-offer requirements effective May 1, 2027. These changes establish distinct Summer and Winter capability periods and impose penalties for failure to offer or certify capacity, which affect how dispatchable resources like hydropower and pumped-storage can deliver capacity across seasonal variations and transmission constraints.

title: 3a MST 5.14 redline
relevance_reason: ICAP capacity-market accreditation and demand-curve methodology directly affect hydro and PSH revenue from capacity markets, particularly the periodic review process that sets accreditation reference points and the demand-curve adjustments that drive cleared capacity prices.
ai_summary: NYISO's tariff Section 5.14 establishes the ICAP Spot Market Auction mechanics and demand-curve parameters that clear monthly unforced-capacity obligations. The document redlines the periodic review methodology (every four years, next review for 2025/2026 capability year), which assesses the levelized cost of a peaking plant, net energy and ancillary-services revenue offsets, and seasonal differences in capacity availability. Beginning with 2025/2026, demand curves will account for seasonal variations in capacity availability—summer and winter curves now reflect different excess-capacity assumptions. The 2025/2026 NYCA summer curve peaks at $21.69/kW-month; winter at $16.39/kW-month, with locational adders (NYC summer peak $41.30). Hydro and PSH capacity accreditation is measured against these ICAP demand curves translated to unforced-capacity terms; seasonal demand-curve splits will alter how seasonal hydro availability and storage round-trip efficiency factor into capacity showings, potentially reducing winter accreditation if available supply diverges seasonally from the reference peaking-plant assumption.

title: 3b Attachment Q - Draft
relevance_reason: Document establishes capacity accreditation and penalty calculation methodologies for external unforced capacity deliverability rights, which directly affects how hydropower and other resources are valued and incentivized in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: This attachment details UCAP calculations for External UDRs/EDRs using capacity accreditation factors, forced outage rates, and derating factors—mechanisms that apply to hydropower resources delivering capacity over external transmission interfaces. The must-offer requirement and associated penalties (1.5× spot market price) create compliance obligations for hydropower operators holding external deliverability rights.

title: 3b Motion

ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee approves revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing automatic ICAP surplus offers, with the Board of Directors to authorize filing under FPA Section 205. The proposal changes the rules by which capacity surplus is offered into the market automatically, affecting how hydro and pumped-storage units manage their capacity accreditation obligations and surplus positioning—particularly relevant for PSH operators managing state-of-charge and capacity ratings. The full tariff language and detailed mechanism are presented in the accompanying slide deck 'Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers' (May 27, 2026); the proposal moves to Board filing stage pending Board authorization.

title: 3c ICAP Manual Revisions - Draft Redline

title: 4 2025 State of the Market Report

stakeholders: David B. Patton (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit)

Joseph Coscia (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit)
Pallas LeeVanSchaick (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit)
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit releases its 2025 annual State of the Market Report, covering wholesale and retail costs, energy and ancillary services market performance, capacity market results and locational pricing assessment, transmission congestion management, demand response, and competitive dynamics. The report includes analysis of fossil and non-fossil generator performance during peak conditions, assessment of transmission security limits' impact on capacity payments, evaluation of excessive qualification of conventional generators, and critique of the large load interconnection process. For hydropower and PSH owners, this report provides the baseline market-monitor assessment of (1) whether ancillary services pricing is adequate to support reservoir and storage operations, (2) locational capacity-market signals and whether transmission constraints are suppressing payments in hydro-rich zones, (3) performance metrics that may inform cost-of-service or mitigation rules, and (4) findings on demand-response and load-growth trends that shift the value of flexibility and scarcity-price exposure. The document is posted to NYISO's Management Committee agenda; no specific vote or comment deadline is stated in the excerpt.

title: 4a 2025 State of the Market Highlights
relevance_reason: The document directly addresses hydropower availability impacts on NYISO energy prices, capacity market performance affecting generation investment incentives, and ancillary services market design—all critical to hydro operations and revenue.
stakeholders: Pallas LeeVanSchaick, Ph.D. (Potomac Economics (NYISO Market Monitoring Unit))
ai_summary: The 2025 State of the Market Report identifies reduced hydropower generation availability as a driver of 2025 energy price increases (66–79%), and highlights planning-market gaps and low net revenues for flexible generation types that affect hydro investment incentives. The report recommends capacity market reforms and improved energy/ancillary services price formation to enhance incentives for flexible and available units, which are essential for hydro profitability and retention.

title: Final Motion

ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee unanimously approved revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers on May 27, 2026, with the Board of Directors now authorized to file the tariff revisions under FPA Section 205. The mechanism affects how ICAP (Installed Capacity) surplus—the cushion above actual resource adequacy requirement—is offered and priced in the capacity market. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators rely on ICAP accreditation for capacity revenue; changes to surplus-offer rules can shift the timing, volume, or price-formation signals that determine when and how much capacity-constrained resources are called upon, altering the effective capacity value and bidding strategy for hydro and PSH assets.

Hydropower and PSH operators must understand these rules to participate in capacity auctions, self-supply mechanisms, and bilateral transactions, and to ensure proper accreditation of their unforced capacity for resource adequacy compliance.

ai_summary: NYISO is reviewing Energy and Ancillary Services credit requirements, which can spike during extreme price events (e.g., January 2026) and remain elevated for six-month periods even when actual exposure declines. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators subject to these requirements should monitor proposed changes to the basis month methodology and average run-rate calculations, as refinements could reduce unnecessary collateral burdens.

title: 00 Agenda - Revised

Order 1920 updates and interconnection agreement enhancements are also discussed, both relevant to hydro facility interconnection and transmission deliverability.

title: 02a 2025-2044 System and Resource Outlook - Presentation
relevance_reason: The document analyzes transmission congestion, resource adequacy, and generation mix across multiple policy scenarios that directly affect hydropower deliverability and market participation in NYCA.
ai_summary: The 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook modeling shows significant transmission congestion (particularly Central East constraints) emerging under high renewable penetration scenarios, which affects hydropower's ability to deliver power and its locational value. The analysis includes net import/export trends and zonal LBMPs that are critical for understanding hydropower's competitive position and the need for flexible ramping resources in New York's evolving resource portfolio.

title: 02b 2025-2044 System and Resource Outlook - Preliminary Draft Report
relevance_reason: This 20-year system planning outlook directly addresses resource adequacy, capacity mix evolution, transmission constraints, and how policy-driven renewable and storage deployment will reshape New York's grid—all critical factors for hydropower and pumped-storage market participation and competitive positioning.
ai_summary: The report models multiple policy scenarios (2025–2044) that will determine hydropower's role in meeting clean energy targets, capacity needs, and transmission-constrained zones. It assesses fossil phase-out timelines, large load growth, offshore wind and nuclear deployment impacts, and regional transmission bottlenecks—all factors that shape market value and operational opportunities for existing and new hydropower/PSH resources in New York.

title: 03 2026 RNA: Scenario Assumptions
relevance_reason: This document describes scenario assumptions for NYISO's 2026 Reliability Needs Assessment (RNA), including generation availability, resource retirement modeling, and transmission security assumptions that directly affect how hydropower and storage resources are modeled in resource adequacy planning.
ai_summary: The RNA scenario development process will model aging generation retirements, planned generator project delays, wind/solar resource availability, and reactive support capabilities. These assumptions are foundational to how hydropower's role in reliability, capacity accreditation, and ancillary service provision are evaluated in NYISO's resource adequacy analysis. Hydropower stakeholders should engage during the scenario feedback process to ensure hydro-specific assumptions (seasonal availability, operational flexibility, reactive capability) are appropriately represented.

title: 04a Order 1920 Update - Presentation
relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 on long-term regional transmission planning directly affects how hydropower projects are evaluated, prioritized, and integrated into NYISO's transmission expansion plans.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing FERC Order 1920 compliance tariff revisions governing long-term regional transmission planning (OATT Sections 31.1, 31.2, 31.7, and others). These rules establish processes for identifying transmission needs, evaluating solutions including storage and flexible resources, and cost allocation—all of which determine whether hydropower and pumped-storage projects receive planning support and transmission access. Hydro stakeholders should engage by the May 22 feedback deadline.

title: 04c - Order 1920 Compliance - Comments from NRDC and Earthjustice
relevance_reason: The document addresses FERC Order 1920 compliance for transmission planning, which directly affects how alternative technologies (including energy storage and PSH) are evaluated and integrated into regional transmission solutions that hydropower facilities depend on.
ai_summary: NRDC and Earthjustice raise questions about NYISO's affirmative obligation to evaluate alternative transmission technologies (ATTs) and right-sizing opportunities under Order 1920. These requirements affect how pumped-storage hydropower and other flexible resources can be considered as alternatives to traditional transmission expansion, and how transmission planning cycles interact with resource adequacy and reliability processes that hydropower resources participate in.

title: Draft Minutes

ai_summary: PJM's Deactivation Enhancements Senior Task Force (DESTF) held its May 19, 2026 meeting to advance voting on scope areas 4/5 and 6, which address long-term transmission solutions and alternatives to Part V arrangements for deactivating generators. Michael Herman, Clara Summers, and Claire Lang-Ree presented solution options for accelerating transmission completion and modifying Part V terms. The group is in a consensus-based issue-resolution process with next DESTF meeting scheduled June 11, 2026. Hydro and PSH owners using Part V arrangements (particularly those with limited water/dispatch seasons or facing transmission constraints) may see revised cost-allocation or exit-timing rules that affect retirement decisions and the cost of remaining online; the June 11 meeting will advance scope-area voting.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: PJM's Gross CONE and Avoidable Cost Rate (ACR) updates directly determine capacity-accreditation values and thus revenue for all resources, including hydropower and pumped-storage plants, in PJM's capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee will review 2026 updates to Gross CONE (Cost of New Entry) and Gross ACR (Avoidable Cost Rate) values on June 16, 2026, with Brattle Group presenting methodology changes from the prior study. Gross CONE and ACR underpin PJM's capacity auction demand curve and set the reference prices against which all resource bids are evaluated; changes to these values directly affect the price floor/ceiling in capacity auctions and can shift accreditation outcomes for hydro and pumped-storage resources. The agenda confirms a working session (9:05 a.m.–12:00 p.m. EPT); no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda document, but the 2026 study review suggests parameters will be finalized for implementation in the next capacity auction cycle.

committee: Special MIC - Gross CONE and Gross ACR Updates
meeting_title: Special MIC - Gross CONE and Gross ACR Updates Meeting
title: Agenda Doc
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM Gross CONE and Gross ACR updates directly affect capacity-market accreditation and auction parameters for all capacity resources, including hydro and pumped-storage hydro participating in PJM's RPM (capacity market).
ai_summary: PJM Special MIC is reviewing updates to Gross CONE (Cost of New Entry) and Gross ACR (Accredited Capacity Rating) — the two foundational parameters that drive RPM capacity-auction demand curves and reserve-margin calculations. Gross CONE is the levelized cost benchmark for a new reference resource; Gross ACR is the proxy capacity value of existing resources. Changes to either reset the economic entry/exit threshold for new capacity and redraw existing-resource accreditation levels — directly affecting reserve-margin adequacy determinations and capacity revenue for all dispatchable and storage units. The meeting agenda indicates updates are under discussion, but the extracted text does not include substantive proposals, comment deadlines, or next steps; the full meeting materials (slides, proposals) would be needed to determine specific impacts on hydro and PSH accreditation or revenue exposure.

committee: Special MIC - Gross CONE and Gross ACR Updates
meeting_title: Special MIC - Gross CONE and Gross ACR Updates Meeting
title: Item 01 1 – Gross CONE And Gross Acr Updates – Preliminary Update Of Default CONE Values
relevance_reason: CONE and ACR are the foundational inputs to PJM's Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC), which directly set offer-floor rules for all capacity resources including hydropower and pumped storage; this periodic review updates default values for the 2030/2031 delivery year that affect how hydro operators must bid and what the floor to market participation is.
ai_summary: PJM is updating its default Gross Cost of New Entry (CONE) and Gross Avoidable Cost Rate (ACR) values, effective for the 2030/2031 capacity auction, informed by consultant analysis of capital and fixed O&M costs from EIA and prior Quadrennial Review data. These default values set the floor for existing hydro and storage offers under the Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) and for new-entrant MOPR bids; PJM will file final updated values with FERC by October 1, 2026, following an Advisory Vote at the MRC/MC in September 2026. The update applies the same methodology as prior cycles but includes preliminary CONE values for all resource types (e.g., Battery Energy Storage $502–$799/MW-day for 2026/27 and 2030/31); existing hydro assets will be affected to the extent their marginal avoidable costs are benchmarked against these updated ACR defaults when calculating unit-specific or default-option MSOC bids.

committee: Special MIC - Gross CONE and Gross ACR Updates
meeting_title: Special MIC - Gross CONE and Gross ACR Updates Meeting
title: Item 01 2 – Gross CONE And Gross Acr Updates – Avoidable Cost Study Methodology
relevance_reason: PJM is updating Gross Avoidable Cost Rates (ACRs) used in the Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC), which directly affects how hydro resources' market-power mitigation default offers are calculated and whether unit-specific cost reviews are triggered — a core revenue-protection mechanism for dispatchable resources.
ai_summary: PJM and Brattle/Sargent & Lundy are updating the Gross ACR study (last done in 2022) to take effect in delivery years beginning 2030/31. The 2022 study covered ~94% of the PJM fleet and established resource-type median costs (e.g., coal at $94/MW-day, natural gas CC at $113/MW-day, onshore wind at $147/MW-day) that feed into default Net ACRs, which are then applied as the MSOC threshold for supplier-side market power mitigation in the capacity market. The 2026 update will review whether current fleet characteristics have shifted the median cost profiles, solicit stakeholder feedback on whether to add a 4-hour BESS as a reference type, and present draft ranges and fleet analysis at the July 8, 2026 MIC meeting. Hydro resources are not explicitly listed as a reference type in either study; the mechanism is indirect — if the MSOC thresholds shift downward relative to hydro's actual avoidable costs, hydro owners will face more frequent unit-specific cost reviews to justify offers above the default, raising compliance burden and regulatory uncertainty around scarcity-related revenues.

title: Draft Minutes
relevance_reason: DESTF is managing PJM's deactivation and retirement rules; scope areas 4/5 explicitly address alternatives to Part V arrangements (RMR/financial constructs) for deactivating generators, which directly affects hydro owner exit and fleet-retirement decisions.
ai_summary: PJM's Deactivation Enhancements Senior Task Force (DESTF) met May 19, 2026, to vote on scope area 6 and review proposed solutions for accelerating long-term transmission solutions and alternatives to Part V arrangements for deactivating generators (scope areas 4/5). Part V is PJM's reliability-must-run mechanism; alternatives under development would reshape the financial and operational rules hydro owners face when retiring or deactivating capacity. The next DESTF meeting is scheduled June 11, 2026 at 1:00–4:00 p.m. EPT (WebEx); materials are due June 5, 2026 by 5:00 p.m. EPT. No voting results, proposed language, or specific alternative structures are disclosed in these minutes.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Order 1920 transmission cost allocation affects the revenue and interconnection economics of generation resources, including hydro and pumped storage, by determining how new transmission investments and upgrades are funded and distributed across market participants, which impacts both the deliverability of hydro output and cost recovery incentives.
initiatives: FERC Order 1920 [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM is convening a TEAC Special Session on Order 1920 compliance to address transmission cost allocation. PARSEC and PJM Transmission Owner will each present proposed cost allocation methodologies (June 4, 2026), with PJM subsequently discussing revisions to the LTRTP Protocol to accommodate competing proposals ahead of a June 12 filing. This proceeding determines how the costs of new transmission and upgrades—critical to hydro deliverability and resource adequacy adequacy across PJM's footprint—are allocated among generators and load-serving entities. The outcome affects the economics of both existing hydro units seeking uprates and potential new dispatchable resources. The next TEAC meeting is scheduled for July 16, 2026, with materials due July 9, 2026.

title: Draft Minutes – 5726
relevance_reason: Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA) Phase II proposal directly affects PSH revenue and operational deployment by establishing participation rules for storage assets used to provide transmission services—a strategic option for PSH owners.
stakeholders: Todd Ament (Cube Hydro Partners, LLC; Eagle Creek Hydro Holdings, LLC; Great Falls Hydroelectric Company Limited Partnership; Mahoning Creek Hydroelectric Company, LLC; York Haven Power Company, LLC)
ai_summary: PJM and Constellation jointly proposed a SATA Phase II solution, with Monitoring Analytics also presenting a competing solution proposal, both reviewed at the May 7, 2026 Operating Committee meeting. The Operating Committee will vote to endorse one or both proposals at the next meeting (June 4, 2026). SATA defines how pumped-storage hydro and other storage can be compensated and operated when functioning as a transmission asset rather than a conventional generator or ancillary-services provider—this directly affects PSH revenue streams and the strategic choice between traditional dispatch and transmission-service roles. Pumped-storage owners in PJM should monitor the June endorsement vote and any tariff filings that follow, as the Phase II framework will govern long-term cost recovery and operating obligations for storage deployments.

title: Item 02 – Options – Packages Matrix – Storage As A Transmission Asset – Sata – Phase 2
relevance_reason: Storage-as-transmission proposal directly affects pumped-storage hydro's strategic participation options and transmission-delivery value, positioning PSH as a non-wires alternative and storage asset class in PJM markets.
initiatives: Storage as a Transmission Asset - Phase II [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM Operating Committee is evaluating options and packages for recognizing and compensating storage as a transmission asset (SATA), Phase 2. This proposal would create a new participation pathway and potentially a new revenue stream for pumped-storage hydro by allowing storage facilities—including PSH—to be credited and compensated for deferring or replacing transmission infrastructure upgrades. If adopted, PSH owners gain a mechanism to monetize transmission-support services beyond energy and ancillary services, and improved deliverability value for constrained plants. The matrix format suggests PJM is presenting multiple design options to the Operating Committee; no vote date or next-step timeline is stated in the extracted material.

title: Item 02 – Storage As A Transmission Asset – Sata – Phase 2 – Package Summary
relevance_reason: SATA establishes a new participation model for energy storage as a regulated transmission asset in PJM, fundamentally altering how storage resources (including pumped-storage hydro) can be deployed and valued — either enrolled in SATA (transmission-only, cost-of-service recovery, no market participation) or remain in competitive markets, creating a strategic fork for PSH owners.
initiatives: Storage as a Transmission Asset - Phase II [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Operating Committee reviewed the Phase 2 framework for Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA), which allows energy storage (including pumped-storage hydro) to operate as regulated transmission facilities to address localized RTEP violations without participating in competitive markets. Under SATA Phase 2, eligible storage assets would discharge automatically to mitigate identified contingencies, charge via owner coordination afterward, and recover costs through transmission rates (Schedule 12 Transmission Enhancement Credits) rather than market revenue, with metering and telemetry matching standard energy-storage requirements. The framework explicitly prohibits SATA resources from queuing for interconnection or claiming capacity value, and expects assets to be small (≤10 MW) and short-duration (≤4 hours). The document advances to MRC review in July 2026, with FERC submission expected September–November 2026. This creates a new revenue pathway for pumped-storage owners willing to accept regulated-utility-like cost recovery in exchange for operational control and transmission-rate certainty, but forecloses market-participation optionality (energy, AS, capacity) for enrolled assets.

title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 13 governs PJM emergency operations procedures that directly affect hydro and other generating resources' obligations during system emergencies, capacity shortages, and transmission security events — particularly dispatch instructions, must-offer requirements, and emergency bid processes that touch hydro revenue and compliance.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 (Emergency Operations), Revision 98, underwent annual review with substantive changes to emergency procedure taxonomy and triggering thresholds effective July 23, 2026. Key changes separate transmission-security emergencies from capacity emergencies, clarify that transmission-security procedures do not trigger Energy Emergency Alerts (EEAs) or Persistent Advisories (PAIs), and add a new Capacity Advisory to provide advance notice when forecasted capacity approaches operating requirements. A new Attachment O codifies procedures for emergency use of backup generators under DOE 202(c) Orders. The revision also updates Advanced Scheduled Resources language for cold/hot-weather alerts and adds RCIS notification to Pre-Emergency Load Management procedures. For hydro operators, the distinction between transmission- and capacity-triggered emergencies affects when they will receive dispatch instructions and emergency bid solicitations — transmission emergencies no longer trigger EEAs, which may narrow the scope of emergency-reserve procurement that traditionally favors hydro's rapid-response capability; the Capacity Advisory may provide earlier signaling of system stress, allowing better strategic positioning. Most affected are large, flexible generators and storage operators participating in PJM's emergency-bid and reserve markets. Revision endorsed by Operating Committee June 4, 2026, with final approval timeline shown through June 24, 2026 (MRC second read).

title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Establishes procedures for emergency dispatch of large loads (data centers) on backup generation during capacity/transmission emergencies, creating new operational constraints and dispatch prioritization rules that affect hydro's role in reserve provision and emergency response.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) introduces a new Emergency Use of Back-up Generators procedure allowing data centers and large loads to disconnect from the grid and operate on-site backup generation during capacity or transmission emergencies—after NERC EEA2 is declared and a DOE 202(c) Order is obtained. PJM will direct Transmission Owners to coordinate disconnection of large loads within 15 minutes, with capacity-deficient control zones exhausting all available large-load capacity before requesting disconnection in zones with excess capacity. This reshapes hydro's strategic value during emergencies: where previously all generation resources (including hydro's full output) would be called to meet contingency reserves, large-load disconnection now becomes an alternative resource, potentially reducing dispatch calls on conventional generators and changing the economic incentive structure for hydro emergency participation. Most exposed are reservoir and pumped-storage operators supplying contingency reserves in constrained zones; run-of-river hydro with fixed output faces less direct impact. The manual becomes effective immediately; no comment deadline is stated.

title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 13 covers emergency operations including capacity emergencies, weather/environmental procedures, and transmission security — obligations that hydro operators must comply with during system stress, plus strategic implications for hydro revenue during scarcity events (shortage pricing, load shedding, emergency procedures that affect dispatch and pricing).
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updates the operator emergency procedures manual with changes to capacity emergency classifications, weather alerts, transmission security procedures, and minimum generation requirements. Key changes include addition of a Capacity Advisory level to the emergency-level structure (Section 2.3), updated language for Advanced Schedule Resources during cold and hot weather alerts, clarification that transmission security procedures do not trigger PAIs or EEAs, and a new Attachment O on emergency use of backup generators. For hydro operators, these procedures determine obligations during system stress (e.g., must-offer and minimum generation requirements), affect the timing and magnitude of scarcity-pricing events and emergency bidding, and establish the dispatch scenarios in which hydro's flexibility is most valued or constrained. All hydro-owning utilities in PJM footprint must comply with these operating standards.

title: Item 04 – 1 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's revision to Manual 14d establishes binding reactive power control requirements for all generators, including hydroelectric units, affecting their compliance obligations and operational constraints on voltage support capability provision.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d Section 7.1.2 to clarify and strengthen reactive power (voltage support) control requirements for all generators in its footprint, driven by NERC's May 2026 lowering of IBR registration thresholds to 20 MVA and PJM's identification of increasing low-voltage risk (citing the 2025 Spain blackout). The revision mandates automatic voltage control mode as the default for all generators; defines volt-var control boundaries and reactive output ranges tied to MW output; specifies two new required reactive capability curve data points ('Maximum AVR capable MW Output' and 'Unit Minimum MW Output with Reactive Control'); and establishes a three-party investigative process (PJM, transmission owner, generator owner/operator) for reactive control performance issues. For hydroelectric owners, this tightens the operating envelope for reactive power supply: hydro units must now document and receive explicit approval for any AVR mode other than voltage control, and must define their reactive capability limits with new precision, constraining dispatch flexibility and potentially requiring retrofits or AVR reconfiguration on legacy units. The revision proceeds through SOS (June 15), MRC (July 1 and July 9), and OC endorsement (July 23), with education and Q&A sessions between June 4 and July 9. The compliance timeline is not yet stated in this first-read presentation.

title: Item 04 – 2 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: reactive power and voltage support obligations — PJM is clarifying VAR control requirements and AVR mode directives in Manual 14d, which directly affects how synchronous hydro and PSH units must operate their excitation systems and reactive-capability dispatch; this tightens an existing compliance obligation and may constrain flexibility in real-time volt-var/watt-var behavior.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d (Generator Operational Requirements), sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D, effective July 23, 2026, to add NERC Category 2 GO references, clarify allowable AVR (automatic voltage regulator) modes, specify acceptable volt-var and watt-var control ranges, and formalize PJM's monitoring and engagement authority over reactive-control behavior. The revision responds to NERC's May 2026 Category 2 GO initiative (expanding inverter-based resource reliability standards) and PJM's operational challenges from rising solar/IBR penetration and load growth outstripping generation, both elevating voltage risk. For synchronous hydro and PSH operators, this tightens the specification of reactive-capability dispatch and AVR operating ranges — reducing ambiguity about allowable volt-var curves but also narrowing operational flexibility in voltage control. Most affected are large reservoir hydro and PSH facilities with synchronous generators supplying reactive power in constrained voltage zones; smaller or run-of-river plants with limited reactive margin may face tighter conformance audits. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14D governs PJM generator operational and data-exchange requirements, metering standards, and dispatch procedures that directly affect hydro and storage operators' compliance obligations, participation pathways, and black-start procurement eligibility.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D (Revision 71, effective June 30, 2026) is a comprehensive operational and technical standard for all generating resources in PJM, including hydro, storage, and hybrid facilities. The redline reflects updates to control-center requirements, EMS data exchange and synchrophasor protocols, metering standards (with specific exhibits for energy-storage and hybrid resources), cold-weather resource preparation checklists for conventional and inverter-based units, black-start procurement processes, and behind-the-meter and microgrid business rules. Hydro and pumped-storage operators must comply with metering precision, reactive-capability testing and reporting (Attachments D–E), dispatch-instruction protocols, outage-scheduling, and cold-weather operational readiness (Attachment N) — and may be selected for black-start service under the revised incremental RFP and reliability backstop processes (Section 10). Mixed-technology facilities (solar–storage hybrids) now have dedicated marketing and metering rules (Section 13, Exhibit 7–8), setting a template that extends to other hybrid and co-located configurations. Revision 71 is already effective as of the meeting date, with no further comment period or vote indicated in the excerpt.

title: Item 05 – Operating Metrics
relevance_reason: Document reports on PJM operational metrics including a specific shortage-case approval where hydro switching to pumping mode triggered a shortage event, directly touching hydro's dispatch flexibility and pumped-storage operational constraints in real-time balancing.
ai_summary: PJM's June 4, 2026 Operating Committee report documents system operations and performance metrics for May 2026. One finding is material to PSH owners: on May 15, 2026, PJM approved a shortage case (09:50 interval) explicitly citing 'hydro switching to pumping' as a factor contributing to the shortage event. This incident highlights how pumped-storage transition from discharge to pumping mode can tighten system energy balance and trigger emergency procedures — a risk PSH operators face when participating in energy markets and providing flexible ramping. The report also notes spinning-reserve response performance, forecast-error tracking, and BAAL (Balancing Authority ACE Limit) compliance, all relevant to the real-time operating environment in which hydro and PSH dispatch decisions occur.

title: Item 07 – Primary Frequency Response Update
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Primary Frequency Response (PFR) is an ancillary service hydro plants are required to supply under NERC BAL-003-2, and this PJM performance update directly measures hydro unit compliance with PFR standards and identifies performance gaps that may trigger must-offer rules or operational penalties.
ai_summary: PJM reports on compliance with NERC BAL-003-2 frequency response obligations for 2025 operating year (FRO = −235 MW/0.1 Hz) and reviews Manual 12 Primary Frequency Response performance across six low-frequency events between March and May 2026. In the 04/13/26 event, 25 hydro units were evaluated; the stacked results across all fuel types show hydro at 71% responding with expected PFR, 13% with no response when expected, and 16% flagged for further evaluation. This puts hydro at or below the fleet-wide average (81% PFR compliance), suggesting potential compliance risk or dispatch/operating constraints limiting hydro's frequency-response capability in PJM. No specific proposal or deadline is stated; this is a monitoring report to the Operating Committee.

title: Item 08 – Regulation Performance Update
relevance_reason: Regulation performance and pricing are ancillary-services revenue streams core to PJM hydro and storage operators; this operational update tracks performance scoring, mileage-based pricing (RMMCP), and regulation requirement changes that directly affect hydro's compliance and compensation in PJM's regulation market.
ai_summary: PJM's Performance Compliance team reports on regulation-market operations as of May 2026, including a nomenclature change (Regulation Market Mileage Clearing Price, effective October 1, 2025) and an update to regulation requirements for summer 2026 (effective May 1). Hydro resources averaged 89% performance score in May 2026 with 967 MW qualified for regulation, compared to storage at 94% score and 230 MW qualified. This metric directly affects hydro's regulation-market revenue; operators should track the new mileage-based pricing framework and summer requirement levels to assess whether pricing or dispatch patterns have shifted under the post-October 2025 rules.

title: Item 09 – Synchronous Reserve Update
relevance_reason: Synchronous reserve performance and penalty administration directly affect ancillary-service revenue and compliance obligations for dispatchable resources, including hydropower units that carry synchronized-reserve assignments in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM's 2026 Q1 synchronized-reserve performance report shows 8 events (1 ≥10 minutes) with 48% average generation response to assignment and 66% demand-side response. March 1 event triggered $330K in penalties (first of the quarter), indicating tightened enforcement of reserve-response obligations. The report documents response-performance metrics by resource type (generation vs. demand-side resources) and region (RTO vs. MAD zone), establishing baseline compliance data that operators—particularly synchronous units like conventional hydro—must track for performance assessment and penalty exposure.

title: Item 10 – Reliability Compliance Update
relevance_reason: NERC large-load interconnection guidance and inverter-based resource ride-through standards directly affect how synchronous hydropower (and competing generation) must operate during grid disturbances, and large computational loads affect hydro's strategic positioning in a scarcity-prone grid.
ai_summary: NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert (due August 3, 2026) requiring registered entities to address risks from customer-initiated large load reductions and oscillations that threaten BPS reliability, alongside a Reliability Guideline recommending risk mitigation for emerging large loads. NERC also released implementation guidance for PRC-029-1 (inverter-based resource ride-through design), which sets standards hydro must comply with if required to operate in grid-support modes. The large-load alert is strategically relevant because rapid load swings create scarcity windows that reward flexible, synchronous resources like hydro; however, the ride-through standard applies primarily to inverter-based resources and does not directly change hydro's existing obligations. Hydro operators in PJM should monitor August 3 deadline compliance requirements and the June 5 MSPP Rules of Procedure webinar for any emerging standards that might extend ride-through or frequency-support mandates to synchronous units.

title: Item 11 – Mtopoc Update
relevance_reason: MTOPOC coordinates major transmission outages that constrain interregional flows and affect generator dispatch flexibility and deliverability — directly impacting hydro owner outage scheduling options and revenue dependability during periods when transmission is constrained.
ai_summary: PJM's Multi-TO Project Outage Coordination (MTOPOC) process manages ~$21 billion in transmission upgrades and associated major outages (230 kV and above, 20+ days) across 2026–2027 to minimize system reliability risk and construction conflicts. Generator outage coordination is becoming critical as load growth and generation retirements outpace new capacity additions; PJM is encouraging generators to submit outages early with detail and to maintain scheduling flexibility, as transmission constraints and emergent system conditions are triggering increased outage adjustments and recalls. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators in PJM face tightening outage windows and must coordinate outage timing with major transmission work affecting their deliverability corridors (Peach Bottom nuclear, northern Dominion, North-South flows); generators should engage with Emanuel Bernabeu and Joe Rushing early in the outage-planning cycle.

title: Item 12 – Regulation Market Phase Ii Simulation Results
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: regulation market bifurcation (splitting single bidirectional product into separate RegUp and RegDown products) changes the offer, clearing, and settlement mechanics that hydro and PSH use to compete for and monetize regulation revenue in PJM, a major market where hydro operates.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Phase 2 of its Regulation Market redesign, effective October 1, 2026 (pending FERC approval), splitting the single bidirectional regulation product into two separate RegUp and RegDown products, each with its own requirement, clearing price, and settlement. The simulation, using FESTIV software across representative weeks in all four seasons, tested area control error performance, controller saturation, and generator movement under the bifurcated design versus the current unified product. Key findings show separate regulation products improve ACE (Area Control Error) performance and reliability metrics, though summer conditions remain challenging; improvements stem from changing resource mix and generator movement behavior. The implication for hydro and PSH operators is that the new product structure will change how their regulation capacity awards are determined and priced — previously a single award covered both up and down service; under Phase 2, RegUp and RegDown awards and prices are independently cleared, potentially altering the revenue profile for units providing regulation. The document does not contain comment deadlines or further implementation milestones beyond the October 1, 2026 effective date.

title: Item 13 – Generation Operational Testing Update
relevance_reason: PJM's Generation Operational Testing program establishes must-offer compliance obligations and performance penalties that directly affect dispatchable resource operators, including hydroelectric generators committed to RPM or FRR plans, through failure charges and retest requirements tied to start-time and minimum-run-time parameters.
ai_summary: PJM's Performance Compliance team reported on the Generation Operational Testing program, which subjects all capacity resources (except variable resources) committed in RPM or FRR to unannounced real-time operational tests up to twice per season. Winter 2025 saw 54 total tests with a 40% failure rate (vs. 24% in summer 2024), driven mainly by delayed start times, mechanical issues, and telemetry errors; retests failed at 64% versus 36% for initial tests. PJM emphasized that generators face financial implications from failed retests and must proactively validate operational readiness and parameter accuracy (start-up time, minimum run time, notification delay) in Markets Gateway. Hydroelectric and pumped-storage operators committed as capacity resources in PJM fall squarely under this obligation; dispatchable hydro must maintain accurate start-time parameters and be capable of reliable real-time response or face test failure and associated penalties.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: The agenda addresses conforming changes to PJM Manual 11 and 18 covering energy and ancillary services market operations, capacity market design, and dual-fuel scheduling—mechanisms that directly affect how dispatchable and storage resources, including hydro, are scheduled, priced, and compensated in PJM.
initiatives: Dual Fuel Schedule Requirements [evaluation in progress]
Fuel Cost Policy Clarifications for Affiliated Fuel Suppliers [evaluation in progress]
Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee will review and vote on conforming changes to Manual 11 (energy and ancillary services operations) regarding Advanced Scheduled Resources Phase 1, FERC Order ER26-846 on economic load response regulation, and dual-fuel schedule requirements; Manual 18 (capacity market) periodic review and conforming changes will receive a First Read for endorsement at the next meeting. The agenda also addresses a periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values (capacity-market cost parameters), Phase 2 of resource scheduling prior to the day-ahead market, and fuel-cost policy clarifications for affiliated fuel suppliers. These revisions touch how hydro and other dispatchable resources are scheduled, must-offer, and how their ancillary service regulation and capacity-accreditation parameters are set. Phase 1 and dual-fuel updates are on track for endorsement June 3, 2026; Phase 2 scheduling and fuel-cost policy remain open working items without stated completion dates.

title: Informational Only – PJM Esr Model Enhancements Position Paper
relevance_reason: PJM's proposed enhancements to energy storage resource market participation rules—particularly opportunity-cost offer methodology, state-of-charge management, and must-offer requirements—directly affect how pumped-storage hydro and battery storage compete for energy and ancillary-services revenue and how their dispatch is constrained by SOC accounting, setting precedent that will shape storage treatment across RTOs.
ai_summary: PJM staff proposes enhancements to its Energy Storage Resource Participation Model to accommodate rapid battery-storage growth (4,800 MW expected by 2030). The proposal addresses four issue areas: (1) a standardized opportunity-cost calculation for real-time energy offers capping cost at max(incremental energy cost, maximum DA LMP) up to $2,000, with emergency override to $2,000; (2) SOC accounting in day-ahead dispatch (rolling calculation to prevent infeasible dispatch) and ancillary-services clearing (recognizing SOC depletion from AS deployment), but explicit rejection of centralized SOC optimization; (3) no change to inter-day offer rules; and (4) complementary work via the Reserve Certainty Senior Task Force. A stakeholder process begins July 2026. The opportunity-cost cap directly affects how pumped storage (and batteries) offer into real-time markets—tying the cap to max DA LMP rather than real-time conditions or a multiplier makes cost-based offers more predictable but may suppress PSH discharge bids during high-priced hours when SO-C is low. SOC accounting prevents infeasible dispatch but stops short of RTO-led optimization, preserving operator discretion. The proposal affects both standalone storage and MTF configurations (including hydro-battery hybrids). Comments and next steps are embedded in a July 2026 stakeholder launch.

title: Item 01 1 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 11 revision 137 includes updates to hydropower operation scheduling rules (Section 9.3) and pumped-storage modeling (Attachment B) under FERC compliance, plus regulation-market redesign affecting how hydro supplies ancillary services; this touches both hydro obligations (scheduling/dispatch) and revenue mechanisms (regulation pricing and settlement).
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 revision 137 (effective July 1, 2026) incorporates three market-design updates relevant to hydro: (1) Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 updates (FERC ER26-0527) affecting resource scheduling prior to day-ahead, reflected in sections 2.3.1, 2.3.3, and 9.1; (2) Regulation Redesign Phase 1 (FERC ER24-1772) introducing revised regulation market clearing, pricing, and obligation rules in new Section 3 and Section 4.4; and (3) updates to hydropower operation procedures in Section 9.3 and pumped-storage modeling in Attachment B. Hydro operators face new scheduling windows and must-offer obligations under the Advance Scheduled Resources framework, while regulation revenue and mileage settlement mechanisms are restructured. Pumped-storage owners are directly affected by modeling changes and participation rules in the revised regulation market. The manual is now effective; no comment period is noted in this redline document, indicating implementation is underway.

title: Item 01 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's advance resource-scheduling rule for units with >24-hour start-up times locks cost-based offers and prevents price-based rebidding, affecting how dispatchable hydro and other resources can position themselves in energy markets and real-time rebidding windows.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Manual 11 revisions (Phase 1) to formalize advance scheduling of resources with notification or start-up times exceeding 24 hours, triggered by lessons from Winter Storm Elliott. Resources pre-scheduled in advance of the Day-ahead Market on binding cost-based offers will have their offer prices locked and the 'Switch to Cost Schedule' flag set by PJM; they cannot update to price-based schedules for the locked days. Resources with start-up/notification <24 hours may update cost-based offers only through the Day-ahead bid period and during real-time rebidding windows. The rule prevents advance-scheduled units from exercising price-based flexibility once committed. Implementation is scheduled for June 2026, with MIC first read May 26 and second read/endorsement in June.

title: Item 01 3 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Manual 11 revisions clarify dispatch and cost-offer rules for resources called prior to the day-ahead market, affecting how conventional hydro and other dispatchable units are scheduled and compensated when operated outside normal market gates.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM approved conforming changes to Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) effective June 24, 2026, under FERC order ER26-527-000, to implement Phase 1 of its Resource Scheduling Prior to Day-Ahead Market initiative. The revisions clarify cost-capping rules for resources with start-up or notification times exceeding 24 hours when called in advance of the day-ahead market, specify how such resources are placed on cheapest-available cost-based offers, and address application of the "switch to cost" flag in day-ahead and real-time operations. For conventional hydro and dispatchable resources operated outside standard day-ahead/real-time windows, the changes affect how dispatch costs are calculated and offers are applied—particularly relevant to run-of-river or reservoir hydro units with long notification times that may be pre-scheduled for reliability or system needs, with voting completed June 3, 2026.

title: Item 02 1 – Manual 18 Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 18 Revision 63 governs the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM) capacity market, including accreditation rules, performance assessment penalties, and participation frameworks that directly affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage resources qualify for and are compensated in PJM's capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM has released Revision 63 of Manual 18 (effective September 1, 2026), a periodic review update to the RPM capacity market rulebook. The revision spans resource accreditation, performance testing, non-performance assessment, seasonal capacity resources, and DER participation—all mechanisms that establish how hydro and PSH assets earn capacity revenue and face compliance penalties. Key sections address Summer/Winter Capability Testing (Section 8.5), Non-Performance Assessment (Section 8.4A), and Seasonal Capacity Performance Resources (Section 4.10), which directly determine whether hydro meets PJM's qualifying-MW standards and penalty exposure. The document shows this is a formal Manual revision in effect as of the meeting date; no comment period or vote deadline is stated in the excerpt, but this redline indicates the revision has been approved and is now operative.

title: Item 02 2 – Manual 18 Periodic Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review includes conforming changes to PJM's capacity market accreditation rules (AUCAP factor penalties, VRR curve parameters, sell-offer requirements) that directly affect how hydro and storage resources are valued and must participate in PJM's RPM auction.
ai_summary: PJM's Manual 18 periodic review incorporates conforming changes to the Capacity Market Manual approved via several tariff filings, including a new penalty for derate caused by decrease in final Accredited AUCAP factor (ER25-2002-000), updates to Variable Resource Requirement curve plotting and parameters (ER26-455-000), and clarifications to must-offer exception processes and resource-specific sell-offer requirements. The document reflects housekeeping and compliance work, with a FERC order expected on June 5th on GDECS changes (ER26-980) that may trigger additional conforming edits; the updated manual is expected for MIC endorsement in July 2026. Most exposed are hydro and storage operators subject to AUCAP accreditation assessment and must-offer obligations; the new penalty structure for AUCAP derate could alter effective capacity value for peaking or seasonal hydro resources whose output factors change year-to-year.

title: Item 04 – Periodic Review Of Default Gross CONE And Gross Acr Values – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values directly affects the Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) calculations that apply to all resources, including hydropower and pumped storage; these values set the floor for offers and determine whether units face mitigation, which is the canonical market-power rule applied to use-limited resources like hydro.
ai_summary: PJM is conducting its mandatory periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values for the 30/31 delivery year, with external consultants Brattle Group and Sargent & Lundy analyzing updates. These values drive MOPR calculations for new resources and ACR-based MSOC calculations for existing units; they directly determine the cost floors against which hydro offers are tested for mitigation. PJM will present detailed methodology and revisions to the Market Implementation Committee on June 16, 2026, seek first read at the MRC, conduct an Advisory Vote at MRC/MC in August, and file changes at FERC no later than October 1, 2026. All resource types—including any hydro or pumped-storage plants in the capacity market—are subject to these mitigation rules, making the updated values material to revenue defensibility and offer strategy for PJM members.

title: Item 06 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Work Plan
relevance_reason: PJM is developing a new market mechanism (resource scheduling prior to the day-ahead market) that would materially affect the timing and rules governing how dispatchable resources including hydropower submit offers and commitments, altering both revenue timing and operational flexibility.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee is developing Phase 2 of a 'Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market' framework, with design work spanning mid-2025 through mid-2026. The work plan shows PJM will identify design components, document their relative importance, develop and narrow options, and construct and compare alternative packages for stakeholder approval around June 2026. The mechanism would reshape when and how hydro (and all dispatchable resources) lock in commitments relative to the day-ahead market, potentially affecting both the value of real-time flexibility and the cost of unit-commitment, particularly for run-of-river and reservoir resources that depend on hour-ahead optionality; the outcome will be critical to PJM-area hydro operators' ability to optimize dispatch timing against price signals. No vote date or public-comment window is yet specified in this work plan.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION planning and interregional coordination affect hydro deliverability and may influence strategic options for export-dependent hydro resources; generator deactivation notifications and reliability analysis also touch fleet-adequacy decisions.
ai_summary: PJM's Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee (TEAC) meets June 2, 2026, to review interregional planning updates, generator deactivation notifications, market-efficiency findings, and supplemental transmission projects from major utilities (UGI, FirstEnergy, AEP, PPL, Exelon, Dominion). The agenda includes reliability analysis and 2026 RTEP capacity-expansion scenarios for the 8-year forward case (2034). While no hydro-specific proposals are flagged, the interregional-planning update and reliability analysis may surface transmission or resource-adequacy constraints affecting hydro deliverability and seasonal energy supply, and generator-deactivation notifications provide early warning of fleet retirements that alter competitive scarcity and hydro value. Next meeting: July 7, 2026.

title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumption Update
relevance_reason: RTEP transmission planning assumptions directly shape interconnection queues, generator deliverability criteria (including hydro and pumped-storage EEFORds), and transmission-constrained revenue for existing and new hydro/PSH resources.
ai_summary: PJM posted its 2026 RTEP modeling assumptions for the Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee (June 2, 2026). Key hydro/PSH impacts: (1) Hydro generic EEFORd set at 8.15% and pumped storage at 5.57% — these forced-outage rates determine how much capacity PJM credits each technology in planning studies and interconnection queues. (2) Battery dispatch rules in base cases now specified (summer minimum charge at ENC × (1−EEFORd)/4, winter /8, light load charging mode), setting a precedent for how storage participates in transmission planning and potentially foreshadowing market participation models. (3) Offshore wind removals from 2031 and 2034 base cases and continued modeling of deactivating units create relief in transmission-constrained zones, directly affecting the scarcity-value of existing hydro and PSH deliverability. Timeline: competitive proposal window opens July 2026; Board approval expected October 2026–February 2027. Most exposed are PSH owners with interconnection in PJM's constrained MAAC/DOM zones, where EEFORd assumptions drive whether their resource clears in capacity/interconnection analysis.

title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumptions Feedback
relevance_reason: PJM's capacity expansion modeling methodology for RTEP transmission planning directly affects how hydro and storage resources are represented in future transmission needs assessments, which determines transmission investment priorities and interregional transfer capability available to hydro exporters.
ai_summary: PJM is incorporating capacity expansion modeling into its 2026 RTEP cycle to project future generation buildout and inform backbone transmission investments through 2046. CEBA and FirstEnergy submitted feedback on April 20–22, 2026, regarding technical assumptions: CEBA supports the expansion modeling but urges PJM to model broader storage durations (6-, 8-, and 10-hour batteries and longer-duration systems), age-based retirement curves, and sensitivity analysis on natural gas prices and load growth. FirstEnergy requests clarification on whether the model will incorporate PJM's proposed "Connect and Manage" load flexibility framework, which would materially affect where new capacity is sited and thus transmission needs. The methodological choices—especially the treatment of storage technologies, retirement assumptions, and interconnection queue completeness—will shape which transmission corridors PJM prioritizes and whether interregional paths serving hydro exports are reinforced. PJM is scheduled to discuss 8-year modeling results at the May 8, 2026 TEAC meeting.

title: Informational Only – PJM Statement On Public Policies Within The 2026 RTEP
relevance_reason: PJM's 2026 RTEP capacity expansion modeling directly affects transmission investment and resource adequacy assumptions that touch hydro revenue (through load-growth scarcity signals and generation mix shifts), strategic options (resource adequacy constructs and storage targets), and planning obligations for all PJM-region generators including any hydro resources.
ai_summary: PJM issued its statement on public policies incorporated into the 2026 RTEP 8-year case (2034 horizon), detailing which state and federal policies shaped two main scenarios (A.1 and B.1) plus five sensitivities. Scenario B.1 models state RPS targets and battery storage targets, including policy-driven generator deactivations; Scenario A.1 does economic resource-stack optimization without specific RPS modeling. The capacity expansion model assumes 218.3 GW summer and 204.5 GW winter peak load in 2034, forcing addition of solar, wind, gas, battery storage, and nuclear uprates. Storage targets (modeled in Sensitivity A.3 and Scenario B.1) and clean energy policies via generator retirements (Illinois CEJA, New Jersey CO₂ Rule, Virginia Clean Economy Act) reshape the assumed generation fleet PJM uses for transmission needs assessment, altering the scarcity and reserve conditions hydro will operate in and the competing resources for RA accreditation. This is the planning foundation for 2026–2034 transmission and resource-adequacy decisions across PJM.

title: Item 01 – Interregional Planning Update
relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning directly affects hydro and PSH deliverability, revenue realization from interregional trade, and strategic positioning in seams between PJM and neighboring RTOs (MISO, NYISO/New England).
ai_summary: PJM's Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee reviewed an interregional planning update covering EIPC initiatives and scheduled IPSAC (Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee) meetings with MISO (June 8 and August 17, 2026) and with New York & New England (TBD). Interregional transmission expansion and seams governance directly shape the economic value of hydro capacity at PJM boundaries — larger transfer capability into PJM increases price realization for exporting hydro (e.g., from New York) and improves market access; conversely, congestion on interregional paths limits arbitrage and reserve-sharing opportunities. Hydro owners with generation in MISO, NYISO, or adjacent regions should monitor these IPSAC meetings for proposals affecting interregional flow capability, cost allocation, and resource adequacy coordination.

title: Item 02 – Generation Deactivation Notification Update
relevance_reason: STRATEGIC OPTION: Generation deactivations and the RMR/DACC mechanism directly affect hydro fleet retirement decisions, resource adequacy, and transmission-driven constraints that can extend profitable plant life or trigger forced operations.
ai_summary: PJM's Q1 2026 deactivation status report tracks seven energy-storage and thermal units requesting exit by April 1, 2027, with reliability analyses completed and no impacts identified for any unit. The report illustrates PJM's deactivation-review process under its tariff: when a proposed exit could adversely affect transmission-system reliability due to upgrade-completion timelines, PJM may request the owner to extend operations under either a Cost of Service Recovery Rate (CSRR) filing at FERC or acceptance of a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC). While this cohort contains storage units (Beech Ridge, Grand Ridge, Mantua Creek, Green Mountain, Cape May County) rather than conventional hydro, the DACC and CSRR mechanisms are available to all dispatchable resources—including pumped-storage facilities and some run-of-river hydro—facing similar retirement pressure and transmission constraints. Hydro owners should monitor how PJM applies these mechanisms when transmission upgrades lag and scarcity conditions develop; forced-operation agreements can materially extend revenue life but require tariff-compliant cost recovery.
