title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM is adopting IEEE 2800 inverter-based resource interconnection standards that will establish ride-through, frequency response, and voltage support requirements applicable to synchronous hydropower and all new generation — creating new operating obligations and potentially raising compliance costs for hydro facilities during disturbances.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14H to adopt IEEE 2800-2022 requirements for inverter-based resources (Phase 1), including voltage and frequency ride-through, primary frequency response, and enter-service criteria, with application to VSC-HVDC transmission facilities and IBR interconnections. The revision adds Section 9.15 and new Attachment K, specifying mandatory ride-through times (e.g., 6 seconds at 0.70–0.90 p.u. voltage) and default frequency-response parameters (droop 5%, dead-band 0.06% × fnom). While Phase 1 targets IBRs, the standards establish precedent for synchronous resource requirements; PJM endorsement is scheduled for June 2, 2026, with MRC endorsement August 19, 2026. Hydro operators should monitor whether these ride-through and frequency-response mandates extend to conventional generators in Phase 2, as compliance during voltage/frequency disturbances will become mandatory, and deviation penalties may apply.

title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: IEEE 2800 adoption establishes operating standards (ride-through, frequency response) that synchronous hydro units must meet, directly affecting compliance obligations and potentially the technical feasibility and cost of hydro participation in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14H to adopt IEEE Standard 2800-2022 requirements for inverter-based resources, with Phase I covering general definitions, voltage and frequency ride-through, ride-through protection, and primary frequency response. The manual changes are effective July 23, 2026, pending Planning Committee approval (MRC first read June 24, 2026; MRC voting July 23, 2026). While IEEE 2800 is designed for inverter-based resources, PJM's alignment with industry standards may establish precedent for how synchronous resources—including conventional hydropower and pumped-storage hydro—are expected to meet similar technical standards in future compliance phases, affecting both capital requirements and operational dispatch.

title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual revision addresses interconnection queue procedures, surplus interconnection service, and generator requirements (including capacity vs. energy resource elections) — core obligations and strategic options for hydro interconnection and deliverability.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) Revision 05 becomes effective July 23, 2026. This manual governs the interconnection queue process for generation, transmission, and merchant transmission requests in PJM. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, the key provisions include: Section 9 sets requirements for generating facilities (power factor, capacity vs. energy resource status elections, behind-the-meter generation, inverter-based resource rules); Section 12 covers surplus interconnection service, the mechanism by which existing hydro plants can access previously-allocated but unused interconnection capacity for uprates or expansion; and Sections 1–5 establish the phased study and agreement process that determines project viability and cost allocation. The manual revision incorporates IEEE 2800 standards (synchronous inverter modeling), affecting how hydro and hybrid resources are studied. Hydro developers and PSH operators in PJM should review Section 9's resource-status elections and Section 12's surplus service process, which determine how much capacity value their projects can claim and how efficiently they move through the queue.

title: Item 03 – Market Efficiency Update
relevance_reason: Market Efficiency analysis drives transmission expansion planning that affects hydro deliverability and resource adequacy assumptions; reserve-requirement forecasting and generation-expansion modeling directly inform the capacity and energy price environment hydro faces.
ai_summary: PJM posts its preliminary 2026/27 Market Efficiency base case in June 2026, with stakeholder feedback due August 31, 2026, and final case in January 2027; analysis will identify congestion drivers and proposed transmission solutions through September 2027, culminating in TEAC board approval by December 2027. The study models PJM's peak demand growing from 160.5 GW (2027) to 241.4 GW (2041) and uses preliminary ELCC class ratings to forecast generation adequacy; reserve requirements and topology are informed by the 2026 RTEP reliability window. Hydro operators should note that this cycle's load forecast, generation-expansion assumptions, and transmission topology directly shape which existing hydro assets are called to deliver energy and when locational scarcity pricing—and thus ancillary-service and energy margins—will emerge; the August 31, 2026 feedback window is the actionable juncture to flag hydro-specific accreditation or deliverability concerns in PJM's modeling.

title: Item 04 – 1 M20a M22 Periodic Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 20A addresses ELCC Class Rating and Resource-Specific ELCC Rating—the core accreditation mechanism for capacity value and resource adequacy showings—which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited toward peak-load obligations and RA requirements.
ai_summary: PJM is conducting a periodic cover-to-cover review of Manual 20A (Resource Adequacy Analysis, Revision 3) and Manual 22 (Generator Resource Performance Indices, Revision 19), with administrative and formulaic updates. Manual 20A now presents ELCC Class Rating and Resource-Specific ELCC Rating as formulas rather than plain text, and adds definitions for Solved Simulated Annual Peak Load and Portfolio EUE. Manual 22 updates references and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor and effective dates for PRISM reliability calculations. First read scheduled June 2, 2026 (Planning Committee); second read and endorsement scheduled June 24, 2026 (Members Resource Committee). These revisions affect how PJM calculates capacity accreditation for all dispatchable and storage resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, which depend on accurate ELCC methodology to establish their contribution to system adequacy.

title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 20a Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: ELCC accreditation methodology for capacity resources directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are valued and compensated in PJM's capacity market; the periodic review updates to ELCC calculation formulas and DER conforming changes impact hydro revenue recognition.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A Revision 3 (effective May 20, 2026) updates the Resource Adequacy Analysis procedure governing ELCC class ratings and resource-specific ELCC ratings used to accredit capacity in PJM's Reliability Pricing Model (RPM). Key changes: two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE) are added, and ELCC calculations are now presented as mathematical formulas rather than text, plus DER conforming changes based on FERC Order 2222 are incorporated (as of Revision 2, December 17, 2025). Hydro and PSH operators rely on ELCC ratings to determine their capacity offering value; reformulated accreditation methodologies can shift which resource types (dispatchable vs. variable; duration-limited vs. unlimited) receive higher accreditation factors, directly altering revenue in PJM's annual capacity auction. The manual governs RTO-wide (0.1 days/year LOLE) and LDA-level (40% × Portfolio EUE) adequacy criteria that drive capacity procurement.

title: Item 04 – 4 Manual 22 Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 22 defines generator performance indices and accredited capacity factors (UCAP) used in PJM's resource adequacy and capacity market, which directly affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities are valued and compensated for capacity accreditation.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 22 (Revision 19, effective May 20, 2026) establishes the technical framework for calculating generator resource performance indices, including forced outage rates, availability factors, and accredited capacity (UCAP factors) used in planning and capacity-market studies. The revision updates references to Manual 21B and 20A (resource adequacy and capability rules) and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor to the Introduction. These metrics directly determine how much capacity credit hydro and pumped-storage units receive in PJM's capacity market and reserve-margin calculations — a core revenue stream for storage and seasonal-limited resources. The manual itself is procedural and defines no new policy, but changes to UCAP accreditation methodology, outage-rate data collection, or reliability-calculation tools (PRISM) flow through this manual and affect what capacity revenue hydro owners can claim.

title: Item 04 – Ugi Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Large customer load interconnection (500 MW in Newport Township) creates transmission expansion need that affects hydro's deliverability and strategic positioning in PJM; load growth directly increases scarcity value of existing hydro capacity.
ai_summary: PJM's UGI transmission zone is evaluating a supplemental transmission project to serve a 500 MW customer load in Newport Township, with phased in-service dates of Q3 2029 (250 MW) and Q1 2030 (250 MW). This is a Need Meeting phase of the M-3 process, meaning stakeholder comments on the project scope are due within 10 days of the June 2, 2026 meeting to inform the Solutions phase. The project demonstrates demand-driven transmission expansion; for hydro owners in PJM, new firm load interconnections reduce transmission congestion risk to existing generation and increase energy scarcity value during peak periods, though the specific route and configuration are not yet determined in this early-stage need meeting.

title: Item 05 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Presentation
relevance_reason: Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) establishes a new parallel queuing process for large generators (≥250 MW UCAP) with compressed timelines, affecting interconnection strategy and deliverability for pumped storage and conventional hydro projects seeking to compete in PJM resource-adequacy procurement.
ai_summary: PJM has filed a tariff amendment (FERC Docket ER26-1563) creating a temporary Expedited Interconnection Track for generators meeting three criteria: ≥250 MW accredited capacity, ability to achieve commercial operation within 36 months, and Primary Siting Authority support. EIT operates as a parallel track outside the standard interconnection queue cycle, compressing the process into ~300 days (Application Review ~180 days, Study Phase ~60 days, Final Agreement Negotiation ~60 days). Manual 14H is being revised to reflect EIT procedures; PJM's Board ruled on the process in June 2026, with FERC ruling anticipated July 31, 2026 and a requested effective date of July 31, 2026. For PSH and large run-of-river projects with construction-readiness and state support, EIT offers a faster path to interconnection and earlier capacity accreditation timing relative to standard queue, affecting project-timing and RA-portfolio strategy; however, EIT projects forfeit the one-time milestone-extension option available to standard-queue projects, tightening execution risk.

title: Item 05 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue reform and expedited interconnection track design directly affect hydro and PSH project deliverability timelines and strategic project-development options in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) to implement an Expedited Interconnection Track, pending FERC approval in Docket ER26-1563-000, effective July 31, 2026. The revision adds a new section 16 describing the expedited process and reorganizes sections to treat it as a parallel pathway alongside the standard interconnection queue. This creates a faster-track option for qualifying projects (specifics on eligibility criteria are not detailed in this executive summary), potentially enabling hydro and PSH developers to achieve COD sooner than the standard process — a strategic advantage in tight RA markets and for repositioning existing generation or pumped-storage capacity additions. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23, 2026.

title: Item 05 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14h revisions establish interconnection queue procedures and surplus interconnection service rules—core obligations affecting hydro and PSH uprate, new-plant, and deliverability pathways in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14h (Revision 06, effective July 23, 2026) governs New Service Requests Cycle Process for generation and transmission interconnections, including system impact study phases, agreement negotiation, deposits, and an expedited interconnection track (EIT). The manual covers generator classification, capacity vs. energy resource election, surplus interconnection service (including deactivation of existing facilities), and modifications at cycle decision points—all mechanics that determine how hydro and PSH plants queue, reserve interconnection capacity, and pursue uprates. This redline version shows the current procedural framework; hydro owners must track revisions to site-control requirements (Section 7), deposit timelines (Section 6), and surplus service deactivation rules (Section 12.3) as they affect project economics and timelines. Most exposed are PSH facilities seeking new queue slots or surplus capacity, and run-of-river plants pursuing uprates via the surplus interconnection pathway.

title: Item 07 – PPL Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large customer load interconnection (900 MW by 2031) in PPL transmission zone triggers transmission-expansion planning that affects hydro deliverability and interregional transfer capability; PJM's supplemental-project review process determines which transmission upgrades get built, reshaping the network through which hydro exports and dispatch instructions flow.
ai_summary: PPL Transmission Zone has received a customer request to serve a 900 MW facility (by 2031) from a 230 kV source near Harrisburg, PA, with in-service date of March 2029. This is classified as a supplemental need under PJM's M-3 transmission-planning process and was presented at the June 2, 2026 TEAC meeting. The document is in the need-meeting stage; stakeholders have 10 days from the meeting to submit comments before the process advances. Large load interconnections like this one drive transmission buildout decisions that alter the network's capability to deliver hydro energy from the Northwest and other hydro-rich regions to load centers; the specifics of how PJM engineers the solution (new circuits, reinforcement, or non-wires alternatives) will shape long-term transmission scarcity rents and locational price signals that hydro operators depend on for revenue.

title: Item 07 – Reliability Compliance Update – Presentation
relevance_reason: NERC's Level 3 Alert and Reliability Guidelines on large computational loads directly affect PJM synchronous and inverter-based resource dispatch, ride-through obligations, and the revenue/operational stability of dispatchable hydro and pumped storage relied upon to manage the second-scale oscillations and frequency disturbances these loads cause.
ai_summary: NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert (responses due August 3, 2026) requiring registered entities to implement seven immediate actions addressing risks posed by computational loads (data centers) causing customer-initiated large load reductions and significant oscillations within seconds — outpacing real-time response capability and threatening BPS reliability. PJM members must comply with this alert and the accompanying NERC Reliability Guideline on risk mitigation for emerging large loads. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, this creates new dispatch obligations (faster frequency response to load swings) and qualification/ride-through requirements under PRC-029-1 Inverter-Based Resource guidance; it also signals potential scarcity value for synchronous hydro's inertial response and for PSH's second-scale ramping to absorb load shocks — contingent on PJM's eventual tariff implementation of NERC's standards revisions (currently in informal comment period through June 17, 2026).

title: Item 11 – DOM Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Massive data-center load growth (3–6.8 GW by 2032) in Dominion's transmission zone directly affects hydro's strategic option to serve co-located or behind-the-meter load, and transmission constraints will shape scarcity pricing and deliverability of hydro exports from the region.
ai_summary: Dominion Energy is presenting supplemental transmission projects to PJM's TEAC to accommodate extraordinary data-center load growth in northern Virginia (North Anna–Thornburg–Bristers corridor and Elmont–Fredericksburg corridor). The Bristers project will install 230 kV conductor to serve ~3 GW of requested data-center load with in-service Q4 2030 ($33.75 M, transmission-only cost). A larger multi-phase Ladysmith/Fredericksburg initiative ($450 M, phases 1 and 2 by 07/2029) targets 4,900–6,800 MW of new load by 2029–2032 via nine new substations in the Elmont–Fredericksburg corridor and ten in the Elmont–Chickahominy corridor. These transmission bottlenecks create windows of scarcity-driven pricing and make hydro's ability to reach and serve co-located or interruptible load behind data-center infrastructure increasingly valuable; owners should monitor whether Dominion's interconnection processes will allow direct-connect hydro or PSH to these load clusters.

title: Item 13 – 2026 Capacity Expansion For RTEP Feedback Assumptions Results
relevance_reason: Capacity expansion modeling for PJM's 2034 8-year planning horizon determines which resource types (solar, wind, battery, nuclear, gas, hydro uprates) are economically selected to meet resource adequacy, directly affecting the revenue competitiveness and dispatch value of existing hydro and potential uprate projects in PJM's footprint.
ai_summary: PJM is using capacity expansion (CapEx) modeling to supplement generation assumptions for the 2034 planning horizon (8-year cases), selecting the most economic resources from interconnection queues (TC2, Cycle 1) to meet a 1-in-10 resource adequacy standard. The model employs ELCC curves that adjust dynamically based on resource-mix penetration—reflecting how hydro's capacity value changes with higher renewable penetration—and includes expansion candidates: solar, onshore/offshore wind, hybrid, combustion turbine, combined cycle, batteries (4–10 hour), and nuclear uprates. PJM will use Scenario B (policy-driven deactivations and Maryland nuclear commitments) as the baseline, with sensitivities testing gas siting restrictions, state storage targets, coal retirements, and gas cost impacts. Results will inform 5-year RTEP solution selection and may highlight development potential in northwestern PJM (ComEd zone). The approach aligns with FERC Order 1920 long-term regional transmission planning requirements and will evolve in successive RTEP cycles.

title: Item 14 – 2026 Capacity Expansion For RTEP Feedback Assumptions Results
relevance_reason: PJM's capacity expansion modeling for the 2034 planning horizon determines generation-mix assumptions that directly affect the transmission needs, resource adequacy targets (ELCC-based), and economic viability of hydro and storage resources competing for capacity value in PJM's footprint.
ai_summary: PJM is conducting capacity-expansion (CapEx) modeling for its 2026 RTEP 8-year case (2034 horizon) to supplement generation assumptions when the system lacks sufficient generation to meet forecast demand (218 GW summer, 205 GW winter peak). The model will select economically optimal resources—solar, wind, hybrid, battery (4–10 hour), CC/CT, and nuclear uprates—from interconnection queues (TC2 and Cycle 1), using ELCC-based resource-adequacy constraints that automatically adjust as resource mix changes. Scenario A.1 (base case) does not build gas in regulated states (NJ, MD, DE, IL, eastern PA) unless projects have ISA/GIA or state agreement backing; Scenario B models policy-driven deactivations and nuclear targets. Results will inform 5-year RTEP solution right-sizing and state commitments, with power flows to be developed for Scenario B. The CapEx approach aligns with FERC Order 1920's long-term regional transmission planning and will evolve over successive cycles. This exercise directly sets the competitive position of hydro (and especially pumped-storage) in PJM's capacity adequacy calculations—any resource sited and given ELCC credit in the 2034 baseline affects scarcity-driven revenue signals and accreditation assumptions that cascade into 5-year capacity-market and real-time dispatch outcomes.

meeting_title: OC Special Session - Manual 13 Revisions Meeting
title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 13 Revision 98 establishes emergency operating procedures affecting how hydropower and other dispatchable resources participate in PJM's capacity and transmission emergency protocols, plus introduces a new Capacity Advisory mechanism that signals resource scarcity — both touch hydro operators' obligations to respond to emergency directives and may alter revenue opportunities during stress periods.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 13 (Emergency Operations) to clarify its roles as Reliability Coordinator, Balancing Authority, and Transmission Operator under NERC EOP-011. The revision introduces a new Capacity Advisory (Section 2.3) to provide advance notice when forecasted available capacity approaches the operating requirement — designed to address gaps in temperature-based emergency triggers and expected to increase in frequency as demand growth outpaces generation interconnection. Revision 98 also establishes Attachment O, a conditional procedure to mobilize backup generation at large data-center loads during extreme emergencies (triggered only if the DOE issues a Section 202(c) order); this procedure was activated during Winter Storm Fern (January 2026) but not implemented. The revision cleanly separates transmission-security emergency procedures from capacity-based procedures, removes capacity-related actions from transmission protocols, and updates language for Advanced Scheduled Resources in cold/hot weather alerts. Endorsement is sought before the summer operating period; the timeline shows second reads at the MRC on June 24, 2026. The Capacity Advisory may increase call frequency for emergency ramping from dispatchable hydro, while data-center load growth (now formally recognized in procedures) creates both scarcity signals that favor hydro dispatch and potential operational constraints if backup-gen mobilization competes for transmission or reserve capacity.

meeting_title: OC Special Session - Manual 13 Revisions Meeting
title: Item 02 – 2 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 13 governs PJM emergency operations including load-shedding procedures, transmission security emergencies, and resource-limitation reporting that directly affect how dispatchable hydro and pumped-storage resources must respond to system stress and what operating obligations they face.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updates emergency-operations procedures across capacity emergencies, weather/environmental events, and transmission security. Key changes include: restructured transmission-security emergency procedures that no longer trigger PAIs or EEAs; split of Maximum Generation Emergency into separate Transmission Security–Local Maximum Generation Emergency Alert and Load Management Alert; addition of Capacity Advisory to Section 2.3; updated Advanced Schedule Resources language for cold and hot weather alerts; and new Attachment O on emergency back-up generator use. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the procedure changes govern mandatory response protocols during transmission constraints (Section 5.2) and capacity shortages (Section 2), and clarify which emergency triggers obligate generation or discharge (e.g., load-shedding directives, transmission relief actions). The removal of PAI/EEA linkage to transmission-security procedures may reduce frequency of certain dispatch signals to storage resources during local constraints. Pumped-storage operators should review Section 2 (reserve and capacity procedures), Section 5.2 (transmission-security load and generation actions), and Attachments E–F (manual load-dump allocation) to confirm charging/discharging obligations under updated protocols.

title: Item 01 – 1 Rmr Resources – Tariff Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Tariff revisions to PJM's RMR (Retained for Reliability) resource capacity-market treatment—specifically, how retained resources clear auctions, accredit UCAP, and receive capacity-market revenues—directly affect hydro and other dispatchable units that PJM may retain to address transmission reliability, and establish the revenue and settlement rules governing their participation in the capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM staff have redlined tariff language (Attachment DD, section 5.3) governing Retained-for-Reliability (RMR) generation resources' participation in the capacity market (RPM auctions and accreditation). The proposal clarifies that RMR resources not clearing an auction on their own offer are deemed to submit a $0/MW-Day Sell Offer in the Base Residual Auction for their full Accredited UCAP (up to Capacity Interconnection Rights), provided they are deliverable, operationally available, and not subject to bilateral restrictions. Any accreditation increase post-auction is automatically offered in the Third Incremental Auction at $0/MW-Day; if accreditation decreases or the resource no longer meets RMR criteria, PJM procures replacement capacity. Capacity-market revenues from RMR units are credited to the load zones paying for the resource's continuing operations under its FERC-accepted rate schedule, rather than to the RMR unit operator; credits cease if the rate schedule expires, the unit deactivates, or catastrophic failure occurs. This provision extends through the 2029/2030 Delivery Year unless FERC accepts an extension. Retained dispatchable and storage resources relying on RMR rate schedules—including some legacy hydro units—will have their capacity revenues redirected to load-serving entities paying for reliability upgrades, fundamentally altering the revenue model for RMR-designated plants. The tariff change appears to be in draft form (redline status suggests it has not yet cleared stakeholder review or FERC).

title: Item 01 – 2 Brandon Shores 12 And Wagner 34 Deactivation And Rmr – Presentation
relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must Run) arrangement extending beyond planned deactivation date directly affects hydro and thermal generator retirement decisions and the transmission-investment timeline that determines when generating resources become dispatchable rather than must-run — a core strategic option for fleet planning.
ai_summary: PJM has assessed whether Brandon Shores Units 1&2 (635 MW + 638 MW, BGE-owned, Maryland) and Wagner Units 3&4 (305 MW + 397 MW, BGE-owned, Maryland) can safely deactivate on their current RMR end date of May 31, 2029. Planning studies found thermal and voltage violations would persist through 2030 even with baseline transmission projects in service, because several major 500 kV and 765 kV lines required for reliability (e.g., Morrisville–Vint Hill–Wishing Star, North Delta–High Ridge, Peach Bottom–Conastone rebuilds, Amos-Welton–Spring–Rocky Point, Joshua Falls–Yeat) do not reach in-service dates until mid-to-late 2030 or 2031. PJM's recommendation is to extend RMR beyond May 2029 through at least 2030 to maintain reliability until these transmission enhancements complete; next steps are RMR agreement negotiation and FERC filing. This determines whether 1,975 MW of generation will remain must-run (and thus unavailable for economic dispatch or retirement) well past currently assumed end dates — a precedent affecting all generators assessing retirement timing in constrained transmission zones.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: Interconnection process rules and cycle schedules directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking to connect to PJM, and System Impact Study Reports may address hydro resource deliverability and grid integration.
ai_summary: This agenda covers PJM's interconnection process, including Cycle Schedule updates, Construction Metrics, and System Impact Study Reports for Transition Cycle 2, Phase 2—all critical procedures that govern how hydropower and pumped-storage projects enter and advance through PJM's interconnection queue.

relevance_reason: Interconnection process rules and queue management directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage project development timelines and cost recovery.

ai_summary: PJM's Interconnection Process Subcommittee meeting addresses the procedures and timeline for interconnecting new generation resources, including hydropower and pumped-storage projects. Changes to interconnection queue management, study timelines, or cost responsibility rules can materially impact hydro developer participation in PJM markets.

title: Item 03 – Cycle Schedule Update
relevance_reason: This document addresses PJM's interconnection process cycles and timelines, which directly affect hydropower and storage project interconnection, network upgrade funding, and participation in generator interconnection queues.
ai_summary: The document updates the status of Transition Cycle 1 (TC1) and Cycle 1 interconnection processes, including retool results showing $172.5M in underfunded network upgrades and $132M in forfeited readiness deposits. It also tracks 278 projects in TC2 Phase II study with June 5th SIS report posting. These processes are critical for hydropower and pumped-storage developers seeking interconnection to PJM's transmission system.

title: Item 04 – Construction Metrics
relevance_reason: The document tracks PJM interconnection construction metrics including explicit hydro project data, storage resources, and future project pipelines—all directly relevant to hydropower and pumped-storage industry participation in the RTO market.
ai_summary: This construction metrics report shows hydro and storage projects in PJM's interconnection queue across multiple development stages (engineering, procurement, under construction, in service). The document tracks 2026 generation additions by fuel type including hydro, storage capacity placed in service, and project status timelines through 2030, providing visibility into hydropower and PSH deployment in the region.

title: System Impact Study Phase Ii Reports IPS May 2026
relevance_reason: This document describes PJM's System Impact Study (SIS) process for interconnection requests, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage projects navigate transmission interconnection and cost allocation.
ai_summary: The document outlines PJM's interconnection study methodology including transmission reinforcement identification, cost allocation, and readiness deposit requirements. These procedures are critical for hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking to interconnect to the PJM transmission system, particularly regarding how network upgrade costs are allocated and dependencies between projects are evaluated.

Item 2.A covers Manual 11 revisions on resource scheduling and the day-ahead energy market. Item 4 addresses Market Investment Incentives, which may affect hydro investment economics.

title: Consent Agenda A – Draft MRC Minutes – 4222026
relevance_reason: The document discusses battery dispatch in transmission planning (Manual 14B) and regulation redesign (Manual 15), both of which affect energy storage and ancillary services markets relevant to pumped-storage hydropower competition and participation.

stakeholders: Clara Summers (Illinois Citizens Utility Board (IL CUB))
Mel El Atieh (Pennsylvania Office of Consumer Advocate (PA OCA))
ai_summary: PJM advanced conforming revisions to Manual 14B addressing how batteries will be dispatched in RTEP base cases, and proposed revisions to Manual 15 addressing Regulation Redesign Phase 2. These changes affect market rules for energy storage and ancillary services that directly impact hydropower and pumped-storage participation in PJM markets.

title: Consent Agenda D – 2 M14a Retirement
relevance_reason: This manual governs PJM's interconnection process for new generation and transmission projects, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities enter the transmission system and participate in markets.
ai_summary: Manual 14A describes the New Services Request process including queue positioning, feasibility/system impact/facilities studies, and interconnection agreements—all critical procedural pathways for hydropower and PSH projects seeking to interconnect to PJM. The document references Interconnection Service Agreements, cost allocation methodologies, and network/local upgrade determinations that affect hydro project viability and transmission deliverability.

title: Consent Agenda D – 3 M14e Retirement
relevance_reason: This manual governs transmission interconnection and upgrade requests in PJM, which directly affects hydropower and pumped-storage facilities seeking to interconnect or expand transmission capacity for deliverability.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14E establishes the process for transmission interconnection requests and upgrade requests, including technical design requirements, transmission injection/withdrawal rights, and incremental deliverability rights—all of which are critical for hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking grid access and transmission service in the PJM region.

title: Consent Agenda E – 1 Manual 14h Elimination Of First Use Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document addresses Manual 14H revisions affecting generation interconnection jurisdiction determination and FERC compliance, which directly impacts how hydropower and pumped-storage projects are classified and processed in PJM interconnection procedures.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing conforming changes to Manual 14H per FERC Docket ER26-5-000, eliminating the "first use" criterion for jurisdiction classification of generation interconnection requests. This affects how hydropower and other resources are categorized as FERC or non-FERC jurisdictional, with changes effective April 28, 2026 for interconnection Cycle 2 and beyond.

title: Consent Agenda E – 3 Manual 14h Elimination Of First Use Revisions – Redlines
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 14H governs the interconnection process for new generation resources, including pumped-storage hydropower, and addresses capacity interconnection rights, generator obligations, and transmission service—all critical to hydro market participation.
ai_summary: This manual establishes PJM's procedural framework for interconnecting new generation facilities, including pumped-storage hydropower projects. Key sections address capacity resource status election (9.4), transmission service requirements for generators, site control documentation, study phases, and interconnection agreements—all directly applicable to hydro and PSH developers seeking grid access and participating in capacity markets.

title: Consent Agenda F – 1 Manual 14h Replacment Generation Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: This document describes PJM's new Replacement Generation Interconnection Service (RGIS) process, which allows expedited transfer of Capacity Interconnection Rights (CIRs) from deactivating generating facilities to replacement resources at the same point of interconnection—a mechanism directly applicable to hydropower and pumped-storage facility replacements and upgrades.
ai_summary: PJM has established an expedited interconnection process (RGIS) outside the standard interconnection queue that allows hydropower and other generation owners to transfer Capacity Interconnection Rights from deactivating units to replacement resources at the same POI more quickly, with faster RTEP model inclusion and GIA execution. This affects how hydro operators can pursue repowering, relicensing, or facility replacement projects in PJM.

title: Consent Agenda F – 2 Manual 14h Replacment Generation Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: The document describes PJM's new Replacement Generation Interconnection Service process, which affects how generation resources—including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities—interconnect to the transmission system and transfer Capacity Interconnection Rights.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing a new Replacement Generation Interconnection Service (FERC ER26-403-000) that establishes a streamlined process for generators replacing existing capacity. This affects how hydropower and PSH projects acquire and transfer Capacity Interconnection Rights (CIRs) and navigate the interconnection queue, with potential implications for project economics and development timelines.

title: Consent Agenda F – 3 Manual 14h Replacment Generation Revisions – Redlines
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 14H governs the interconnection process for new generation and transmission service requests, which directly affects hydropower and pumped-storage hydropower project interconnection and transmission deliverability in PJM.
ai_summary: This manual establishes the procedural framework for how hydropower and pumped-storage projects submit interconnection requests, progress through study phases, negotiate interconnection agreements, and ultimately achieve commercial operation in PJM. It covers application requirements, system impact studies, deposit obligations, and final agreement execution—all critical to hydro project developers seeking to interconnect facilities or obtain transmission service.

title: Consent Agenda G – 2 Manual 15 Biennial Review Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Manual 15 changes to opportunity cost calculator and VOM adder formats directly affect how hydropower and storage resources are valued and dispatched in PJM's market.

These changes impact how variable and dispatchable hydro resources—particularly pumped-storage hydropower—have their operating and startup costs calculated and reflected in unit commitment and dispatch decisions.

title: Consent Agenda G – 3 Manual 15 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Section 7 explicitly covers hydroelectric and pumped-storage hydro cost development guidelines, including pumping efficiency, incremental energy cost, and ancillary services—core to hydro market participation in PJM.
ai_summary: This manual establishes cost development methodologies for hydroelectric and pumped-storage units in PJM, covering pumping efficiency, fuel costs (water value), incremental energy costs, start-up and no-load costs, and ancillary service costs. These guidelines directly affect how hydro resources are compensated and bid into PJM markets.

title: Consent Agenda H – 1 Manual 15 Regulation Redesign Phase 2 Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: Document updates PJM Manual 15 cost development guidelines for regulation ancillary services, including specific provisions for Energy Storage Unit losses and cost calculations under the new Regulation-Up/Regulation-Down market products, directly affecting how hydropower and storage resources are compensated for providing regulation services.

These changes affect how pumped-storage and run-of-river hydropower units bid and recover costs for regulation capability.

title: Consent Agenda H – 2 Manual 15 Reg Redesign Phase 2 Revisions – Executive Summary

The changes specifically mention Energy Storage Unit Losses and create separate mileage costs for each regulation product, directly affecting how PSH and battery storage resources recover costs for providing regulation services.

ai_summary: PJM's Congestion Management Process working group is evaluating Parallel Flow Visualization (PFV) as an alternative to the Freeze Date method for market-to-market settlements, and explicitly considering energy storage resource implications in flowgate allocation. FERC Order 881 impacts are under review, which may affect how hydropower and storage resources are treated in transmission planning and market operations.

title: Item 01 1 – Manual 15 Revisions On Regulation Redesign Phase 2 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: The document describes Manual-15 revisions to regulation market design affecting cost calculations for Regulation-Up and Regulation-Down products, which directly impact how energy storage resources (including pumped-storage hydropower) are compensated in PJM's ancillary services markets.
ai_summary: PJM is revising its regulation cost adder formulas and mileage cost calculations to accommodate new Regulation-Up and Regulation-Down products under Regulation Redesign Phase II. For storage resources, the changes include halving VOM cost increases and energy storage unit losses for regulation-only resources, and lowering the Margin Risk Adder cap from $12 to $6—material changes affecting PSH and battery storage economics in the regulation market.

title: Item 01 2 – Manual 15 Revisions On Regulation Redesign Phase 2 – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document addresses cost development guidelines for regulation service products (Regulation-Up and Regulation-Down) under PJM's Regulation Redesign Phase II, which directly affects how energy storage and pumped-storage hydropower can participate in ancillary services markets.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 15 Rev. 49 updates cost calculation methodologies for regulation services, including specific adjustments for "Energy Storage Unit Losses" that are divided by 2 for Regulation-Up or Regulation-Down products. These changes affect how pumped-storage hydropower and battery storage resources can bid into regulation markets and recover operating costs, with revised margin risk adders capped at $6 per unit of service.

title: Item 01 3 – Manual 15 Revisions On Regulation Redesign Phase 2 – Redline

title: Item 04 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Presentation

This reform affects hydropower and other dispatchable resources' compensation for ancillary services and reserve provision, with a soft launch in May 2026 and full implementation in Q2 2027. The changes hold generators more accountable to their bid-in ramp rates and operating parameters.

ai_summary: PJM proposes de-risking ELCC forecasts for RBP resources by fixing payments based on PJM's 15-year ELCC forecast rather than exposing resources to annual ELCC volatility. The proposal uses fixed-for-floating swaps to guarantee Resource Fixed Payments, which could substantially improve project bankability for storage resources but also creates true-up mechanisms that shift ELCC risk from suppliers to large loads.

ai_summary: The report shows capacity costs increased 263% year-over-year and ancillary service revenues (regulation up 64%, synchronized reserves up 96%) are growing, indicating favorable market conditions for dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage. Rising congestion costs (up 81%) and uplift credits (up 183%) also affect hydro deliverability and compensation mechanisms.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: The agenda includes battery dispatch in transmission planning (Manual 14B), regulation redesign Phase 2, and reserve certainty discussions—all relevant to energy storage and hydropower market participation.

ai_summary: PJM is updating Manual 14B to address how batteries will be dispatched in RTEP base cases, revising Manual 15 for Regulation Redesign Phase 2, and holding a Reserve Certainty Senior Task Force update. These tariff and manual changes affect storage and dispatchable resource participation in PJM markets.

title: Consent Agenda A – Draft MRC Minutes – 3252026
relevance_reason: The document discusses battery dispatch in transmission planning (Manual 14B), energy storage market rules, and flexible load management—all topics that directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage participation in PJM markets.

Large Load Customer Flexibility [evaluation in progress]
Load Management and Price Responsive Demand Event Performance [awaiting implementation & ferc approval]
ai_summary: Manual 14B revisions addressing battery dispatch in RTEP base cases are under review, which establishes operational and market frameworks that affect how storage (including PSH) competes and is planned alongside other resources. Load management and price-responsive demand rules also influence the ancillary services and flexibility markets in which hydropower typically participates.

title: Consent Agenda B – 1 Manual 12 Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document discusses updates to PJM Manual 12 on balancing operations, including removal of the Hydro Calculator tool and changes to reserves activation procedures that affect hydropower dispatch and ancillary service participation.
ai_summary: Revision 57 removes the Hydro Calculator from PJM's PC Applications (Section 2.1.2), updates synchronized reserves loading procedures, and renames/restructures reserve activation protocols. These operational and tool changes have direct implications for how hydropower resources participate in PJM's real-time balancing and reserve markets.

title: Consent Agenda B – 2 Manual 12 Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: The document addresses Manual 12: Balancing Operations updates including removal of the Hydro Calculator, changes to real-time data sources, and reserve activation procedures that directly affect hydropower dispatch and ancillary service participation in PJM markets.
ai_summary: PJM is updating its Balancing Operations manual to remove the Hydro Calculator tool (Section 2.1.2) and modernize supporting systems, while also clarifying procedures for synchronized reserve loading and simultaneous activation of reserves. These changes affect how hydropower resources interface with PJM's real-time operations and ancillary service markets.

title: Consent Agenda B – 3 Manual 12 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: This manual covers dispatching, ancillary services (regulation, reserves, black start), and balancing operations that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are controlled and compensated in PJM markets.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 12 establishes operational procedures for regulation service qualification (Section 4.4), reserve provision (Section 4.1), and black start capability (Section 4.5)—all services that hydropower and PSH typically supply. The manual also addresses primary frequency response and dynamic transfers, which are relevant to flexible hydro resource dispatch and interconnection.

title: Consent Agenda C – 1 Manual 14b Revisions For Battery Dispatch – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document addresses battery dispatch methodology in transmission planning and capacity accreditation (ELCC classes and Effective Nameplate Capacity), which directly affects how energy storage resources—including pumped-storage hydropower and co-located facilities—are modeled and valued in PJM's resource adequacy and interconnection processes.
initiatives: Battery Dispatch Update [awaiting implementation & ferc approval]
ai_summary: PJM is updating Manual 14B to dispatch batteries in the RTEP base case rather than modeling them offline, improving alignment with actual interconnection queue projects and state policies. The revision also references Limited Duration Resource capacity accreditation rules tied to ELCC classes and co-located resources, which establish the framework for how PSH and mixed-technology facilities (including hydro-storage hybrids) are credited for capacity purposes.

title: Consent Agenda C – 3 Manual 14b Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 14B on transmission planning directly addresses generation deliverability testing methods and reliability criteria that affect hydropower and pumped-storage interconnection and market participation in PJM.
initiatives: Battery Dispatch Update [awaiting implementation & ferc approval]
ai_summary: This transmission planning manual includes deliverability testing methods for generation (Attachment C) and reliability planning criteria (Attachment D) that are foundational to how hydropower and pumped-storage projects are evaluated for interconnection and their ability to deliver power to load centers. The document also addresses market efficiency analysis and system operating limits that influence hydro's role in transmission planning.

title: Consent Agenda C – 4 Battery Dispatch Problem Statement – Information Only
relevance_reason: Battery dispatch methodology and transmission planning rule changes directly affect energy storage resources, including pumped-storage hydropower, in PJM's RTEP base case modeling and market participation.
initiatives: Battery Dispatch Update [awaiting implementation & ferc approval]
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14B to allow batteries (including PSH) to be modeled as dispatched in RTEP base cases rather than offline, improving alignment with state policies and interconnection queue assumptions. This change affects how storage resources are credited for capacity and transmission planning purposes, with an April 22, 2026 effective date.

title: Consent Agenda C – 5 Battery Dispatch Issue Charge – Information Only
relevance_reason: Battery dispatch rules in transmission planning (RTEP) directly affect how energy storage resources, including pumped-storage hydropower, compete and are modeled in PJM's resource adequacy and planning processes.
initiatives: Battery Dispatch Update [awaiting implementation & ferc approval]
ai_summary: PJM is updating Manual 14-B to revise how battery resources are dispatched in RTEP base cases, which will impact transmission planning assumptions and interconnection queue treatment. This is relevant to PSH because battery dispatch methodology and modeling directly influence resource adequacy assumptions, transmission needs assessment, and competitive positioning of all flexible storage resources in PJM.

title: Consent Agenda D – 1 Manual 33 202c Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document addresses Manual 33 revisions regarding market data postings and cost allocation transparency for DOE 202(c) orders that require resources to continue operating beyond deactivation dates, which directly affects dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 33 to memorialize transparency provisions for DOE 202(c) cost allocation, requiring public posting of monthly credits, capacity obligation denominators, and resulting rates for resources mandated to run beyond requested deactivation. This affects hydropower and PSH operators subject to these emergency orders and their participation in capacity market cost recovery mechanisms.

title: Item 01a – 2 Load Management And Prd Event Performance Tariff And Raa Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Document directly addresses capacity market performance measurement rules affecting storage resources (including pumped-storage hydropower) during emergency actions, with specific provisions for how storage resource performance is calculated and charged.
initiatives: Load Management and Price Responsive Demand Event Performance [awaiting implementation & ferc approval]
ai_summary: This tariff revision modifies PJM's non-performance charges and performance credits mechanism for capacity resources, with explicit treatment of 'Capacity Storage Resources' in the performance shortfall calculation. The rules define how storage resources' metered output and reserve/regulation assignments are measured during Performance Assessment Intervals triggered by Emergency Actions, directly affecting PSH market participation and revenue.

title: Item 02 – 1 Connect And Manage Problem – Statement – Il Cub Proposed Revisions
relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market design changes that will affect how large flexible loads participate in PJM's RPM auction, which directly impacts resource adequacy rules and market participation opportunities for all generation resources including hydropower.
initiatives: Implementation of Connect and Manage Framework for Large Load Interconnections [solution alternatives proposed]
ai_summary: PJM is implementing a 'Connect and Manage' framework for large loads (primarily data centers) with curtailment obligations during system stress. The document discusses whether and how these loads should be reflected in the RPM demand curve, which affects capacity market clearing prices and revenue opportunities for all generators, including hydropower facilities that provide flexible ramping and reserve services.

title: Item 02 – 3 Connect And Manage Issue – Charge – Il Cub Proposed Revisions With PJM Icc Amendments
relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market rules and demand curve mechanisms that directly affect how large loads are treated in PJM's resource adequacy framework, which has implications for hydropower and storage participation in capacity markets.
initiatives: Implementation of Connect and Manage Framework for Large Load Interconnections [solution alternatives proposed]
stakeholders: Illinois Citizens Utility Board (Illinois Citizens Utility Board)
ai_summary: PJM is implementing a 'Connect and Manage' framework for new large load additions that lack their own generation, which affects how demand is included in the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM) capacity market. This framework may influence capacity market clearing prices and reserve margin requirements that hydropower and pumped-storage facilities depend on for adequate compensation, and could create opportunities for flexible resources (including storage) to provide demand response and curtailment services.

title: Item 03c – 1 Manual 14a And 14e Retirements – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 14A and 14E govern new services requests and transmission interconnection requests, which directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage projects gain grid access and participate in PJM markets.
ai_summary: PJM is retiring Manual 14A (New Services Request Process) and Manual 14E (Upgrade and Transmission Interconnection Requests) as of June 30, 2026, consolidating their contents into Manual 14H. These manuals are critical to hydropower and PSH interconnection and must be monitored to ensure hydro-friendly provisions are preserved in the consolidated Manual 14H.

title: Item 03c – 2 Manual 14a Retirement
relevance_reason: This manual describes PJM's interconnection process for new generation and transmission services, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage projects enter the queue, undergo feasibility and system impact studies, and negotiate interconnection agreements—all critical to hydro industry participation.
ai_summary: Manual 14A governs the New Services Request process for generation interconnections in PJM, including study procedures (feasibility, system impact, facilities), cost allocation, queue positioning, and interconnection agreements. Hydropower and pumped-storage projects must follow these procedures to interconnect to the PJM transmission system and participate in markets.

title: Item 03c – 3 Manual 14e Retirement
relevance_reason: This manual governs transmission interconnection and upgrade requests in PJM, which directly affects hydropower and pumped-storage project interconnection, deliverability rights, and transmission service eligibility.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14E establishes the queue process, customer classifications, and technical requirements for transmission interconnection requests and upgrades. For hydropower and PSH developers, this manual is critical because it defines how projects obtain transmission injection/withdrawal rights, incremental deliverability rights, and capacity transfer rights—all essential for project viability and market participation. The manual also specifies the interconnection customer obligations and technical design standards that hydro facilities must meet.

title: Item 03d – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Elimination Of First Use – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document addresses Manual 14H revisions affecting interconnection requirements and FERC jurisdictional classification for generation projects, which directly impacts hydropower and pumped-storage facility interconnection processes.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing conforming changes to Manual 14H per FERC Docket ER26-5-000 that eliminate the 'first use' concept and clarify jurisdictional determination (FERC vs non-FERC) for generation interconnection applications. These procedural changes effective April 28, 2026 will affect how hydropower and pumped-storage projects navigate the interconnection queue and regulatory classification in Cycle 2 and beyond.

title: Item 03d – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Elimination Of First Use – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: The document addresses generation interconnection rules and FERC jurisdictional determinations that directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking interconnection to the PJM network.
ai_summary: PJM is modifying Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) to conform with FERC Docket ER26-5-000, which eliminates the "first use" requirement and clarifies application procedures for non-FERC jurisdictional generation interconnection. These changes affect how hydropower and pumped-storage projects navigate interconnection qualification and jurisdictional classification within PJM.

title: Item 03d – 3 Manual 14h Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: This manual governs PJM's interconnection process for new generation and transmission service requests, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage projects navigate transmission interconnection and capacity accreditation pathways.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H establishes the procedural framework for interconnecting new generating facilities, including generation interconnection ASA requirements, system impact studies, and capacity resource status elections (Section 9.4). Hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking to interconnect or modify existing facilities must follow these phases and decision points, including deposit requirements and transmission service agreements.

title: Item 03e – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Replacment Gen Updates – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document details PJM's new Replacement Generation Interconnection Service (RGIS) process, which expedites transfer of Capacity Interconnection Rights (CIRs) from deactivating to replacement generation resources at the same point of interconnection—directly relevant to hydropower and pumped-storage facilities seeking to relicense or replace retired units while preserving interconnection rights.
ai_summary: PJM has established an expedited RGIS process outside the standard interconnection queue to allow deactivating generators (including hydropower) to transfer CIRs to replacement resources at the same POI, with faster timeline inclusion in RTEP studies. Manual 14H revisions codify this process with new definitions, study agreements, and milestones applicable to replacement generation project developers.

title: Item 03e – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Replacement Generation – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: The document describes PJM's new Replacement Generation Interconnection Service process, which directly affects how generators—including hydropower and pumped-storage projects—interconnect and transfer capacity rights in the RTO.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing a Replacement Generation Interconnection Service that creates a parallel interconnection pathway and modifies Capacity Interconnection Rights (CIR) transfer procedures. This affects how hydro and storage developers access the grid and manage interconnection agreements, with conforming changes to Manual 14H under FERC Docket ER26-403-000.

title: Item 03e – 3 Manual 14h Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: This manual governs the interconnection process for new generation and transmission service requests in PJM, which directly affects hydropower and pumped-storage facility interconnection, capacity accreditation, and transmission deliverability.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H outlines the complete cycle process for new service requests including generation interconnection, which is critical for hydropower and pumped-storage developers. The manual covers system impact studies, interconnection agreements, capacity resource status election (Section 9.4), and deposit requirements—all of which affect how hydro facilities enter and participate in PJM markets.

title: Item 03f – 1 Manual 15 Revisions Biennial Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 15 biennial review includes clarifications to opportunity cost calculator and VOM adder formats that directly affect how generators—including hydropower—structure their dispatch bids and cost submissions in PJM markets.
ai_summary: Changes to Manual 15 sections 2.6, 12.7.1, and 12.7.6 clarify Variable Operations and Maintenance (VOM) adder formats and Opportunity Cost Calculator dispatch-level adders on $/start and $/hour bases. These provisions govern how all resources, including hydro, submit incremental operating costs and startup costs that influence their competitive positioning in energy and ancillary service markets.

title: Item 03f – 3 Manual 15 Revisions Biennial Review – Redline
relevance_reason: Section 7 is dedicated to hydropower and pumped-storage cost development guidelines, covering pumping efficiency, incremental energy cost, start-up cost, no-load cost, and maintenance—all critical to hydro participation in PJM markets.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 15 Revision 48 establishes cost development guidelines for hydropower and pumped-storage units operating in PJM markets. Section 7 specifically addresses pumping efficiency and performance factors for PSH, fuel cost (water value), incremental energy cost, start-up cost, no-load cost, and maintenance cost methodology—essential inputs for hydro units to submit bids and compete in energy and ancillary service markets.

title: Item 03g – 1 Manual 15 Revisions Regulation Redesign Phase 2 – Presentation
relevance_reason: The document details revisions to PJM's regulation service cost development guidelines, specifically addressing energy storage unit losses and cost allocation for Regulation-Up/Down products, which directly affect how pumped-storage hydropower and other storage resources are compensated for ancillary services.
ai_summary: Manual 15 Rev. 49 implements Regulation Redesign Phase II changes that modify how regulation service costs are calculated for energy storage units, including adjustments to VOM costs, energy losses, and margin risk adders. These changes affect the cost recovery and dispatch economics for pumped-storage hydropower and other storage resources participating in PJM's regulation market.

title: Item 03g – 2 Manual 15 Revisions Regulation Redesign Phase 2 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: The document addresses regulation market design changes including cost adders and mileage calculations that directly affect how energy storage units (including pumped-storage hydropower) are compensated in PJM's ancillary services markets.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Regulation Redesign Phase II with changes to Manual-15 that split regulation service into Regulation-Up and Regulation-Down products. The changes specifically address cost calculations for Energy Storage Units, including revised margin risk adders, mileage costs, and variable operating maintenance (VOM) treatment—all of which materially affect PSH and battery storage participation in regulation markets.

title: Item 03g – 3 Manual 15 Revisions Regulation Redesign Phase 2 – Redline
relevance_reason: Section 7 provides cost development guidelines specifically for hydroelectric and pumped-storage hydroelectric units, covering incremental energy cost, start-up cost, no-load cost, maintenance, and ancillary services relevant to PJM market participation.
ai_summary: This manual establishes PJM's cost methodology for hydro and pumped-storage units, including pumping efficiency performance factors, fuel cost (water) treatment, incremental energy cost calculation, start-up and no-load costs, and maintenance cost guidelines—all essential for hydro unit participation and bid development in PJM markets.

title: Item 04 – 1 Rcstf Next Steps – Presentation
relevance_reason: This document presents PJM's comprehensive reserve market reform proposals that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources can participate in reserve services, pricing mechanisms, and locational procurement.
ai_summary: The Reserve Certainty Senior Task Force (RCSTF) is proposing significant changes to PJM's reserve market structure, including new/modified reserve products, updated eligibility requirements, revised offer price rules, and changes to locational reserve procurement. These reforms will directly impact how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities can offer regulation, spinning reserve, and ramping services, and will affect their revenue through reserve market pricing and co-optimization with energy markets.

title: Item 05 – PJM Reliability Backstop Procurement Design – PJM Presentation
relevance_reason: The document establishes a reliability backstop procurement mechanism that directly affects eligible supply resources, including energy storage and demand response, with procurement rules, ELCC requirements, and interconnection processes that will shape hydropower and pumped-storage participation in PJM markets.
ai_summary: PJM is designing a reliability backstop procurement with two phases: bilateral contracting (April 2026–March 2027) and central procurement (March 2027 onward) targeting ~15,000 MW of new capacity by 2031. Eligible resources include new generation, storage, and demand response; procurement is capacity-only (UCAP) with 2–15 year terms and ELCC-based offers, which directly affects how pumped-storage hydropower and flexible hydro resources compete and are valued.

ai_summary: PJM is designing a two-phase reliability backstop procurement process (bilateral contracting followed by central procurement) to procure 14,862 MW of new capacity by June 1, 2031. Storage resources are explicitly listed as eligible supply, and the mechanism's design—including ELCC-based UCAP offerings, contract-for-differences settlement against RPM, and 2-to-15-year terms—will materially affect how hydropower and pumped-storage projects participate in PJM's resource adequacy framework.
