title: Item 04 – PJM Destf Package April 2026
relevance_reason: The document addresses RMR (Reliability Must-Run) reduction and generation/transmission solutions for reliability, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources participate in resource adequacy and interconnection processes in PJM.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM is developing a two-path framework to reduce or eliminate RMR requirements by allowing either PJM-led transmission solicitation (Path 1) or state-sponsored generation alternatives (Path 2). Hydropower and pumped-storage operators should monitor this because it affects how storage and dispatchable hydro resources can compete to meet reliability needs, the role of expedited interconnection, and cost recovery for generation solutions retained as backups.

title: 00-Markets+ Intro Slides 20260611.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: Markets+ is a seams-governance initiative directly affecting Western hydro's strategic options for RTO participation, regional market integration, and revenue-recovery opportunities across SPP footprint expansion.
ai_summary: This is an introductory presentation for the Markets+ Seams Working Group (MSWG) net conference on June 11, 2026. The document establishes meeting logistics, anti-trust protocols, and engagement rules for panelists (task-force members) versus attendees. No substantive market-design proposals or decisions are presented in these slides; the document serves as a procedural framing for the working group's ongoing deliberations on seams issues and market integration. For hydro owners tracking Markets+ as a potential alternative or complement to EDAM/WEIM membership, this confirms active engagement by Western utilities (BPA via proxy, Idaho Power via proxy) in the MSWG, signaling continued development of the SPP-led seams framework.

title: 00-Markets+ Intro Slides 20260611.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
relevance_reason: SEAMS GOVERNANCE: This is a foundational Markets+ Seams Working Group meeting establishing the forum and process for regional market integration, which directly affects hydro owners' strategic options for participation across multiple RTOs and their ability to access different market designs and revenue mechanisms.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ Seams Working Group convened June 11, 2026, with introductory slides establishing meeting procedures, attendee roles (panelists vs. observers), accessibility, and governance. This is the operational kickoff of Markets+ — the multiregional market-seams coordination initiative NHA members track as an alternative/complement to EDAM. The working group's decisions on data sharing, operational coordination, and interregional product design will determine which hydro assets can participate in which markets and under what rules. Hydro owners with operations or contract exposure across the SPP, ERCOT, and other affiliated regions should monitor MSWG outputs for changes to transmission use, dispatch authority, capacity accreditation portability, and ancillary-service procurement that affect asset monetization and operational flexibility.

title: 01-MSWG Agenda 20260611.docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ Seams Working Group agenda addresses interregional market coordination, export scheduling, and system operations affecting hydro deliverability and revenue across SPP–CAISO boundary; specific action items on export tag adjustments, interchange transaction scheduling (MITSPS), and high-priority export surplus calculations directly shape how hydro exports are priced and procured at the seam.
ai_summary: Markets+ Seams Working Group (MSWG) convenes June 11, 2026, to advance several seams-coordination mechanisms affecting export scheduling and pricing between SPP and CAISO. Agenda items include MIR75-R (export tag adjustments), MIR60-R (Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules—MITSPS), MIR83 (eTag status), and high-priority export surplus calculation methodology. These mechanisms govern how hydro and other resources schedule and price power flowing across the SPP–CAISO boundary. The working group is coordinating with a planned September in-person joint MSWG–Seams Advisory Group meeting in Denver and will maintain regular monthly meetings through December 2026. Owners with generation near or exporting through the SPP–CAISO seam should monitor export tag rule changes and interchange scheduling impacts on revenues.

title: 01-MSWG Agenda 20260611.docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: this agenda covers Markets+ seams governance and interregional transaction/export processes (MIR60-R MITSPS, MIR83 eTag, SPP-CAISO dialogue) that directly affect how hydropower and PSH operators participate in and settle interregional energy transfers, which is critical to Northwest hydro exports and revenue.
ai_summary: The Seams Working Group is meeting June 11, 2026, to address three operational seams topics: Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) scheduling, eTag status coordination, and an SPP-CAISO dialogue on seams issues. The agenda also includes policy questions on High Priority System Sales. These administrative and governance items directly govern how hydro owners execute interregional transactions, settle exports, and allocate transmission points across the SPP-CAISO boundary — core to monetizing hydro generation in multi-RTO footprints. The working group will also discuss future meeting logistics, including a possible September in-person joint meeting in Denver with the broader Seams Advisory Group.

title: 02-MIR75-R SPP Comments-20260520 (1).docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes e-Tag adjustment rules for high-priority export transactions when a Resource's maximum output (Max ECOL for conventional units, discharge limit for MSRs, or VER forecast) is less than the export bid amount. This directly affects hydro and pumped-storage export revenue by setting when and how export clearings are reduced pro-rata across multiple tags using the same source — a critical rule for dispatchable export scheduling.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes mechanics (MIR75-R) for adjusting high-priority export e-Tags when a Resource's maximum available capacity (Max ECOL/discharge limit) or VER forecast falls below the sum of all Normal-transaction export bids. When reduction is required, adjustments are made pro-rata across all tags sharing the same source, rounded to whole MW, with a 1 MW minimum threshold. The rule applies in Day-Ahead Market (adjustment posted at market close) and Real-Time Balancing Market (adjustment in Pre-RTBM SCED study). This clarifies policy intent from prior MSWG/MDWG decisions (April 16, 2026 meeting) by specifying the pro-rata allocation method — eliminating ambiguity that could have forced discretionary cuts. Most affected are conventional hydro and pumped-storage operators with multiple simultaneous export tags from a single resource, where the pro-rata rule now guarantees fair allocation rather than first-come-first-served exposure. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the document.

title: 03-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ System Points (MPSP) and MITSPS establish new interchange transaction pathways for imports/exports across the Markets+ boundary; this directly affects how hydropower resources in participating BAAs can schedule and tag energy transactions into/out of the footprint, potentially altering revenue streams and operational flexibility for hydro-rich members considering or already committed to Markets+ governance.
ai_summary: SPP staff (Kelli Schermerhorn) submitted MIR 60-R to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a framework enabling Market Participants to schedule and e-Tag import and export transactions using generic Markets+ System Points (MPSP) as sources and sinks, rather than resource- or load-specific points. MITSPS will allow hydropower operators and other dispatchable resources in participating BAAs to route energy to and from the Markets+ boundary with greater operational ease, with the MPSP functioning as a generic scheduling node that must be registered and used for all import/export transactions crossing the footprint. The design is classified as low complexity and expected implementation in Q1 2027 after MPEC approval in Q3 2026; this modification to interchange scheduling and tagging directly affects revenue attribution, transaction flexibility, and portfolio management for hydro and storage resources participating in Markets+ or considering accession to it, particularly relevant for Northwest and Southwest operators evaluating Markets+ membership versus EDAM or other seams options.

title: 03-MIR75-R SPP Comments-20260520 (1).docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Defines e-Tag adjustment mechanics for high-priority exports from hydro and storage resources (MSRs) when resource capacity limits are exceeded; directly affects export revenue certainty and intra-day settlement for run-of-river hydro and pumped-storage discharge exports in Markets+ footprint.
ai_summary: SPP submitted a Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR75-R) specifying the operational mechanics for adjusting export e-Tags when a resource's maximum economic limit or (for pumped-storage and variable energy resources) available output falls below the MW amount specified on the e-Tag. The proposal establishes that adjustments occur in day-ahead (at market close) and real-time (pre-RTBM SCED study) windows, with pro-rata allocation across multiple e-Tags using the same resource source, rounded to whole-MW increments with a 1 MW minimum threshold. This clarifies language from an April 2026 MSWG/MDWG policy decision and applies to high-priority exports (WRAP forward showings, WRAP holdbacks, and NERC Priority 6–7 transmission service from internal resources). The proposal distinguishes between conventional resources (limited by Max ECOL) and pumped-storage/hybrid resources (limited by Maximum Discharge Limit or VER forecast). Most exposed are pumped-storage operators exporting during tight resource conditions and run-of-river hydro operators whose unforecasted generation changes mid-day; the pro-rata settlement rule means their export revenue depends on how many other same-source e-Tags are active. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this submitter-comment form.

title: 04-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a new transaction pathway enabling hydropower and other dispatchable resources to schedule and tag export/import transactions across the Markets+ boundary without pre-scheduling bilaterals, affecting how hydro participates in interregional power flow and market clearing within the emerging Markets+ seam governance structure.
ai_summary: SPP staff submitted MIR 60-R to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), creating a framework for generic Markets+ System Points (MPSPs) to facilitate unscheduled interchange transactions cleared through day-ahead and real-time markets rather than bilateral pre-scheduling. The framework establishes that MITSPS transactions are classified as 'Uncommitted' imports/exports and do not count toward a participant's must-offer obligation, while each Balancing Authority Area may set optional hourly import/export/net limits on MPSP interchange via the Market User Interface. Implementation is targeted for Q1 2027, with MPEC approval sought in Q3 2026. For hydropower owners participating in Markets+—particularly those seeking to export renewable generation or manage interchange flexibility across the SPP-region boundary—this creates a new operational pathway that could alter revenue stacking (energy cleared in DAM/RTBM plus potential bilateral premiums) and strategic choices around scheduling vs. market participation; the mechanism is most relevant to larger hydro and PSH facilities managing outflow across seams into non-Markets+ balancing areas, since it enables market-cleared rather than pre-scheduled boundary transactions.

title: 04_MPSP_MITPSPS and NSI.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: The document explains how Markets+ seams constructs (MPSPs, MITSPS, MTIs, CISLs) affect net scheduled interchange (NSI) and real-time dispatch, which determines how interregional power transfers are scheduled and constrained — directly impacting hydro deliverability, interregional export revenue, and operational flexibility for hydro-rich regions seeking to participate in Markets+.
ai_summary: This Markets+ Seams Working Group presentation illustrates how Multi-Party Scheduling Points (MPSPs), Market-Initiated Transfers through SPP Scheduling (MITSPS), Market Transfer Interchange (MTI), and Constraint Interchange Scheduling Limits (CISLs) function in real-time net scheduled interchange (NSI) calculation and dispatch. The document walks through examples showing how bilateral transactions, economic MITSPS cleared in day-ahead, normal and dynamic MTIs, and MPSP constraints combine to determine a BAA's total NSI and how real-time redispatch occurs when constraints bind. The mechanism is directly relevant to hydro owners evaluating Markets+ membership because it defines how hydropower exports cross seams, how interregional transfer capability is allocated and constrained, and how dynamic real-time adjustments may increase or reduce revenue opportunities from selling flexibility across balancing-authority boundaries. The presentation contains no action items, deadlines, or decision dates — it is explanatory material for stakeholders learning Markets+ architecture.

title: 05_MIR83-R e-Tag Status.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: e-Tag confirmation requirements affect interchange transaction eligibility in Markets+, which directly impacts how hydro (especially run-of-river and variable output) participates in interregional energy markets and settlement accuracy — a core revenue mechanism for hydro exports and flexibility sales across seams.
ai_summary: Markets+ is evaluating whether to accept e-Tags at the 'Valid' (pending approval) or 'Confirmed' (approved) stage for import/export/through interchange transactions. SPP and its vendor recommend 'Confirmed e-Tags' — only those with completed multi-party approvals — to avoid settlement discrepancies, divergence between day-ahead awards and real-time operations, and failed transactions affecting market assumptions. The 'Valid' option offers earlier visibility and participant flexibility but creates operational and settlement risk: ~10% of Western Interconnect e-Tags in May 2026 were ultimately denied, and using unapproved schedules in market solutions could force settlements on transactions that never become operational. The recommendation aligns with existing Integrated Marketplace practices and reduces vendor development costs. Markets+ protocol sections 4.2.2.7, 4.2.3.3, and 4.2.4 currently reference 'valid' e-Tags; this decision will either codify or revise that language. Discussion topics and a decision timeline (MSWG → MDWG → MPEC) are outlined but no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

title: 06_High Priority Exports Surplus Calculation Change BHE Presentation Final.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: Proposes changes to Markets+ export surplus calculation that would affect how hydropower and other dispatchable resources can qualify for firm export revenue and planning certainty across the SPP/Markets+ seam.
ai_summary: Black Hills Energy proposes realigning the Markets+ High Priority Export surplus calculation to reflect near-real-time operational availability rather than static seasonal peak-load forecasts, allowing resources with zero Operations Program obligations to classify exports as firm rather than being forced into non-firm/uncommitted status. The proposal highlights a misalignment between WRAP Forward Showing (seasonal, static) and Operations Program (hourly, dynamic), and a tension between Markets+ Export constructs and WSPP Schedule C firmness requirements — uncommitted exports are currently reducible for economic dispatch and congestion despite Schedule C limiting interruptions to mutual agreement and native-load reliability. Hydropower and other dispatchable resources operating in Rocky Mountain constrained-path regions rely on firm bilateral trades; this change would restore export eligibility for operators with seasonal RA obligations but zero hour-ahead commitments, increasing firm-trade liquidity and planning certainty for load-serving entities. Next step not stated in presentation materials.

title: 06_High Priority Exports Surplus Calculation Change BHE Presentation Final.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Interregional export rules and surplus-capacity calculations directly affect hydropower's ability to contract and schedule firm exports across seams, touching both revenue streams (bilateral trade liquidity and firmness classification) and strategic options (Markets+ participation and cross-border transmission utilization).
ai_summary: Black Hills Energy presents a proposal to align Markets+ High Priority Export surplus calculations with Operations Program obligations, rather than keeping them static across the entire WRAP binding season. Currently, surplus capacity is calculated once per season against forward RA obligations but cannot be updated as operational conditions change, forcing high-priority exports to be classified as non-firm (uncommitted) even when actual operational capacity exists. The proposal would allow surplus recalculation on a near-term basis to reflect real availability, addressing a misalignment between WSPP Schedule C (firm-service language) and Markets+ treatment of uncommitted exports as economically dispatchable. This affects hydropower owners relying on firm bilateral export trades across the SPP-WEIM seam, particularly in constrained regions like Rocky Mountain; the change would restore liquidity of firm bilateral trades and planning certainty for load-serving entities. Status and next steps are not stated in this presentation.

title: 07_2026_Q2_JUNE_MSWG_StakeholderTrainingUpdate_1.0.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: SPP Markets+ is a proposed multi-RTO governance and market-design seam affecting Western hydro operators' strategic options and compliance obligations; this training material establishes what participants must learn to engage with the new framework, including storage resource registration, must-offer rules, congestion and GHG modeling, and settlement mechanics that directly shape hydro revenue and dispatch control.
ai_summary: SPP is rolling out a tiered stakeholder training program for Markets+, a multi-RTO consolidation framework affecting governance, market design, and operations across the Southwest and potential expansion regions. The training curriculum covers foundational market architecture (Level 1), topical deep-dives on transmission, congestion rent, GHG accounting, must-offer obligations, day-ahead/real-time/RUC mechanics, market monitoring, and settlements (Level 2), plus task-based implementation training (Level 3). Hydro and pumped-storage operators in or considering SPP Markets+ must complete Level 1 foundational training and select Level 2 and 3 courses to participate; the curriculum explicitly includes modules on Market Storage Resources (MSR), resource registration (including Jointly Owned Units and Combined Interest Resources), and dispatchable demand response—frameworks that will govern how PSH charges, how hydro's must-offer obligations apply, and how congestion and greenhouse-gas costs flow through settlement. Next stakeholder-training update is August 13, 2026; training registrations are open in the SPP Learning Center.

title: 07_2026_Q2_JUNE_MSWG_StakeholderTrainingUpdate_1.0.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
relevance_reason: Training update on Markets+ participant readiness program establishes how hydro and PSH operators will comply with the new SPP Markets+ market system — a strategic governance choice with revenue and operational implications for NHA members considering or already committed to Markets+ participation.
ai_summary: SPP is rolling out a phased training program (Levels 1–3) to prepare market participants for Markets+ operations, with Level 1 foundational content, Level 2 topic-specific instructor-led sessions, and Level 3 task-based training. The Level 2 curriculum includes sessions on day-ahead market, RTBM, RUC, must-offer obligations, market storage resources (MSR), dispatchable demand response, and variable energy resources — all directly relevant to how hydro and PSH register, bid, and settle under Markets+. Trainers and market participants must complete readiness milestones; the training team is gathering feedback from entity trainers and collecting input on Level 3 development. Next working-group meeting is August 13, 2026.

title: 04) Proposed Changes Must Offer.potx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
relevance_reason: Must-offer is a core compliance obligation for dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage operators under Markets+ tariff; changes to settlement calculation and reporting directly affect how hydro owners are charged or credited for must-offer compliance and how those outcomes are tracked and billed.
ai_summary: Markets+ is revising how must-offer charges and credits are calculated and reported in settlement systems. Currently, must-offer assessment occurs at the Market Participant (MP) level only, creating gaps in Asset Owner (AO)-level determinants for shadow settlement and invoicing. The proposed resolution allocates MP-level must-offer charges proportionally by load ratio share across each AO modeled under an MP, preserving MP-level assessment (and financial responsibility) while enabling AO-level reporting. Changes affect sections 2.6.17, 2.6.18, 2.7.26, and 2.7.27 of the Markets+ Settlement Calculation Guide. A two-week public comment period runs through June 24, 2026, via the SPP RMS system; comments require no formal tariff vote if the guide changes stand alone. Most exposed are hydro operators managing multiple asset owners under a single market participant profile, since the new load-ratio-share allocation will determine how aggregate must-offer penalties or credits flow back to individual facilities for purposes of settlement reporting and invoice tie-out.

title: 05) Markets+ Program Update.pptx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
relevance_reason: Markets+ is SPP's new RTO market design launching in the western Great Plains; this program-status update documents settlement system development, testing timelines, and operational readiness milestones that directly affect how hydro and storage assets will be settled, must-offer, and participate in SPP markets post-go-live.
ai_summary: SPP's Markets+ program (the new RTO market design for the western footprint) is in development with core settlement and financial systems in active build. The Settlements & Financial Apps Tower shows Core Settlement Management System (SMS) in wave 3 (of 3) development, with calculation sets 1–6 complete and sets 7–8 (including Must Offer calculations) in progress, targeted for delivery in parallel with market-system functionality. The program published its Participant Test Plan v1.0 in May 2026, outlining connectivity testing, data exchange testing, market trials, and parallel operations phases through 2027. A Markets/Operations Tower faces schedule risk (yellow status) due to vendor delays on detailed design; SPP has established a strike team to assess design complexity from supporting both CPT (SPP legacy) and PPT (M+) time zones simultaneously. Hydro and storage operators in SPP's footprint will need to complete connectivity and data exchange testing before market trials; the Must Offer calculation finalization (set 8, in progress) directly affects how dispatchable resources—including run-of-river and pumped storage—will be treated in the new settlement framework. Next steps include continued vendor coordination, SPP Integration Testing Period adjustments, and stakeholder training.

title: 07) MDWG Updates.potx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
relevance_reason: Make-whole payment rules for dispatchable resources and GHG attribution directly affect hydro and storage revenue recovery under Markets+ energy price formation and cost disallowance mechanisms.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ approved MIR52, which specifies that GHG adders in real-time unit commitment (RUC) make-whole payments (MWP) should be included in the Manual Status Cost Disallowance for non-dispatchable injections, ensuring costs above a resource's economic operating point are not recovered. MIR77 addresses removal of inapplicable Integrated Marketplace language regarding dispatchable variable energy resource (DVER) deployment in the real-time balancing market, clarifying that Markets+ does not co-optimize for operating reserves (a responsibility left to balancing authorities). For hydro and pumped-storage operators, these rules affect how startup, shutdown, and GHG-related costs are treated in make-whole uplift payments — a key revenue recovery pathway when energy prices fall below operating costs. The mechanism is tightened cost disallowance for manual-status resources, which may constrain recovery for storage units or hydro plants operating outside economic dispatch.

title: 09) Calibration Testing Overview.pptx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
relevance_reason: Calibration testing and metering/settlement rules are direct obligations for all hydro and PSH participants in SPP Markets+; this document details the settlement-area meter-data submission processes and calibration exercises that will govern billing adjustments for generation, load, and interchange ties during market trials.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is conducting preliminary calibration testing in July–August 2026 to validate meter-data submissions and settlement-area constructs ahead of data exchange testing and market trials. Market participants and meter agents in each settlement area must submit meter data via SPP's portal, participate in calibration exercises to identify and resolve unaccounted-for-energy issues, and agree to share meter data during settlement-area-specific troubleshooting meetings. Hydro and PSH operators will be required to submit accurate generator meter data and tie-line data (with correct sign convention for imports/exports) and to resolve calibration disputes at the settlement-area level; high unaccounted-for-energy balances will shift settlement MWhs via load-ratio-share distribution, creating financial impacts if meter data is inaccurate or submission deadlines are missed. No formal vote or comment deadline is stated; next steps include SPP identifying meter agents and market participants by settlement area, distributing spreadsheet templates and exercise intervals in July, and scheduling settlement-area meetings for August.
